title: GenAI-Powered Text Personalization: Natural Language Processing Validation of Adaptation Capabilities
venue: Applied Sciences (Switzerland)
llm_base: Claude-3.5-Sonnet Claude 3.5 Sonnet (70-100B (estimated)); Llama-3-70B Llama 3.1 (70B); otros Gemini Pro 1.5 (~100B); GPT-4 GPT-4o (ChatGPT 4) (~175B (not officially disclosed))
dataset_name: iStART Texts Library (StairStepper)
dataset_size: 10 expository texts (318-492 words each); 160 modifications in Exp1 (4 LLMs x 4 profiles x 10 texts) + 40 modifications in Exp2 (Gemini x 4 profiles x 10 texts)
dataset_domain: science expository texts (biology, chemistry, physics, biochemistry)
personalization_mechanism: Four hypothetical reader profiles varying along two dimensions: reading skills (RS: high/low, quantified in Exp2 via ACT English/Reading percentile scores) and prior knowledge (PK: high/low, quantified via ACT Math/Science scores and science coursework background). Profiles also include age, educational level, major, learning goals, interests, and reading disabilities (e.g., dyslexia for Reader 3). Grounded in the Construction-Integration comprehension model and cohesion/prior-knowledge interaction theory.
subject_area: science reading comprehension (biology, chemistry, physics, biochemistry)
evaluation_metrics: FKGL; custom-rubric; Academic Writing index; Idea Development; Sentence Cohesion; Noun-to-Verb Ratio; Sentence Length; Language Variety; Sophisticated Wording; Academic Frequency
quantitative_results: Exp1 MANCOVA (4x4): significant main effect of reader profile on all linguistic features (e.g., FKGL F(3,320)=355.64, p<0.001, eta2=0.79; Academic Writing F=228.05, eta2=0.70; Sophisticated Wording F=342.11, eta2=0.78); FKGL ranged M=16.97 (High RS/High PK) to M=8.50 (Low RS/Low PK). Significant main effect of LLM: Llama produced highest complexity (FKGL M=11.87), Claude lowest cohesion (M=30.38) vs Gemini (M=52.65) and ChatGPT (M=51.06), Gemini highest language variety (M=55.51). Exp2 two-way ANOVA (prompt x profile) with Gemini: significant interactions for academic writing F(3,240)=10.45 eta2=0.12, cohesion F=6.18 eta2=0.07, language variety F=6.24 eta2=0.08, sophisticated wording F=17.44 eta2=0.19. Augmented prompt better aligned cohesion (higher for low-knowledge readers, lower for high-knowledge) and lexical/syntactic complexity with reader profiles.
limitations: Only four reader profiles used, limiting generalizability and not capturing the full diversity of learners (interests, motivation, cultural background, cognitive traits, disabilities beyond dyslexia).; Each text was modified only once per reader profile; single-run trials cannot capture inherent LLM non-determinism and output variability across repeated prompts.; Only 10 scientific texts in a limited domain; should be tested on a larger and more diverse text domain for external validity.; Risk of LLM hallucinations required a human-in-the-loop rubric check to verify that key concepts and relevance to the original text were preserved; automation would require multi-agent verification.; Experiment 2 focused on a single LLM (Gemini), isolating prompt effects but not generalizing augmented-prompt improvements across all models.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Demonstrates and validates an NLP-based, scalable, real-time method (using the Writing Analytics Tool) to evaluate GenAI-powered personalization of educational science texts against theory-driven reader profiles, comparing four commercial LLMs (Claude 3.5, Llama 3.1, Gemini Pro 1.5, GPT-4o) and showing that an augmented prompt combining personification, chain-of-thought, and RAG significantly improves alignment of linguistic features (cohesion, syntactic/lexical complexity, academic writing) with readers' prior knowledge and reading skills.
notes: No fine-tuning is performed; adaptation is achieved purely through prompt engineering (single-shot vs augmented prompt with personification + CoT + RAG). Models accessed via poe.com web deployment with default configurations (31 Aug 2024). The RAG knowledge base consisted of peer-reviewed papers and book chapters on reading comprehension, cohesion, and individual differences (Appendix D). Personalization target is text readability adaptation (modifying existing expository texts, not generating quizzes or lessons), so output_format best maps to 'other' (adapted/simplified-or-complexified science passages). Writing Analytics Tool (WAT) used for automated linguistic feature extraction. Human evaluation was a rubric-guided sanity check (Appendix E) to confirm concept preservation, not a scored comparison. Supported by IES Grant R305T240035 to Arizona State University. Data available in public Google Drive folder.

title: Steerable Generative AI-Based Virtual Tutor for Design Learning
venue: ICCE-Taiwan - IEEE Int. Conf. Consum. Electron. - Taiwan: Gener. AI Innov. Consum. Technol., Proc.
llm_base: Llama-3-Taiwan 8B Instruct (yentinglin/Llama-3-Taiwan-70B-Instruct referenced; Llama-3-Taiwan-8B used for PoC) (8B)
dataset_name: Philippe Starck custom dataset (interview transcripts, official website content, bibliographies, portfolios in Q&A format)
dataset_size: not specified (30% held out as test set)
dataset_domain: Industrial/product design (Philippe Starck persona)
personalization_mechanism: Steering the LLM to emulate the persona of a renowned designer (Philippe Starck) via LoRA fine-tuning on his materials; dialogue-based interaction in mixed reality adapts to student questions, but no per-student profiling is reported
subject_area: Industrial design education (design learning)
evaluation_metrics: Acceptability rate / expert agreement with master's original materials (>90%)
quantitative_results: Industrial design professionals manually evaluated each generated response for acceptability, reaching over 90% agreement with the master's (Philippe Starck) original materials on the 30% held-out test set.
limitations: Proof-of-concept without controlled user study measuring learning outcomes; Dataset size not reported; limited to a single designer persona (Philippe Starck); Evaluation based on acceptability agreement rather than pedagogical effectiveness or comprehension tests; No comparison against baselines or alternative adaptation techniques; Training materials translated/preprocessed to English while student interaction is in Mandarin (Whisper + VITS pipeline), introducing potential translation artifacts; Hardware dependency on Meta Quest 3 / Unity limits accessibility; Future work needed to add emotion recognition, facial expression generation and formal learning-outcome evaluation
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Proof-of-concept of a steerable generative AI virtual tutor for design learning that fine-tunes Llama-3-Taiwan-8B with LoRA (rank 8, alpha 16, lr 0.003, 30 epochs, batch 16, dropout 0.05) on a curated Philippe Starck Q&A dataset, integrated with Whisper (Mandarin ASR) and VITS/VITS2 (TTS) and deployed on Meta Quest 3 via Unity to deliver dialogue-based mixed-reality tutoring that emulates a renowned designer with >90% expert acceptability.
notes: Conference paper: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics - Taiwan (ICCE-Taiwan), pp. 247-248. Base model reference yentinglin/Llama-3-Taiwan-70B-Instruct cited, but the deployed/fine-tuned model is Llama-3-Taiwan-8B. Quantization not mentioned. Pipeline: Mandarin speech processed by Whisper (ASR), then fed to the fine-tuned LLM (English prompt/response), then to VITS/VITS2 (TTS), and finally rendered as avatar output in Unity/Meta Quest 3. Output format 'tutoring-dialogue' and 'explanation' chosen because the system delivers spoken Q&A tutoring about design; no quizzes/exercises/lesson plans produced. Personalization coded 'partial' because steering is toward a designer persona (content-level adaptation) rather than per-student adaptive learning.

title: Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning of LLMs Enhanced with RAG to Assist Students with Lectures
venue: Proc. ICITR - Int. Conf. Inf. Technol. Res.: Adv. Glob. Connect. Through Emerg. Inf. Technol. Solut.
dataset_name: Custom lecture transcripts dataset (self-created, paraphrased)
dataset_size: 500 small lecture transcripts (~15 min audio) + 124 large lecture transcripts (~30 min audio); 90% train / 10% eval; 100 Q&A pairs for RAG evaluation
dataset_domain: Higher education lectures (general academic)
personalization_mechanism: No explicit user-profile personalization; dual-mode retrieval (metadata filtering by date vs top-k semantic search) adapts responses to query intent (question-answering vs question-generation) but not to individual learner profile
subject_area: General higher education lectures (university note-taking assistance)
evaluation_metrics: ROUGE-1; ROUGE-2; ROUGE-L; BERTScore-F1; Perplexity; RAGAS: Context Precision, Context Recall, Faithfulness, Answer Relevancy, Semantic Similarity, Answer Correctness; Hallucination rate; Latency (seconds)
quantitative_results: Fine-tuned Phi-3-mini-4k-Instruct on small transcripts: ROUGE-1=0.7907, ROUGE-2=0.6510, ROUGE-L=0.7444, BERTScore-F1=0.7405 (outperformed Llama-3.2-1B-ft: R1=0.8441 on some metrics but Phi-3 preferred overall after chunk-merge). Before vs after fine-tuning Phi-3: ROUGE-1 0.6129 to 0.7907; ROUGE-2 0.3965 to 0.6510; ROUGE-L 0.5281 to 0.7444; BERTScore-F1 0.3700 to 0.7405; Perplexity 4.9808 to 2.8920. ROUGE precision/recall (phi3-lecture_note-ft): R1 P=0.5167 Rec=0.8747; R2 P=0.3669 Rec=0.6157; RL P=0.4300 Rec=0.7218. RAGAS: Context Precision=0.9857, Context Recall=0.9571, Faithfulness=0.7679, Answer Relevancy=0.9351, Semantic Similarity=0.9224, Answer Correctness=0.8377. Hallucination rate: 43.33% without RAG vs 6.33% with RAG (reduction of 37 percentage points). Latency: 17.01s transcription, 112.4s note-generation for 20-min audio, 6.47s query answering, 17.2s question generation.
limitations: Inference limited to Google Colab GPUs due to computational constraints; Absence of an inference API at HF Hub at time of experimentation limited offline deployment; Quality of generated notes declines in lectures relying heavily on whiteboard explanations (no visual modality); User experience and satisfaction not extensively evaluated; Limited quantity of long-lecture transcript data (only 124 samples); Simply increasing context length did not improve LLM ability to handle large prompts; ASR transcription errors may propagate to note generation; Dataset not publicly released; paraphrasing-based augmentation may affect generalization
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Proposes an end-to-end automated lecture assistance tool integrating AssemblyAI ASR, a LoRA-fine-tuned Phi-3-mini-4k-Instruct (named phi3-lecture_note-ft) and a RAG pipeline (ChromaDB + all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings, dual-mode retrieval) to generate detailed lecture notes highlighting announcements, answer queries on past lectures and auto-generate theoretical questions. Demonstrates that high-quality lecture note-taking can run on modest consumer-grade hardware (Google Colab free GPU) via PEFT, and that RAG integration reduces hallucinations by 37 percentage points (43.33% to 6.33%) while simply increasing LLM context length does not improve long-prompt handling.
notes: ICITR 2025 (IEEE). Authors: Jiyadh, Thirukumaran, Ratnarajah (University of Vavuniya, Sri Lanka). Pipeline: ReactJS frontend + FastAPI backend + Gradio (Colab) inference + ChromaDB vector store. ASR comparison: Whisper (small) versus AssemblyAI, with AssemblyAI selected (higher accuracy, 23.2s latency for 30-min recording). LLM selection process: TinyLlama-1.1B (failed on large transcripts, incoherent), Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct (acceptable), Phi-3-mini-4k-Instruct (best), with Phi-3 chosen as final base. Chunk-merge strategy (2000-token overlapping chunks) used for long lectures. Model published to Hugging Face Hub as 'phi3-lecture_note-ft'. Dataset created by manual paraphrasing from simulated lecture recordings; gold-standard notes manually created. 100 Q&A pairs (generic + specific) used for hallucination analysis with/without RAG. Keywords: ASR, GenAI, lecture assistance, LLM, PEFT, RAG.

title: AI-GENERATED LEARNING RESOURCES FOR CREATIVE FIELDS
venue: ShodhKosh: Journal of Visual and Performing Arts
dataset_domain: creative fields (art, design, music, media, architecture)
personalization_mechanism: Learner profiling modules that capture stylistic inclinations, expressive preferences, and learning preferences; adaptive feedback loops, individualized prompts, and varying difficulty levels based on learner behavior and performance data
subject_area: Creative fields: art, design, music, media studies, and architecture (creative education)
evaluation_metrics: Conceptual Understanding (%); Skill Acquisition Rate (%); Practice Efficiency (%); Learner Engagement (%); Retention of Concepts (%); Idea Diversity (%); Originality Score (%); Conceptual Coherence (%); Expressive Quality (%); Iterative Improvement Rate (%)
quantitative_results: Reported illustrative aggregate improvements of AI-generated vs traditional resources: Conceptual Understanding 68.2% to 87.6% (+19.4), Skill Acquisition 65.9% to 86.3% (+20.4), Practice Efficiency 62.7% to 84.8% (+22.1), Learner Engagement 66.4% to 88.9% (+22.5), Retention 64.1% to 82.7% (+18.6). Creative performance with AI: Idea Diversity +18.8, Originality +15.3, Conceptual Coherence +16.1, Expressive Quality +19.4, Iterative Improvement Rate +23.5. Values are presented as conceptual/aggregate figures without described experimental protocol, sample size, or statistical tests
limitations: Quality control and pedagogical alignment: generative models can be stylistically attractive but pedagogically inconsistent, over-generic, or misaligned with learning purpose; Bias, originality and cultural sensitivity: training data biased toward cultural majorities and historical disproportions may homogenize creative standards; Over-reliance on AI-generated content may suppress creative thinking, motivation and independent decision-making; Unpredictability of generative outputs and need for human-in-the-loop oversight; Ethical issues of authorship, attribution, data privacy, transparency and learner autonomy; Conceptual paper: no empirical implementation, no described dataset, no model training details, no experimental protocol
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_contribution: Conceptual framework and modular system architecture for AI-generated learning resources in creative disciplines (art, design, music, media, architecture), composed of creative knowledge repositories, model training/fine-tuning pipelines, and personalization/learner-profiling modules. Discusses adaptation strategies (supervised fine-tuning, educator-feedback reinforcement learning, prompt conditioning) at a high level and critically analyzes pedagogical alignment, quality control, cultural bias, and over-reliance on AI
notes: Conceptual/framework paper (11 pages, ShodhKosh 2026). No original fine-tuning or empirical experiments are conducted: no base LLM, size, dataset, hyperparameters, or evaluation protocol are specified. Supervised fine-tuning, educator-feedback RL, and prompt conditioning are mentioned as possible strategies, but without implementation. Tables 2 and 3 with percentages are illustrative/aggregate figures with no described experimental methodology. Covers creative education (art, design, music, media, architecture) in higher education. Relevant for architectural/pedagogical RQs of the mapping (personalization, multimodal formats, ethical limitations) but contributes little to the technical fine-tuning RQs (RQ2, RQ3).

title: LLaMa-Based Personalized Learning: Fine-Tuning Generative AI in Educational Recommendations
venue: SECOND INTERNATIONAL SYMPOSIUM ON GENERATIVE AI AND EDUCATION, ISGAIE 2025
dataset_name: K-12 educational datasets + OpenEdX learning platform logs
dataset_size: 50,000 students; 1,200 courses; 2,500,000 interactions; 500,000 quiz attempts; avg 50 interactions/student
dataset_domain: K-12 education (e.g., Algebra, Geometry) via OpenEdX LMS
personalization_mechanism: Contextual embeddings built from concatenated student learning history (h_s) and resource metadata (h_r) as X_{s,r}=[CLS,h_s,h_r,SEP]; transformer self-attention computes relevance score y_hat_{s,r}=sigma(WH+b); ranking via combined BCE + BPR pairwise loss to prioritize relevant resources per student profile, past interactions and learning preferences
subject_area: K-12 general curriculum with examples in Mathematics (Algebra, Geometry); courses delivered via OpenEdX
evaluation_metrics: Precision@10; Recall@k; NDCG@10; 5-fold cross-validation
quantitative_results: General scenario: LLaMA Precision@10=0.721, NDCG@10=0.754 vs Hybrid CF+CBF 0.672/0.689, CBF 0.628/0.642, CF 0.602/0.621. Cold-start users: LLaMA 0.569/0.598 vs Hybrid 0.491/0.527, CBF 0.435/0.479, CF 0.412/0.451 (38.1% higher Precision@10 than CF). Diverse Learning: LLaMA 0.689/0.712 vs Hybrid 0.621/0.643, CBF 0.588/0.605, CF 0.561/0.582 (17.8% improvement in Precision@10 over best baseline Hybrid CF+CBF).
limitations: High computational complexity: fine-tuning and deploying large-scale transformer models requires significant GPU resources, making large-scale implementation costly; Risk of reinforcing biases present in the training data; fairness concerns in AI-generated recommendations; Real-time adaptability limited: periodic retraining required, impractical for rapidly changing learning needs; Ethical issues around transparency and data privacy; Specific LLaMA version/size and fine-tuning hyperparameter values not reported, limiting reproducibility
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5
key_contribution: Proposes a fine-tuned LLaMA model for personalized K-12 educational resource recommendation on OpenEdX, formulating recommendation as a ranking optimization trained with BCE + BPR pairwise loss and AdamW; reports significant gains over CF, CBF and Hybrid CF+CBF baselines in general, cold-start and sparse-data scenarios (NDCG@10 0.754 vs 0.689 hybrid).
notes: Focus is recommendation/ranking of existing learning resources rather than generation of didactic content; fine-tuning is described at a conceptual level (SFT + RLHF mentioned) without numeric hyperparameters, specific LLaMA size, or dataset/code release. RLHF is mentioned in Section 3.2 but not operationalized with explicit reward model details.

title: A Novel Approach to Scalable and Automatic Topic-Controlled Question Generation in Education
venue: Int. Conf. Learn. Anal. Knowl., LAK
ft_quantization: 8-bit (LLM.int8) and 4-bit (QLoRA) post-training quantised variants: TopicQG8bit and TopicQG4bit
dataset_name: SQuAD 1.1 + derived SQuAD+, MixSQuAD, MixSQuAD2X (training); KhanQ transformed into MixKhanQ (evaluation); peS2o (pre-training)
dataset_size: SQuAD 1.1 ~100,000 questions over 536 Wikipedia articles; MixSQuAD ~10,000 mixed-context entries; MixSQuAD2X doubles it via data augmentation; KhanQ 1,034 STEM questions
dataset_domain: General education / STEM (Computing, Economics, Chemistry, Art, Biology; Khan Academy content)
personalization_mechanism: Topic-controlled generation: the model conditions question generation on a paragraph context c and a target topic t extracted via Wikification (top concepts by PageRank), enabling topic-specific question generation aligned with learner/teacher-selected topics. Discussed as enabler for personalised learning management systems and ITSs, but no explicit learner-model-driven personalisation is implemented.
subject_area: Multiple subjects (Computing, Economics, Chemistry, Art, Biology); STEM and non-STEM topics from Khan Academy courses; Wikipedia-grounded educational content
evaluation_metrics: BLEU-1; BLEU-2; BLEU-3; BLEU-4; F1 score; METEOR; ROUGE-L; Perplexity (TextDescriptives, en_core_web_lg); BERTScore; WikiSemRel (w2v); WikiSemRel (Jaccard); Mean Absolute Error (MAE) between human annotators and SemRel scores; Fleiss' kappa (inter-rater agreement = 0.933)
quantitative_results: TopicQG outperforms baseline: BLEU-1 0.551 (vs 0.519), BLEU-4 0.191 (vs 0.175), F1 0.330 (vs 0.319), METEOR 0.233 (vs 0.216), ROUGE-L 0.230 (vs 0.207); perplexity 1.323 vs baseline 1.303. Quantised variants keep near-equivalent quality: TopicQG8bit (BLEU-1 0.546, F1 0.319, METEOR 0.226, ROUGE-L 0.225) and TopicQG4bit (BLEU-1 0.543, F1 0.318). TopicQG2X (data augmentation) achieves best topic alignment: WikiSimRel Jaccard difference of 0.680 between prescribed vs alternative topic (vs baseline 0.545). MAE(Human, SemRel) metrics: BERTScore 0.48, WikiSemRel w2v 0.36, WikiSemRel Jaccard 0.23 (best). Memory footprint: ~230 MB full precision, ~110 MB 8bit (59% reduction), ~94 MB 4bit (53% of 8bit size). Paired t-test TopicQGedu vs TopicQG p=0.083 (no statistically significant improvement from scientific pre-training).
limitations: Limited human evaluation sample size (n=4 annotators, 30 question pairs) hinders statistical power; Random context pairing strategy used; alternative pairings respecting subject domain, subtopics, difficulty level could yield more effective training sets; Method focuses only on topical relevance; other aspects such as linguistic complexity, cognitive load and generation length (short-question constraint) are not controlled; Small model (~60M params) generates questions of lower quality than large LLMs like ChatGPT; output may need post-processing with grammar correction models; Pre-training on scientific text (peS2o) did not yield statistically significant gains, possibly due to mismatch with T5's original web/Wikipedia training corpora; Extreme (4-bit) quantisation can deteriorate accuracy on topic alignment metrics; Evaluation is limited to English and to a specific Khan Academy derived dataset
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Proposes T-CQG (Topic-Controlled Question Generation) method that fine-tunes a very small language model (T5-small, ~60M params) to generate paragraph-context-grounded, topic-specific educational questions. Introduces a novel dataset enrichment pipeline using Wikification and contrastive concatenated contexts (SQuAD+, MixSQuAD, MixSQuAD2X) and validates quantised variants (8-bit, 4-bit QLoRA) that preserve quality while cutting memory to ~94 MB, enabling scalable, low-cost, on-device educational QG without reliance on proprietary LLMs. Also proposes WikiSemRel as a novel automated metric for evaluating topical relatedness, shown to align with human judgement better than BERTScore.
notes: Published at LAK 2025 (ACM). Code available at https://github.com/Cathgy/Topic-controllable-Question-Generator.git. Base model: t5-small from HuggingFace. Quantisation via bitsandbytes (LLM.int8 algorithm for 8-bit and QLoRA algorithm for 4-bit). Datasets SQuAD, KhanQ and peS2o are publicly released by their original authors; newly derived datasets (SQuAD+, MixSQuAD, MixSQuAD2X, MixKhanQ) are produced by the authors' scripts. Adaptation techniques include SFT (primary), QLoRA (for 4-bit quantisation) and continued-pretraining (TopicQGedu on peS2o). Output format is answer-agnostic short-answer / open-ended question generation.

title: ReviseSmart: An AI-Powered Personalized Revision Platform
venue: IEEE Int. Conf. Intelligent Signal Processing Effective Communication Technologies, INSPECT
dataset_name: mlabonne/guanaco-llama2-1k
dataset_size: 1k samples
dataset_domain: general instruction-following (used for educational content fine-tuning)
personalization_mechanism: User-provided inputs (topic, subtopics, uploaded PDF/DOCX, YouTube links, number and type of questions) drive content generation; post-quiz performance dashboard tracks accuracy, time, and question types to give feedback. No explicit learner-profile modelling.
subject_area: General (pilot covered SQL, Python, Blockchain among undergraduate-level subjects)
evaluation_metrics: pre/post-test scores; learning improvement (%); user satisfaction (%); two-sample t-test (p<0.05); average generation time (s); standard deviation (s)
quantitative_results: Experimental group (n=20) improved +32% (pre 52.3% to post 84.3%) vs control (n=20) +12% (pre 53.1% to post 65.1%), statistically significant (p<0.05). 87% user satisfaction; 85% ease of use, 78% content quality, 72% engagement, overall 4.3/5. System performance: course generation avg 92.5s (SD 10.3), quiz generation avg 45.8s (SD 8.7). Fine-tuning: 26 min, 250 steps, avg loss 1.36.
limitations: Small sample size (n=40), limited generalizability; Slow course generation during longer sessions due to Cohere/YouTube API and fine-tuned LLaMA processing; Repetition of quiz questions reported by users; Potential LLM hallucinations at scale; Inherited biases from training data affecting Q&A generation; No real-time progress indicators or doubt-clearing assistance yet; Lack of curriculum-aligned and parameter-efficient methods beyond LoRA/QLoRA (future work mentions adapter/prompt tuning and RAG)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: ReviseSmart: a four-module web platform (Next.js/TypeScript/Supabase/Prisma) that integrates a QLoRA-fine-tuned Llama-2-7b-chat-hf for automated generation of MCQ/open-ended quizzes, YouTube-transcript-based course outlines, scraped educational blogs, and document-upload-driven quizzes, validated with a pilot study showing +32% learning improvement and 87% satisfaction vs traditional revision.
notes: LoRA params explicitly reported: r=64, alpha=16, dropout=0.1. QLoRA applies 4-bit NF quantization to extend LoRA. Fine-tuning dataset is the generic mlabonne/guanaco-llama2-1k instruction dataset, not a purpose-built educational corpus, which is a notable limitation for pedagogical specialization. Cohere and Gemini are used alongside the fine-tuned Llama-2 for prompting (course outlines, document parsing) but the fine-tuned model is the one reported in detail. Pilot with 40 undergraduate/self-learner/educator participants over one month.

title: Design and Optimization of an Offline AI Model for Enhancing Student Learning and Academic Assistance
venue: IEEE Int. Conf. Sustain. Technol. Ind. 5.0, STI
ft_quantization: 4-bit GGUF (Q4_0) via llama.cpp; uniform 4-bit post-merge quantization
dataset_name: OpenStax textbooks (Physics, Biology, Chemistry) + Yahma/Stanford Alpaca instruction-following dataset
dataset_size: 1,000 test samples used for evaluation; 70% train / 15% val / 15% test split; thousands of structured chapters from OpenStax; lightweight 5% subset available on request
dataset_domain: Educational / academic (Physics, Biology, Chemistry textbooks) + general instruction-following
personalization_mechanism: Curriculum-aligned instruction tuning on OpenStax textbook content provides domain-specific academic assistance; modular client-server architecture designed to support future subject-specific adapters; no per-student personalization implemented in this work
subject_area: STEM (Physics, Biology, Chemistry) - college-level academic assistance
evaluation_metrics: BLEU (sacreBLEU); ROUGE-L (rouge_score); Accuracy; Macro-F1; Precision; Recall; Latency (seconds); Memory Usage (GB); Throughput (tokens/s); Power draw (W); Human rating on factuality and clarity (5-point scale); Exact Match (EM); paired t-tests (p<0.05)
quantitative_results: BLEU improved from 12.3 (before tuning) to 86.94 (after tuning); ROUGE-L improved from 27.5 to 96.50; Latency reduced from 2.3s to 2.0s; Memory usage reduced from 6.8 GB to 4.2 GB. Model size compressed from 16.1 GB (FP16) to 4.2 GB (Q4_0 GGUF), ~38% reduction with less than 2-point BLEU degradation from quantization. Human evaluation (10 educators, 100 responses): factuality and clarity rated 4.6 ± 0.3 / 5. Outperforms Phi-3 Mini (BLEU 72.1, ROUGE-L 88.4, Acc 78.9%), Mistral 7B Instruct Q4_0 (74.6, 90.2, 80.5%), Gemma 7B (75.4, 91.1, 82.3%) and OpenHermes 2.5 (77.8, 92.5, 83.7%) with final model: BLEU 86.9, ROUGE-L 96.5, Accuracy 89.2%, Latency 2.0s, Memory 4.2 GB. Curriculum-aligned variant improves EM by ~7-10 points and reduces contradictory outputs by ~30% in human spot-checks. Throughput 25.8 tokens/s vs 14-18 tokens/s baselines (45% faster decoding). Power draw ~22 W (20% lower than comparable 7B models). Statistical significance (p<0.05) confirmed via paired t-tests vs zero-shot LLaMA 3.1-8B, Mistral 7B, and Phi-3 Mini.
limitations: Training restricted to English-language OpenStax + Alpaca data; limited multilingual coverage; Domain coverage limited to Physics, Biology and Chemistry from OpenStax; Only lightweight 5% subset of data and scripts to be released upon request (not full open release at time of publication); Requires 16 GB RAM consumer device; may degrade on lower-end hardware (<4-6 GB RAM target); Small-scale human evaluation (only 10 educators, 100 responses); No validation in authentic classroom settings (left for future work); Lacks multi-step complex reasoning depth of larger cloud-based LLMs; Potential for degraded output under long prompts (graceful degradation noted but not fully quantified); No integration of speech-based interfaces, subject-specific adapters or assessment tools yet; Ethical and safety considerations discussed but not formally audited
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: End-to-end reproducible pipeline for an offline, instruction-tuned LLaMA 3.1-8B educational assistant using LoRA fine-tuning (r=8, alpha=16) on OpenStax+Alpaca data and 4-bit GGUF quantization via llama.cpp, achieving near-interactive latency (2.0s) and 4.2 GB footprint on a 16 GB consumer MacBook Pro while outperforming Phi-3 Mini, Mistral 7B, Gemma 7B and OpenHermes 2.5 on an educational QA benchmark. Provides a Tkinter desktop GUI for local interaction and addresses privacy/accessibility concerns in low-resource, connectivity-limited educational environments.
notes: Venue: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Sustainable Technologies for Industry 5.0 (STI 2025), Dhaka, Bangladesh. DOI: 10.1109/STI69347.2025.11367565. Authors from Green University of Bangladesh. Paper explicitly combines LoRA parameter-efficient fine-tuning with post-training 4-bit GGUF quantization for edge/offline deployment; uses Unsloth for training acceleration and llama.cpp for inference. Interface is a Python Tkinter client connecting to llama.cpp HTTP server on localhost:8000. Release package promised with Python scripts for preprocessing and LoRA fine-tuning, model card, and quantized checkpoint (~4.2 GB).

title: Agentic RAG for Personalized Learning: Design of an AI-Powered Learning Agent Using Open-Source Small Language Models
venue: Electronic Journal of e-Learning
llm_base: Gemma 2 gemma2:2b (Ollama) (2B); Llava Ollama llava (unknown); MiniCPM-V Ollama minicpm-v (unknown)
dataset_name: 4 OERs from OER Commons / federal sources (case studies/readings): 'Addressing Links Between Climate and Public Health in Alaska Native Villages' (Environmental Science), 'Adjusting for Inflation' (Business Communication), 'Equity vs. Equality' (Law), 'Anatomy and Physiology of the Respiratory System' (Life Sciences)
dataset_size: 4 OERs; 25 evaluation queries (probes) covering summarization, retrieval-QA, quiz generation, flashcards and visual QA
dataset_domain: Open Educational Resources (OER) - multi-domain (Environmental Science, Business/Economics, Law, Life Sciences)
personalization_mechanism: Learner-driven personalization: the learner uploads their own OER of interest and the agent autonomously selects the appropriate tool (Summary / Flashcards / Quiz / RAG-QA / Visual-QA) based on the learner's query intent via a Router Query Engine with tool calling. No learner-profile modeling; personalization is by learner-chosen content and request type.
subject_area: Multi-domain OERs: Environmental Science, Business Communication/Economics, Law, Life Sciences (Anatomy & Physiology)
evaluation_metrics: BERTScore (Precision, Recall, F1) vs. GPT-4o reference summaries; 3-point tool-selection scoring scheme (0/1/2) with log-based verification of actual vs expected tool
quantitative_results: BERTScore F1 >= 0.84 on all 4 OER summaries vs. GPT-4o reference (Environmental Science P=0.88/R=0.89/F1=0.88; Business Communication P=0.90/R=0.89/F1=0.89; Law P=0.90/R=0.91/F1=0.90; Life Sciences P=0.85/R=0.84/F1=0.84). Agentic tool selection: 24/25 queries (96%) invoked the expected tool; only Q5 showed a mismatch (expected RT+QT, agent called only QT but output was still reasonable).
limitations: Small evaluation set: only 4 OERs and 25 probe queries, no statistical significance testing; No human-student evaluation of learning outcomes; pedagogical value not empirically validated with learners; Hallucinations reduced by RAG but not eliminated; SLM responses can still be irrelevant or fabricated; Quality degrades for highly technical or image-intensive OERs with fine details or complex diagrams; Image-tool accuracy depends on image clarity, domain-specific labels, and text-image alignment; Possible offensive/inappropriate language output (shared SLM/LLM concern), no guardrails implemented; BERTScore uses GPT-4o as pseudo-ground-truth, which is itself imperfect; no expert-authored gold summaries; Requires 8-16 GB RAM and >=4-core CPU, limiting deployment on low-resource/mobile devices
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Design and prototype of a locally-deployable Agentic RAG learning agent built entirely on open-source small language models (Gemma 2:2B, Llava, MiniCPM-V via Ollama) and LlamaIndex, with a Router Query Engine that autonomously selects among five specialized tools (Summary, Quiz, Flashcards, RAG-QA, Visual-QA) to generate personalized learning aids from learner-provided OERs. Demonstrates that small open-source models can approach GPT-4o summary quality (BERTScore F1 >= 0.84) while preserving privacy and affordability, with detailed implementation guidelines rarely shared in prior literature.
notes: Electronic Journal of e-Learning 23(4), 2025, pp. 69-80. Builds on prior work (Taneja et al., in press, IJTEL). Uses List/Summary Index + Tree Summarize mode for summaries, Vector Store Index + Compact mode for flashcards/quizzes/QA, and CLIP-based multimodal vector index + custom query engine with minicpm-v for visual QA. Front-end: Gradio chatbot with chat history. AI Statement: authors declare no AI tools used in conceptualization/writing. Primary RQs: Q1 on Agentic RAG for learning aids, Q2 on affordable multimodal local learning agents vs. cloud-based tools.

title: LightRAG-Enhanced Automated Question Generation System for Multi-Domain Educational Assessments
venue: Int. Workshop Artif. Intell. Educ., WAIE
dataset_name: User-uploaded educational documents (PDFs, textbooks, lecture notes) across Electronics Engineering and Engineering Sciences
dataset_size: 240 MCQs generated (120 per domain, 30 per topic, 5 per Bloom level)
dataset_domain: Electronics Engineering (Diodes and Transistors, Operational Amplifiers, Digital and Data Communications, Fiber Optics) and Engineering Sciences (Physics, Materials Science, Technopreneurship, ECE Laws and Engineering Management)
personalization_mechanism: Users upload their own instructional documents (PDFs) and set exam parameters (number of questions, topic, Bloom's Taxonomy level, retrieval strategy) via a Streamlit GUI; system adapts dynamically to user-uploaded materials and selected cognitive level.
subject_area: Electronics Engineering and Engineering Sciences (STEM / technical higher education)
evaluation_metrics: Accuracy (factual correctness); Relevance (topic alignment); Grammatical Correctness (clarity and structure); Cognitive Level Alignment (Bloom's Taxonomy fit); Diversity (uniqueness); Binary rubric (1=meets, 0=does not meet)
quantitative_results: Hybrid LightRAG vs Naive LightRAG. Electronics Engineering: LLM accuracy 95% (Hybrid) vs 93% (Naive); human expert accuracy 94.17% (Hybrid) vs 91.11% (Naive); Application-level questions 86% (Hybrid) vs 72% (Naive) in LLM eval, 80% vs 70% in human eval; diversity 100% (Hybrid) vs 94% (Naive LLM eval) and 100% vs 92% (human eval). Engineering Sciences: near-perfect scores across both, Hybrid 100% accuracy (human) vs 99.2% (Naive); LLM Evaluation-level 99.7% (Hybrid) vs 100% (Naive). Both models scored 100% on relevance, grammatical correctness, and cognitive-level alignment across domains.
limitations: Imprecise arithmetic and inconsistent stepwise logic in computation-based Electronics Engineering questions (amplifier gain, modulation index, transmission line parameters); Lack of intermediate solution steps hinders traceability in numerical questions; No integration with symbolic reasoning tools (Wolfram, SymPy) for mathematical solving; Evaluated on only two domains (Electronics Engineering and Engineering Sciences); Small evaluation panel (three human experts and three LLMs); System depends on user-provided OpenAI API key and GPT-4 availability; No real-world classroom deployment yet; pilot validation remains future work; Limited to MCQ format
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes a LightRAG-Enhanced Automated Question Generation (AQG) system that integrates graph-based text indexing and a dual-level retrieval mechanism (local exact-match keywords + global semantic keywords) to generate Bloom's-Taxonomy-aligned MCQs. Deployed as a Streamlit GUI with Canvas-compatible DOCX export for LMS integration. Hybrid LightRAG variant outperforms Naive LightRAG in accuracy, diversity, and cognitive alignment across Electronics Engineering and Engineering Sciences.
notes: No fine-tuning is performed; the contribution is a RAG-based pipeline using GPT-4 as generator with LightRAG (Guo et al. 2024) for retrieval. Evaluation uses three LLM judges (ChatGPT, Gemini, DeepSeek) plus three human subject-matter experts with a binary rubric across five criteria and all six Bloom levels. Published in 2025 7th International Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Education (IEEE, DOI 10.1109/WAIE67422.2025.11381179).

title: CogniLearn: Integrating AI-Powered Insights for Class 10 Syllabus
venue: Proc. - Int. Conf. Comput. Artif. Intell., ICCAI
adaptation_technique: LoRA; QLoRA; RAG; prompting; few-shot; chain-of-thought
ft_quantization: 4-bit (bitsandbytes, nf4, float16 compute dtype, nested quantization disabled)
dataset_name: Custom CBSE Class 10 Q&A dataset (proprietary) + NCERT textbooks for RAG
dataset_size: ~3,000+ question-answer pairs (150-200 pairs per chapter); MCQs, short (1-2 mark) and long/descriptive (3-5 mark) answers
dataset_domain: CBSE Class 10 syllabus: Science, Social Studies, English
personalization_mechanism: Role-based prompting (teacher/student roles); dynamic temperature adjustment based on marking scheme and question complexity (factual answers = lower temp, multi-step reasoning = higher temp); chain-of-thought and few-shot prompting tailored to query type
subject_area: CBSE Class 10 board exam preparation: Science, Social Studies, English
evaluation_metrics: BLEU; ROUGE-1; ROUGE-2; ROUGE-L; BERTScore; BERTScore cosine; Cosine similarity; Word Mover's Distance (WMD)
quantitative_results: Three configurations compared per model (base, fine-tuned, fine-tuned+RAG). LLaMA-2 7B: base already strong; fine-tuning yields only slight gains in BLEU and ROUGE; fine-tuned+RAG slightly better than base and FT alone in semantic metrics (BLEU, WMD, Cosine); overall improvements moderate. Qwen 0.5B: fine-tuning yields more noticeable improvements in BLEU, ROUGE, BERT than LLaMA-2; fine-tuned+RAG performs best across all metrics for Qwen with significant boost in BLEU and WMD. Qwen 0.5B FT+RAG matches LLaMA-2 7B FT+RAG in semantic metrics (Fig. 6). Exact numeric values shown only in bar charts (Figs. 4-6), not reported in table form.
limitations: Small custom dataset (~3,000 QA pairs) limits fine-tuning gains for large models; Diminishing returns of fine-tuning for LLaMA-2 7B with small dataset; Evaluation relies solely on automatic semantic metrics; no human/expert or student evaluation reported; Proprietary dataset not publicly released, limiting reproducibility; Only two base LLMs compared (LLaMA-2 7B and Qwen 0.5B); limited model diversity; Restricted to CBSE Class 10 syllabus (Science, Social Studies, English); generalization unclear; No reported ground-truth numeric tables; results presented only in bar charts
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: CogniLearn: an AI-powered educational platform that fine-tunes LLaMA-2 7B and Qwen 0.5B on a custom ~3,000-pair CBSE Class 10 Q&A dataset using QLoRA (rank 64, alpha 16, 4-bit quantization) and augments inference with RAG (LlamaIndex + Sentence Transformer embeddings + LangChain retrieval) plus prompt engineering (role-based, chain-of-thought, few-shot). Empirically shows that smaller models (Qwen 0.5B) benefit more from FT+RAG than large models (LLaMA-2 7B) when training data is scarce, achieving comparable semantic quality at a fraction of the parameter count.
notes: ICCAI 2025 conference paper. Experiments on L4/T4 GPUs via Lightning AI and Google Colab. Abstract/intro mention both LoRA and QLoRA as applied techniques, but the experimental setup (Sec. IV-A) explicitly specifies QLoRA hyperparameters (attention dim 64, alpha 16, dropout 0.1, 4-bit bitsandbytes nf4). RAG stack: NCERT textbook PDFs indexed with Sentence Transformers + LlamaIndex; retrieval via LangChain; ConversationBufferMemory for context. UI screenshots (Fig. 7) show deployed Gradio-style interface. No fine-tuning of LLaMA-2 beyond QLoRA reported. No human evaluation, no ablation of prompt-engineering components reported numerically.

title: Pedagogy-R1: Pedagogical Large Reasoning Model and Well-balanced Educational Benchmark
venue: CIKM - Proc. ACM Int. Conf. Info. Knowl. Manage.
llm_base: QwQ QwQ-32B (32B); DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B (1.5B); DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B (7B)
dataset_name: WBEB (Well-balanced Educational Benchmark) - aggregates: cj-eval-T, kice-pedagogy (elementary/middle/middle-special), dbe-kt-22, xes3g5m-simple, csedm19-spring, learning-agency-lab-aes, ncte-eval (mcq-aa, bn-fq, bn-hu, bn-sr, mcq-sr)
dataset_size: ~27,000+ samples across 14 datasets; 1,948 training examples (filtered correctness-verified from 80% train split, 5,000 seed items sampled from WBEB) used for instruction tuning
dataset_language: English (with translation from Chinese and Korean via GPT-4.1-mini/GPT-4o-mini)
dataset_domain: Education - 5 dimensions: Subject Knowledge (SK), Pedagogical Knowledge (PK), Knowledge Tracing (KT), Automated Essay Scoring (AES), Real-World Teacher Decision-Making (DM)
personalization_mechanism: Chain-of-Pedagogy (CoP) prompting elicits teacher-like reasoning responsive to student needs (formative feedback, scaffolding, pedagogical judgment). Model reasons about student understanding and instructional decisions, but does not explicitly adapt to individual student profiles at inference.
subject_area: Mathematics, Science, Programming (Java), History, Education, and general subject knowledge across elementary, middle, and special-ed levels; includes classroom teaching decision-making for 4th-5th grade math
evaluation_metrics: Accuracy (ACC); AUC; Exact match (MCQA); Rubric-based 1-6 scale (AES); Underthinking (UT) Score; Reasoning token counts; Transition word ratios; Grounded-theory qualitative coding (10-code codebook: IA-SQ, IA-CC, IA-IA, IA-P, IA-C, OA-SR, OA-ME, T-U, T-C)
quantitative_results: Pedagogy-R1-7B achieves highest scores across WBEB: PK 31.67% (+6.49 over Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct), AES 15.83% (+8.75), DM 54.76% (+31.34), KT-ACC 52.03% (+11.43), KT-AUC 54.99% (-4.82). Pedagogy-R1-1.5B: PK 30.60% (+6.34 vs Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct 24.26%), KT-AUC 52.57% (+3.00), DM 24.73% (+1.22). Pedagogy-R1-CoP-7B (distillation) reaches DM 73.71% (+33.11), KT-ACC 73.71%. CoP prompting reduces UT Score and reasoning tokens, improves metacognitive codes (IA-SQ, IA-P increase in SK/KT). Pedagogy-R1 models outperform SK only marginally (SK ACC drops ~1-2% vs some baselines), but pedagogically aligned instruction tuning yields better performance than standard instruction tuning across pedagogical dimensions.
limitations: SK (Subject Knowledge) performance does not consistently improve; sometimes drops vs base models (e.g., -2.49% for 7B); KT-AUC occasionally degrades after pedagogical tuning (-4.82% for 7B); CoP distillation gains are less consistent in PK and AES where smaller pedagogically-aligned models outperform larger ones; Machine-human agreement analysis for qualitative coding left as future work; Many WBEB datasets (KICE-pedagogy, NCTE-eval) are private/internal use only due to copyright and data-sharing agreements; Translation of Chinese/Korean datasets via GPT-4.1-mini introduces potential translation artifacts; ~65% of tokens classified as filler/noise (verbatim stem repetitions, generic phatic remarks) inflating token cost; Absence of metacognitive self-critique (IA-SQ, OA-ME) in vanilla prompting before CoP; Limited to English (after translation); no multilingual evaluation in native language; Evaluation scope for real-world deployment constrained by benchmark-based setting
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes Pedagogy-R1, a pedagogical Large Reasoning Model (LRM) family (1.5B/7B) built via distillation from QwQ-32B teacher with pedagogically filtered outputs and Chain-of-Pedagogy (CoP) prompting. Introduces the Well-balanced Educational Benchmark (WBEB) spanning five educational dimensions (SK, PK, KT, AES, DM) and provides a mixed-methods evaluation combining quantitative metrics (UT Score, reasoning tokens) with grounded-theory qualitative coding of reasoning traces, bridging LRMs and authentic classroom tasks.
notes: CIKM '25 publication. Three-stage pipeline: (1) WBEB construction via data collection + LLM translation + human curation; (2) Pedagogical Reasoning via distillation (DeepSeek-R1 format guide, QwQ-32B teacher, Pedagogy-R1 student) and CoP prompting; (3) Mixed-method analysis using Schon's tripartite conception of professional reflection (reflection-in-action, on-action, for-action). CoP prompt refinement example: 'Please reason step by step' revised to 'Consider the pedagogical step by step'. Primary adaptation technique is knowledge-distillation + SFT/instruction-tuning on filtered teacher outputs. The paper explicitly frames its approach as distillation-based training pipeline (Sections 1.1, 2.1). Does NOT use RLHF/DPO/LoRA - full SFT instruction tuning on distilled data. GenAI disclosure: GPT-4o/GPT-4.1 for editing; Claude-3.7-sonnet and Gemini-2.5-Pro for code assistance.

title: YMCQ: Reasoning-Enhanced MCQ Generation
venue: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN EDUCATION, AIED 2025, PT VI
llm_base: Llama 3.1 Instruct 3.1 (8B); Llama 3.1 3.1 (70B (4-bit quantized, used only for synthetic data augmentation)); GPT-4o (unknown (used as baseline, not finetuned))
ft_quantization: bf16 mixed precision (training); Llama 3.1 70B used in 4-bit only for synthetic data generation
dataset_name: YMCQ (combined: SQuAD + HotpotQA + NarrativeQA + FairytaleQA + MCTest + RACE + EduQG, augmented with synthetic reasoning explanations and misconceptions); iSTART subset (55 K-12 texts) for evaluation
dataset_size: 337,175 training examples and 27,164 validation samples; ~300k questions augmented with explanations (of which ~85k are MCQs)
dataset_domain: General reading comprehension / educational MCQ (multi-domain: history, geography, science, technology, narratives)
personalization_mechanism: No explicit learner-profile personalization; questions are generated from context. The method targets pedagogical quality (distractor plausibility tied to misconceptions) rather than per-student adaptation.
subject_area: Multi-domain reading comprehension (history, geography, science, technology, literature) for K-12 education; evaluation performed on iSTART intelligent tutoring system subset
evaluation_metrics: pairwise human comparison (win/lose/tie with 5 annotators, 275 comparisons per pair); Bradley-Terry ranking scores; token entropy filtering (inference-time, not evaluation); cosine similarity via Sentence Transformers (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) for distractor/question de-duplication; factuality score via Bespoke-MiniCheck-7B fact-checking model
quantitative_results: Bradley-Terry scores: GPT-4o 2.89 > Human-authored 0.86 > YMCQ with explanations 0.70 > YMCQ without explanations 0.57. Pairwise: YMCQ w/ explanations beat YMCQ w/o explanations 49.1% vs 43.6% (ties 7.3%). YMCQ w/ explanations vs Human: 44.7% vs 49.8%. YMCQ w/ explanations vs GPT-4o: 34.9% vs 58.9%. YMCQ w/o explanations vs Human: 37.8% vs 57.8%. Human vs GPT-4o: 38.9% vs 55.3%. The explanation-augmented finetuned model narrows the gap with human-authored questions and remains competitive despite being far smaller than GPT-4o.
limitations: Distractor quality, although improved by incorporating misconceptions, still requires further research to reflect authentic student misunderstandings across diverse subjects and grade levels; External validity limited by small number of human evaluators (only 5 annotators); Absence of native English speakers among evaluators; Does not surpass GPT-4o due to much smaller model scale and less extensive alignment; Does not fully match human-authored questions in nuanced reasoning, structuring and domain expertise; No preference-based alignment yet (DPO proposed as future work)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: YMCQ: a reasoning-enhanced MCQ generation pipeline that (1) finetunes an open-source Llama 3.1 8B-Instruct to jointly generate question, reasoning for the correct answer, correct answer, misconception details and distractors; (2) synthetically augments >300k publicly available QA items with reasoning explanations and misconception-based distractor rationales produced by a 4-bit quantized Llama 3.1 70B; and (3) applies a multi-step inference-time filtering pipeline (min-p sampling, token-entropy filter, Sentence-Transformer-based duplicate distractor/question filtering, MiniCheck factuality filter, log-likelihood ranking) to ensure validity and diversity. The full model, code and YMCQ dataset are released open-source, providing a cost-effective, accessible alternative to proprietary systems.
notes: Training uses full fine-tuning (not LoRA/QLoRA) of Llama 3.1 8B-Instruct in bf16 mixed precision. The 70B model is used ONLY to generate synthetic reasoning/misconception explanations for data augmentation (quantized to 4-bit), not as the target of fine-tuning. Evaluation is on iSTART K-12 corpus (55 texts averaging ~500 words). Baselines: ablation without explanations, GPT-4o zero-shot, and human-authored questions. Future work: apply DPO using the pairwise comparison data. GitHub: https://github.com/upb-nlp/AIED-MCQ-with-Explanations. Published in AIED 2025 LNAI 15882, pp. 308-315.

title: Enhancing Educational Content Through AI-Assisted Transformation of Video Transcriptions
venue: IEEE Digit. Educ. MOOCS Conf., DEMOcon
llm_base: Gemini 2.0 Flash 2.0; GPT-4o; GPT-4o mini; Llama 3.3 70B 3.3 (70B); Gemini 2.5 Flash 2.5
dataset_name: media.upv.es video transcriptions (UPV repository)
dataset_size: 51,403 videos processed; 40 videos used for comparative model evaluation; 20 transcripts for Whisper/WhisperX quality assessment
dataset_domain: Higher education (university lecture videos across multiple subjects)
personalization_mechanism: Faculty editing interface in media.upv.es lets instructors review, modify, add or remove distractors and choose between short-answer and multiple-choice formats to match pedagogical preferences; no learner-profile adaptation
subject_area: Multidisciplinary university lectures (UPV repository); examples include SQL/databases (e.g., WHERE clause in SELECT statements)
evaluation_metrics: Utility rating on 1-10 scale by three evaluators (class notes, key ideas, abstracts, MCQs); token consumption per video (prompt/response); API cost in euros for 55,000 videos; count of questions filtered for referencing 'video'/'presenter'
quantitative_results: 51,403 videos processed, producing 255,464 paragraph-response questions and 1,021,845 short-answer questions plus class notes, summaries and abstracts. Utility ratings (1-10) for informational materials: GPT-4o 8.7/8.7/8.8 (notes/key ideas/abstract); GPT-4o mini 9/8.5/8.6; Gemini 2.0 Flash 8.9/8.6/8.9; Llama 3.1 70B 8/7.9/7.9. MCQ quality ratings (out of 10): GPT-4o 5; GPT-4o mini 4.5; Gemini 2.0 Flash 4.5; Llama 3.1 70B 4.3. Two-step distractor regeneration with Gemini 2.5 Flash raised MCQ rating to 5.75. Estimated token usage: 192.5M prompt + 302.5M output tokens for 55,000 videos. Total costs: GPT-4o EUR3,506; GPT-4o mini EUR211; Gemini 2.0 Flash EUR140; Llama 3.1 70B EUR355. Quality filtering removed 14,022 paragraph-response questions and 36,424 short-answer questions that referenced 'video' or 'presenter'.
limitations: Distractor generation for multiple-choice questions remains the weakest component, with ratings well below informational materials; Gemini occasionally ignored instructions and produced direct transcriptions instead of class notes (~1 in every 30 videos); Generated questions sometimes violate the constraint of not referencing 'video' or 'presenter', requiring post-hoc keyword filtering; Quality assessment is a major bottleneck because it requires subject-matter expertise and substantial human time per video; Results depend on Google's free-tier daily quota (recently reduced to 500 requests/day for Gemini 2.5 Flash), so scaling beyond this requires paid API usage; No comparison against fine-tuned or open-weights models beyond prompting; scope limited to prompt engineering
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Scalable prompt-engineering pipeline that transforms over 50,000 university video transcriptions (via WhisperX) into structured study notes, key-concept summaries, SEO-optimized abstracts and assessment items (paragraph, short-answer and 5-option MCQ with distractors), using a single six-step sequential prompt; systematic cost/quality comparison of GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, Llama 3.3 70B and Gemini 2.0 Flash, selecting Gemini 2.0 Flash as the best cost/quality trade-off, plus a two-step distractor regeneration approach with Gemini 2.5 Flash that improves MCQ quality.
notes: Paper uses only prompt engineering (no fine-tuning, no LoRA, no RAG). Output stored as JSON for integration in media.upv.es LMS; faculty review UI shown in Figure 1. Authors declare Claude 3.7/4 Sonnet was used to help structure introduction and state-of-the-art sections and format references. Whisper/WhisperX used for transcription quality. Some inconsistency between tables (Llama 3.3 70B vs Llama 3.1 70B) in the paper itself.

title: Automatic generation of introductory programming exercises with large language models
venue: Research and Practice in Technology Enhanced Learning
llm_base: GPT-3.5 Turbo gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 (unknown); GPT-4 Turbo gpt-4-0125-preview (unknown)
dataset_name: ExGen seed set (manually crafted Python exercises used as shots; split into 'concept' and 'domain' subsets)
dataset_size: 59 seed exercises (28 concept + 31 domain); 1404 generated exercises evaluated (468 per difficulty level) with GPT-3.5 Turbo, plus 702 additional with GPT-4 Turbo
dataset_domain: Introductory Python programming exercises (higher education)
personalization_mechanism: Student/instructor-specified difficulty level (easy/intermediate/hard), keyword types (simple, complex, domain), and seed exercises from a personalized local TinyDB database; few-shot prompting uses the student's current exercise and similar exercises from their personalized database as shots
subject_area: Introductory programming / Computer Science education (Python)
evaluation_metrics: Accuracy (percentage of ready-to-use exercises vs. manual inspection); average cost per ready-to-use exercise (USD); average generation time per ready-to-use exercise (seconds); filtering-check agreement with manual inspection; ratio of unique ready-to-use exercises vs. generated exercises
quantitative_results: GPT-3.5 Turbo few-shot accuracy: Easy 51.3%, Intermediate 20.9%, Hard 13.7% (vs zero-shot Easy 32.9%, Intermediate 19.6%, Hard 12.4%). Chained filter (compilation + unit-test + difficulty) agreement with manual inspection under few-shot: Easy 82.9%, Intermediate 85.5%, Hard 91.5% (vs unit-test-only 81.6%/54.7%/56.4%). GPT-4 Turbo few-shot accuracy: Easy 45.3%, Intermediate 37.2%, Hard 29.1% (costs ~20x GPT-3.5 Turbo; e.g., Hard cost $0.168 vs $0.016, time 23.4s vs 15.6s). Few-shot cost per easy ready-to-use exercise ~28% lower than zero-shot with GPT-3.5 Turbo. Total 1404 exercises evaluated with GPT-3.5 Turbo and 702 with GPT-4 Turbo.
limitations: LLM outputs are non-deterministic, affecting reproducibility and quality consistency; Manual evaluation done by a single experienced instructor (1st author); subjective bias possible despite multi-round triangulation; Generating 'intermediate' and 'hard' ready-to-use exercises remains difficult (accuracy still ~13-37%); Results may not generalize to newer LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o) or other programming languages; Seed dataset is small (59 exercises) and specific to the authors' introductory course; Restricted to Python; English-only prompts and exercises; Cost of GPT-4 Turbo is ~20x GPT-3.5 Turbo, limiting on-demand scalability; Difficulty check is itself LLM-based and probabilistic
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: ExGen: a VS Code plugin that generates on-demand, ready-to-use, customized introductory Python programming exercises at three difficulty levels using GPT-3.5/GPT-4 Turbo with zero-shot and few-shot prompting, combined with a novel chain of auto-filters (compilation check, unit testing, and LLM-based difficulty classification). Extensive evaluation with >1400 generated exercises shows few-shot + chained filters yields the best accuracy-cost tradeoff; provides empirical insights on prompting strategy, keyword types, example types, and the tradeoff between GPT-3.5 Turbo and GPT-4 Turbo for on-demand educational content generation.
notes: No fine-tuning is performed; the approach is purely prompt engineering with few-shot conversational prompting via the OpenAI Chat Completions API. The 'difficulty classification' filter is an LLM-as-judge step. Subject: Python introductory programming at Singapore Management University. TinyDB used for the student's personalized local exercise database. Funded by Singapore Ministry of Education (MOE2021-TRF-014). Data availability: 'might be provided upon request, subject to permission from the Ministry of Education, Singapore'. Relevant to TFM for: prompt-engineering vs fine-tuning baseline, difficulty-controlled generation, LLM-judge filtering, and human expert evaluation protocols for educational content.

title: NOVA: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assistant in Spanish for Parallel Computing Education with Large Language Models
venue: Applied Sciences (Switzerland)
dataset_name: NOVA Parallel Computing course corpus (Universidad Politecnica Salesiana)
dataset_size: 40 documents across 4 units (12 lecture slides, 20 practical guides, 8 quizzes); evaluation suite of 100 test cases (55 Q&A, 25 guides, 20 quizzes)
dataset_domain: Parallel Computing / higher-education STEM
personalization_mechanism: Metadata-based filtering by instructional unit and topic selected by the student; custom prompt templates per resource type (Q&A, quiz, practical guide); optional user-defined parameters (topic, programming language, additional instruction) sent via /qa, /quiz, /practice endpoints; role-based access (student/instructor) via JWT
subject_area: Parallel Computing (Computer Science; CUDA, PyCUDA, MPI, OpenMP, clustering, high-performance computing)
evaluation_metrics: Excellence rating (1-3 scale, Poor/Fair/Excellent via LLM-as-judge); coverage (retrieved fragments used in response); relevance; accuracy; average response time
quantitative_results: 92% of 100 test cases scored Excellent (score 3); breakdown by type: Q&A 94.5%, Practical Guides 88.0%, Quizzes 90.0% (no scores of 1 in any category). Average response time 21 s (simple Q&A 5-10 s; quizzes/guides 25-35 s). Retrieval coverage 72%. GPT-4o mini MMLU benchmark cited at 82%.
limitations: Evaluation limited to 100 test cases designed by the development team, without direct input from end users (real students) during validation; Coverage and relevance metrics rely on subjective interpretation of retrieval adequacy and may be influenced by manual annotation of metadata and corpus selection; No formal usability testing or inter-rater reliability analysis, limiting generalizability; Corpus specific to one institution/course (UPS Parallel Computing), potential overfitting to its content; Reliance on commercial LLMs (GPT-4o mini) and dedicated vector databases entails costs that may be challenging for some institutions; Coverage of 72% reflects limits of curated corpus (some answers required information not explicitly present in uploaded materials); Potential cultural/linguistic biases due to regional Spanish variations despite Spanish-language operation; No controlled experiment or inferential statistical analysis; comparisons with prior work are architectural/contextual only
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: NOVA: a modular RAG-based educational virtual assistant in Spanish for a university Parallel Computing course at Universidad Politecnica Salesiana. Integrates GPT-4o mini and Gemini Flash 2.0 with a Qdrant vector store indexed with text-embedding-3-small/ada-002 over curated course materials, exposed via FastAPI + Next.js + n8n workflows. Supports three output flows (Q&A, quizzes, practical guides with rubrics) grounded in instructor-validated content, achieving 92% excellent ratings and 72% retrieval coverage over 100 test cases. Addresses the gap of Spanish-language, domain-specific AI tutoring in advanced STEM.
notes: No fine-tuning is performed: adaptation is achieved purely through RAG (top-5 retrieval from Qdrant, text-embedding-3-small 768-dim and text-embedding-ada-002) and custom prompt engineering per resource type, with temperature tuned to 0.2-0.35. Stack: Python 3.10, LangChain, NLTK, Selenium, FastAPI, JWT, PostgreSQL, n8n, Next.js, Docker. The system is temporarily hosted at https://cloudcomputing.ups.edu.ec/asistente-educativo for academic evaluation. Study published in Applied Sciences 2025, 15, 8175 (MDPI).

title: Language Models for Educational Question Generation: Practical Challenges, Personalization Opportunities, and Parameter Optimization
venue: GENERATIVE SYSTEMS AND INTELLIGENT TUTORING SYSTEMS, ITS 2025, PT I
adaptation_technique: LoRA; SFT; prompting; few-shot; chain-of-thought; instruction-tuning
dataset_name: Custom software engineering course corpus (SWEBOK textbook v4.0, scholarly papers, web pages)
dataset_size: 6 course units; e.g., Software Engineering Process: 86 pages, 886 paragraphs, 2441 sentences, 46591 words; totals across 6 units ~282 pages, 3202 paragraphs, 8194 sentences, ~212628 words
dataset_domain: Software engineering (higher education)
personalization_mechanism: Half of the prompts specify a desired difficulty level (beginner, intermediate, advanced) based on Bloom's Taxonomy; generated questions evaluated via a Difficulty Match metric against prompted difficulty
subject_area: Software engineering (6 units: Scope of Software Engineering, Software Requirements, Software Design, Software Construction, Software Maintenance, Software Engineering Process)
evaluation_metrics: Difficulty Match % (DM); Relevance (1-5, higher better); Quality (1-5, higher better; includes readability); Expert assessment by software engineering expert with 20 years industry and university teaching experience
quantitative_results: Depending on settings, a question with the proper difficulty level was generated between 61.5% and 67.4% of the time. Gemma-7B average: DM 64.1%, Rel 4.39, Q 2.83. Gemma-7B-it average: DM 65.0%, Rel 4.54, Q 3.38 (better overall quality but struggled with beginner-level questions: only 18.2% beginner vs 7B's 41.7%). Best granularity with unit-only training: Sentences (DM 65.6%, Rel 4.70, Q 3.08). Best prompt styles: Roleplaying (highest quality avg Q 3.15), Few Shot (highest DM 67.4% and Rel 4.94), Direct Instruction (DM 64.3%, Rel 4.76, Q 3.12). LM-generated prompts (avg DM 63.6%, Rel 4.66, Q 3.00) outperformed human-created prompts (Human avg DM 63.2%, Rel 4.58, Q 2.93).
limitations: Cannot be certain findings on parameter optimization generalize to other courses or broader domains (optimal settings vary per task); Preparing training data was time-consuming and largely manual due to PDF extraction difficulties (multi-column layouts, interwoven non-learning text); Automatic question extraction from LM responses required considerable manual work; Few Shot and Chain-of-Thought responses particularly problematic; LMs often generated verbose responses with explanations/step-by-step reasoning instead of just the question, complicating extraction; Single expert evaluator (software engineering expert) introduces evaluator bias; Both Gemma 7B and 7B-it exhibited a quirk of asking essay-style or programming questions as beginner-level questions; Confusion on Unit 1 (SoSE) due to ambiguous term 'Scope'; No evaluation with real students (future work) and no answer grading (future work); Limited to short-answer questions (not multiple-choice); Only software engineering course studied
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Systematic study (84 experiments) of fine-tuning two small LMs (Gemma-7B and Gemma-7B-it) with LoRA rank-4 on a software engineering course corpus to generate educational short-answer questions at three Bloom difficulty levels. Evaluates granularity (page/paragraph/sentence), scope (unit vs course), and 7 prompt styles (Zero Shot, Few Shot, Chain-of-Thought, Direct Instruction, Roleplaying, Contextual, Decomposition) plus human prompts. Finds instruction-pretuned models better overall but weaker on beginner questions; sentence-granularity with unit-only data is best; Roleplaying prompts give highest quality; LM-generated prompts outperform human-created prompts.
notes: Published in ITS 2025 (LNCS 15723, pp. 144-158, Springer 2026) by Jason Bernard and Sabine Graf (Athabasca University). RQ coverage mapping: RQ2 (prompting strategies: 7 styles + human), RQ3 (multiformat generation: questions at 3 difficulty levels), RQ4 (personalization: difficulty-level prompting with Difficulty Match metric against Bloom's Taxonomy), RQ6 (evaluation: Difficulty Match, Relevance, Quality via expert), RQ7 (reproducibility: Gemma public on HuggingFace, LoRA hyperparams specified, dataset not shared). Fine-tuning method: LoRA rank 4, batch size 10, 20 epochs, 4x V100 32GB. Funded by NSERC Canada [RGPIN-2020-05837].

title: LOOKALIKE: Consistent Distractor Generation in Math MCQs
venue: PROCEEDINGS OF THE 20TH WORKSHOP ON INNOVATIVE USE OF NLP FOR BUILDING EDUCATIONAL APPLICATIONS, BEA 2025
dataset_name: Real-world math MCQ dataset (Eedi / large learning platform)
dataset_size: 1,434 math MCQs with 3 distractors and teacher-written error descriptions each
dataset_domain: Middle-school mathematics (ages 10-13; 41 distinct mathematical subtopics including Arithmetic, Fractions, Solving Equations)
personalization_mechanism: The system generates distractors consistent with common student errors/misconceptions but does not adapt outputs to individual student profiles
subject_area: Mathematics (middle-school math: arithmetic, fractions, solving equations; 41 subtopics)
evaluation_metrics: Exact match (distractor generation); LLM-as-a-Judge with GPT-4o-mini for mathematical equivalence (error generation); Human annotator ordinal ratings (Yes/Partially/No) on consistency; Quadratic-weighted Cohen's kappa (inter-annotator agreement); Linear Kappa (annotator vs GPT-4o-mini judge); Wilcoxon signed-rank test for statistical significance
quantitative_results: LookAlike achieves 51.56% exact match on distractor generation and 57.18% LLM-as-a-Judge accuracy on error generation (5-fold cross-validation). Outperforms SFT (44.76% / 46.68), DiVERT (45.64% / 47.72) by 6.8%/10.5% (distractor) and 5.92%/9.46% (error), comparable to DPO-GT (51.44% / 57.02). Improvements over DiVERT statistically significant (p<0.05, Wilcoxon signed-rank). Ablation: alternating per-batch/per-epoch regularization outperforms DPOP and RPO. Human evaluation: LookAlike mean consistency rating = 0.587 vs SFT 0.400 vs ground truth 0.812; highest inter-annotator agreement (quadratic-weighted kappa = 0.740).
limitations: Currently restricted to middle-school mathematics domain; extension to science or language arts would require modifying error/distractor representations; Preference mining assumes the base model is sufficiently trained to surface pedagogically meaningful contrastive samples; Exact match criterion for labeling dispreferred samples is conservative and strict (softer similarity or human judgments could refine preference mining); Template overfitting: LookAlike sometimes generates errors overfit to error-distractor templates from similar questions in training; Generalizability to math MCQs from unseen topics remains unexplored; Inconsistent generations still persist despite improvements
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: LookAlike: a method for generating consistent distractors and error explanations in math MCQs by (a) mining synthetic preference pairs from model generation inconsistencies (instead of human-annotated or heuristic pairs) and (b) alternating SFT and DPO optimization at per-batch/per-epoch level as regularization to stabilize DPO training; achieves 51.6% distractor accuracy and 57.2% error accuracy, outperforming SFT and DiVERT baselines on a real-world 1,434 math MCQ dataset.
notes: Base model is MetaMath-Mistral 7B (Yu et al., 2024b). All fine-tuning uses LoRA (r=128, alpha=256). Code available at https://github.com/umass-ml4ed/LookAlike. Uses GPT-4o-mini as LLM-as-a-Judge. Dataset comes from Eedi learning platform. Authors: Parikh, Scarlatos, Fernandez, Woodhead, Lan (UMass Amherst, Eedi), 2025, arXiv:2505.01903v2.

title: Using Old Lessons for New AI - A Trainer for Project Risk Management
venue: FLEXIBLE QUERY ANSWERING SYSTEMS, FQAS 2025
llm_base: unspecified (various models tested via Vercel.AI platform)
dataset_name: Project Risk Management (PRM) course training materials
dataset_size: not specified (a small set of prompt-input-output triples crafted from an existing PRM course)
dataset_domain: Project Risk Management (higher-education course)
personalization_mechanism: Multi-dimensional personalization via prompt-input-output triples that can be diversified project-wise (concrete project specifics) or standard-wise (e.g., PMI or PRINCE methodology as input); ITS/ALP context uses user feedback as input to adapt responses
subject_area: Project Risk Management (PRM) / project management education
evaluation_metrics: qualitative assessment of prompt adherence; conceptual understanding; scenario analysis; quantitative reasoning; process application; ethical considerations (qualitative dimensions evaluated via test triples)
quantitative_results: No quantitative metrics reported. The paper presents a qualitative case study: the fine-tuned model was evaluated with test triples across five dimensions (conceptual understanding, scenario analysis, quantitative reasoning, process application, ethical considerations) by critically comparing generated outputs against expected outputs.
limitations: No concrete base LLM, hyperparameters, dataset size, or quantitative results reported; Prompt engineering complexity: designing effective and diverse prompts requires careful consideration; Data preparation overhead: building prompt-input-output triples is time-consuming and resource-intensive; Risk of overfitting to specific prompts used in training (poor generalization to slightly different prompts); Evaluation requires more nuanced metrics than simple accuracy; Risk of memorization vs. genuine learning to follow instructions; Not all ITS architectures support every instructional strategy; pedagogical alignment must be considered; Quality and relevance of training data is crucial to mitigate overfitting
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes a simplified triples-based (prompt-input-output) fine-tuning methodology to reuse existing educational training materials as fine-tuning data for LLM-powered Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS) and Adaptive Learning Platforms (ALP). Demonstrated via a step-by-step case study applied to a Project Risk Management (PRM) course, showing how prompt-input-output triples can be crafted, used for instruction tuning, and evaluated across multiple pedagogical dimensions (conceptual understanding, scenario analysis, quantitative reasoning, process application, ethical considerations).
notes: Conceptual / methodological paper with an illustrative case study rather than an empirical fine-tuning experiment. No specific base LLM is named (models were tested via the Vercel.AI platform/AI Toolkit for TypeScript). No hyperparameters, dataset sizes, or quantitative benchmark results are reported. The triples-based approach is explicitly characterized as a form of instruction tuning, with few-shot examples possible inside prompts. Personalization is discussed at a conceptual level (adapting outputs to project-specific context or standards such as PMI/PRINCE). Published in FQAS 2025 (LNAI 16119), Springer, 2026.

title: Enhancing Reading Comprehension: A Comparative Study of Models for Generating Questions in Children's Storybooks
venue: APIT - Asia Pac. Inf. Technol. Conf.
llm_base: T5-base t5-base-squad-qag (Hugging Face) (220M); Llama 2 Llama-2-7b-hf (7B); Mistral 7B Mistral-7B-v0.1 / Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 (7.3B)
dataset_name: FairytaleQA
dataset_size: 10,580 QA pairs (8,548 train / 1,025 val / 1,007 test) from 278 fairy tale storybooks from Project Gutenberg
dataset_domain: Children's fairy tale storybooks (pre-K to 8th grade, narrative reading comprehension)
personalization_mechanism: No per-student profile personalization; questions are generated from story sections without individual adaptation.
subject_area: Reading comprehension / narrative understanding (children's literature)
evaluation_metrics: ROUGE-L; BLEU-4; training/validation loss; inference time (seconds)
quantitative_results: Prompting (0-shot / 10-shot) ROUGE-L: T5-base 0.41 / 0.43; Llama 2 7B 0.32 / 0.44; Mistral 7B 0.37 / 0.40. Prompting BLEU-4: T5-base 0.17 / 0.19; Llama 2 7B 0.06 / 0.17; Mistral 7B 0.10 / 0.13. Fine-tuning (LoRA) ROUGE-L / BLEU-4: T5-base 0.48 / 0.25; Llama 2 7B 0.53 / 0.32; Mistral 7B 0.58 / 0.39 (best model). Generation time: T5-base is fastest; fine-tuning is slower than prompting (Mistral 7B FT ~28s vs 0-shot ~1.5s).
limitations: Overfitting observed in Llama 2 7B and Mistral 7B after epoch 2 (validation loss increases); Evaluation limited to English and the narrative domain of children's fairy tales (FairytaleQA); No human evaluation with experts or children; only automatic metrics (ROUGE-L, BLEU-4); Other PEFT techniques (QLoRA, adapters, prefix-tuning) and RLHF/DPO are not explored; Fine-tuning requires significantly more time and resources (GPU A100 40GB) than prompting; Only the question is generated, not distractors or MCQ format; answer generation is not covered; Limited few-shot example count (10) with no systematic exploration of the optimal number; Pedagogical quality and alignment with cognitive levels (Bloom's taxonomy, etc.) are not analyzed
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Empirical comparative study of three open-source LLMs (T5-base 220M, Llama 2 7B, Mistral 7B) and three strategies (zero-shot prompting, 10-shot prompting, LoRA fine-tuning) for automatic reading comprehension question generation in children's fairy tales using FairytaleQA. Demonstrates that LoRA fine-tuning outperforms prompting at the cost of higher training time, and that fine-tuned Mistral 7B achieves the best performance (ROUGE-L 0.58, BLEU-4 0.39).
notes: Paper published at APIT 2025 (7th Asia Pacific Information Technology Conference, Hong Kong, January 10-12, 2025). Authors: Thunpitcha Sattabun and Thitirat Siriborvornratanakul (NIDA, Bangkok, Thailand). Explicit educational context (K-12 reading comprehension). Relevant to the TFM for: (1) systematic comparison of prompting vs fine-tuning across open-source LLMs, (2) use of LoRA as PEFT with specifically documented configurations, (3) application to educational material generation (questions) with a validated educational dataset (FairytaleQA, validated with 120 pre-K/K children), (4) evaluation with standard NLP metrics. Notable gaps: no per-student profile personalization, no evaluation with students or teachers, no multiple output formats, English only.

title: An Adaptable Client-Server Architecture for Generating Educational Content using Large Language Models
venue: BULLETIN OF THE TECHNICAL COMMITTEE ON LEARNING TECHNOLOGY
dataset_name: Instructor-uploaded course materials (textbooks, lecture notes, supplementary resources) indexed via MongoDB Atlas Vector Search
dataset_size: unknown
dataset_domain: General educational content; demonstrated example uses Data Structures and Algorithms course (Linked Lists LO)
output_format: quiz-mcq; quiz-short-answer; explanation; lesson-plan; exercise; feedback
personalization_mechanism: Structured course model (courses, units, LOs, KCs) plus student models and instructor-selected metadata (course, unit, LO, content type, Question Validation Criteria, pedagogical frameworks such as GROW model or Feynman's Technique) injected into the prompt via LangChain templates; RAG retrieves instructor-specific course documents to ground content. Iterative HAI refinement loop (EMGen) lets instructors steer outputs across multiple rounds.
subject_area: General (architecture demonstrated with Computer Science/Data Structures example; intended as domain-agnostic)
evaluation_metrics: Subject matter expert quality ratings of generated MCQs (favorable overall, but no statistically significant differences across prompting strategies)
quantitative_results: Preliminary results across three prompting strategies (zero-shot single prompt, two-round critic-based, multi-round per-criterion refinement) showed no statistically significant differences in quality of generated questions; subject matter experts rated overall quality as favorable. No numeric scores, accuracy figures, or effect sizes reported.
limitations: Preliminary evaluation only; no real-world user studies with instructors or students yet; No statistically significant improvement across the three prompting/refinement strategies tested; Validation criteria (QVCs) are not yet precisely defined enough to meaningfully improve quality; System currently relies solely on prompt engineering and RAG; no fine-tuning, RLHF, or knowledge graph integration implemented; No quantitative metrics (accuracy, BLEU, ROUGE, expert agreement scores) reported; Content quality depends on instructor-uploaded materials and manually crafted prompt templates; Risk of LLM hallucinations acknowledged; mitigation via RAG but not quantified
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
