key_contribution: Modular client-server web architecture (Next.js + Node.js + MongoDB + LangChain + OpenAI GPT) for LLM-based generation of educational content, combining structured curriculum/course modeling, RAG over instructor-uploaded documents, and prompt engineering with Question Validation Criteria. Validated through three applications (QuizGen for MCQs with QVCs, QuizMaster for adaptive formative assessment, EMGen for iterative HAI content refinement) demonstrating human-in-the-loop generation, validation, and editing workflows.
notes: Article is an architecture/system paper rather than a fine-tuning study. Uses OpenAI GPT models (primarily gpt-4o-mini per Fig. 3 UI screenshot) via LangChain; supports Anthropic, Mistral, Vertex AI, AWS as alternative providers. Embeddings via OpenAI embedding model stored in MongoDB Atlas Vector Search; files in AWS S3. Prompting strategies compared: (1) zero-shot single prompt with all QVCs embedded, (2) two-round critic-based self-refinement, (3) multi-round per-criterion iterative refinement. Pedagogical frameworks integrated: Bloom's Taxonomy (referenced), GROW model, Feynman's Technique, Question Validation Criteria (7 criteria: Relevance/Alignment, Clarity, Fairness/Bias, Plausibility of Distractors, Readability, Test-wise/Logic Cues, Independency of Options). Authors at Athabasca University (Canada); funded by NSERC and Alberta Innovates. Future work explicitly mentions fine-tuning, RLHF, expert feedback loops, and knowledge graphs as next directions.

title: QUIZ-TUBE: ENHANCING VIDEO-BASED LEARNING WITH AUTOMATED AI QUIZ GENERATION
venue: Journal of Engineering and Technology for Industrial Applications
dataset_name: Educational YouTube video transcripts (custom)
dataset_size: 1000 transcripts
dataset_domain: Educational YouTube videos (general educational content)
personalization_mechanism: User-selected parameters (difficulty level: easy/medium/hard; number of questions: 5/10/15; topic focus) and adaptive difficulty adjustment based on historical quiz performance
subject_area: General educational content from YouTube videos (domain-agnostic)
evaluation_metrics: BLEU; ROUGE-L; Accuracy; F1; Perplexity
quantitative_results: Pre-finetuning vs post-finetuning: BLEU 45.3 to 68.7; ROUGE-L 57.2 to 74.8; Accuracy 64.5% to 81.9%
limitations: Transcript availability: system depends on YouTube Transcript API, and fails or degrades for videos without transcripts or non-English content. Only English content is supported (multilingual support proposed as future work). Only MCQ output format, no subjective/open-ended questions. No human-based evaluation of question pedagogical quality (only automatic metrics). Small fine-tuning dataset (1,000 transcripts). Gemma-9B context window limited to 8,192 tokens, constraining long-video processing. No user study or learning outcome measurement reported.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Quiz-Tube: an AI-powered web platform that converts educational YouTube videos into interactive MCQ quizzes using a LoRA/PEFT fine-tuned Gemma-9B model, with transcript extraction via YouTube Transcript API, RAG-based question generation, user-customizable difficulty and quantity, and adaptive difficulty based on performance history. Reports large gains over baseline Gemma-9B (BLEU 45.3 to 68.7, ROUGE-L 57.2 to 74.8, Accuracy 64.5% to 81.9%).
notes: Base model is Gemma-9B (Gemma 2 family, confirmed by the stated 3,584-dim embeddings and 32 layers). Paper discusses both PEFT (LoRA, Adapters, Prefix Tuning) and ReFT/LoReFT conceptually, but states LoRA was the technique actually applied. Dataset augmented with synthetic Q-A pairs generated via Gemini-1.5-flash (knowledge-distillation-like augmentation, though paper does not use that term). Pipeline also uses TF-IDF keyword extraction, NER, and knowledge graph construction (ConceptNet/Wikidata) upstream of the LLM. Evaluation is purely automatic (BLEU, ROUGE-L, accuracy) with pre/post fine-tuning comparison; no human-expert, student, or LLM-judge evaluation reported, and no external baseline beyond the pre-fine-tuned Gemma-9B. Accuracy metric as defined is ambiguous (described as proportion of correctly generated quiz questions/answers). No public links to code, model checkpoints, or dataset provided in the paper.

title: Fine-Tuning Lightweight LLMs With Human-Curated Data on Electrical Circuit Fundamentals for E-Learning
venue: Computer Applications in Engineering Education
dataset_name: Electrical Circuit Fundamentals (FEELE/TCIRC) human-curated dataset (HALO)
dataset_size: 75 topics across 11 chapters; generated 200 QA pairs per category per topic; final ~50k conversation examples covering 150k QA pairs after n=100 augmentation and conversation chaining (3 QA pairs per conversation)
dataset_language: bilingual (Portuguese original, translated to English for training via gpt-4o-2024-11-20)
dataset_domain: electrical engineering / electrical circuit analysis (DC and AC circuits, LCM, NVM)
personalization_mechanism: QA pairs generated per topic aligned to Bloom's revised taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create); chat-based conversational format with system prompts; bilingual (PT/EN) access for learners; focus on domain-aware tutor for first-year ECE students
subject_area: electrical engineering / electrical circuit analysis (Loop Current Method, Node Voltage Method)
evaluation_metrics: 5-point Likert scale on 5 dimensions (student eval): D1 Correctness, D2 Clarity, D3 Learning value, D4 Relevance, D5 Writing style; 5-point Likert scale on 5 dimensions (expert eval): D1 Correctness, D2 Logical structure, D3 Completeness, D4 Precision, D5 Conciseness; Mean rating and % of 4-5 ratings; Kruskal-Wallis test, Pearson Chi-square, Bonferroni-corrected pairwise comparisons, standardized residuals; Training loss (cross-entropy); Hardware metrics: VRAM usage, CPU/RAM utilization, GPU utilization, power draw, elapsed time (preprocessing, training, inference, model saving)
quantitative_results: Blind user study with 77 first-year ECE students and 12 higher-education experts. Student eval (mean rating, %4-5): halo-lcm-full-8B scored D1=4.24(85.7%), D2=4.29(86.8%), D3=3.81(67.0%), D4=4.20(83.5%), D5=4.04(74.7%), within 1 Likert point of gpt-4.5-preview (D1=4.45/90.5%, D2=4.28/91.6%, D3=3.95/71.6%, D4=4.48/92.6%, D5=4.26/86.3%); halo-lcm-full-1B: D1=4.43(95.7%), D2=4.20(83.0%), D3=3.80(68.1%), D4=4.36(93.6%), D5=3.91(72.3%). In D2 (clarity) halo-lcm-full-8B slightly surpassed gpt-4.5 (4.29 vs 4.28). Untuned baselines Llama-3.1-8B and Llama-3.2-1B scored lower across all dimensions. Kruskal-Wallis global tests significant for all dimensions (p<0.001; D3 p=0.014). Bonferroni post-hoc showed tuned models NOT statistically different from gpt-4.5. Training setup: baseline exam analysis of 260 FEELE students found 28.5% solved LCM problem without error, 61.64% miscounted equations, 95.89% mislabelled independent meshes. End-to-end pipeline (<4 min training): Llama 3.1 8B total=361.61s (training 266.91s), Llama 3.2 3B total=219.90s, Llama 3.2 1B total=207.02s. Hardware: single RTX 3090 (24GB VRAM, 23.69GB used / 81.7%); RAM 9.15-9.17GB; GPU utilization near saturation at ~350W. Expert eval (N=7-24): halo-lcm-full-1B D4=4.57(100%), D5=4.71(100%); gpt-4.5 D2=4.33(87.5%), D3=4.17(75%), D4=4.33(83.3%). Expert ratings largely corroborated student rankings though with caveats on small N for some fine-tuned variants.
limitations: Dataset intentionally focused on algebraic circuit analysis methods only (as proof-of-concept); excluded transient and AC circuit analysis; Full step-by-step mathematical resolutions deliberately excluded to avoid overfitting; may limit procedural problem-solving capability; Base model benchmarks (GPT-4.5 official parameters undisclosed) prevent head-to-head reasoning comparison; LLaMA models score poorly on GPQA and MATH benchmarks, expected to provide incorrect answers on advanced math; Small sample size in expert evaluations for some fine-tuned variants (N=7) limits statistical power and robustness of mean comparisons; Potential hallucination risk mitigated via prompt engineering (custom dictionary, error mitigation) but not eliminated; 20% sparse expert verification rate of QA dataset during generation; not all 200 QA pairs per topic individually verified; Data contamination risk in translation step mitigated by splitting into parts and custom dictionary, but not fully eliminated; Evaluation restricted to three prompts for students (P8, P9, P11) due to workload constraints (~2 min per pair); Bonferroni-adjusted post-hoc for D3 (learning value) largely non-significant; robust differences between tuned models and commercial baseline could not be established for perceived learning gains; Kruskal-Wallis statistic reported from SPSS adjusted for ties but inference assumes independence not fully validated across prompt-response pairs
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ7; RQ8
key_contribution: Demonstrates that QLoRA fine-tuning of lightweight open-source LLMs (Llama 3.2 1B/3B and Llama 3.1 8B) on a bilingual human-curated domain-specific dataset for electrical circuit analysis (Loop Current Method) can yield tutoring assistants rated within 1 Likert point of GPT-4.5 by 77 first-year ECE students, with complete end-to-end training pipeline running in under 7 min on a single consumer-grade GPU (RTX 3090, 24GB VRAM). Also contributes a validated error taxonomy derived from 260 student exam scripts, a Bloom-aligned QA dataset generation methodology via knowledge distillation from GPT-4o, and open access to the curated dataset. Fine-tuned models were consistently preferred over untuned baselines especially for clarity and task coverage, validated through both student (N=77) and expert (N=12) blind evaluations.
notes: Dataset hosted at http://urisolve.pt/app/nlp (freely available). Distillation teacher model: gpt-4o-2024-11-20. Translation performed via gpt-4o-2024-11-20 with custom dictionary. Commercial baseline for comparison: gpt-4.5-preview-2025-02-27. Fine-tuned models branded as 'halo-lcm-full-{1B,8B}' and 'halo-nvm-full-{1B,8B}'. Keywords: electrical engineering education, LLM data set curation, LLM human evaluation, loop current method, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, QLoRA. Code availability not explicitly stated (data availability statement says data from corresponding author upon reasonable request, though dataset URL is provided). Model weights public availability not explicitly stated. LoRA alpha not reported in paper text. Training hardware: 2x RTX 3090 available but only one used. Loss function: token-level cross-entropy (default). Chat format: Alpaca structure converted to conversational (3 QA pairs per conversation). Framework: Hugging Face SFTTrainer with BitsAndBytes 4-bit NF4 quantization. Affiliation: ISEP Porto / INESC TEC FEUP Portugal. Published in Computer Applications in Engineering Education 2026, vol 34, e70176.

title: Advancing AI in Higher Education: A Comparative Study of Large Language Model-Based Agents for Exam Question Generation, Improvement, and Evaluation
venue: Algorithms
dataset_name: Custom 4-courses dataset (Data Science, Web Design, Integrated Systems, Operating Systems) derived from lecture notes, slides, textbook excerpts, laboratory guides; published model: vlada22/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-finki-edu-4courses
dataset_size: 3605 valid agent responses in final evaluation dataset; original exam questions distributed across 4 courses (counts range 126-414 per cell in Table 1)
dataset_domain: Higher education - Computer Science (Data Science, Web Design, Integrated Systems, Operating Systems)
personalization_mechanism: Domain adaptation to specific university courses and Bloom's taxonomy levels (Knowledge-Based, Application-Based, Creation/Evaluation); question variants tailored to cognitive level and question design (single-step/multi-step); no per-student personalization
subject_area: Computer Science (Data Science, Web Design, Integrated Systems, Operating Systems)
evaluation_metrics: Averaged weighted absolute error (Task 1 alignment); Averaged meta score 1-10 (Tasks 2 and 3: alignment, clarity, difficulty, originality); Self-reflection error (agent self-score vs meta ground-truth); Signed over-/under-grading error; Linear mixed-effects model coefficients with 95% CI; Wilcoxon signed-rank test; Paired t-test; Bland-Altman limits of agreement
quantitative_results: Final dataset: 3605 valid responses x 3 agents x 10 subdimensions = 108,150 long-format records. Task 1 (alignment error, lower=better): Fine-Tuned LLM intercept 0.619; VectorGraphRAG +0.503 and VectorRAG +0.215 baseline error, but negative interactions with single-step (-1.271 and -1.134). Explanation quality subgrades reduce errors (specificity_relevance coef -0.431). Task 2 (meta score, higher=better): Fine-Tuned LLM intercept 7.812; VectorRAG +0.234, VectorGraphRAG +0.193; explanation-quality coefficients ~0.2-0.35. Task 3: Fine-Tuned LLM intercept 7.969; originality coef +0.336. Self-reflection: Fine-Tuned LLM overestimates by +1.3 to +1.5; VectorRAG/VectorGraphRAG near +0.1 to +0.2. Real-world validation (100 human-created questions, Task 1): mean agent 6.88 vs human 6.65, mean difference 0.23, p=0.1224 (no significant difference); LoA [-2.62, 3.08].
limitations: Expert judgments retained subjectivity; review process relied on norms of a single institution. Partial real-world validation only on Task 1 with 100 questions; Tasks 2 and 3 not validated at scale. Focus on text-centric STEM-based courses; humanities/social sciences unexplored. Approach hinges on carefully curated lecture notes and condensed texts rather than raw unstructured sources. Meta-evaluator tied to GPT-4o and specific prompt design (reproducibility depends on that ecosystem). Scalability challenges: fine-tuning, knowledge graphs, and real-time retrieval require significant compute. No direct student feedback on AI-generated items. Fine-tuned LLM systematically overestimates own performance (+1 to +1.5 points). Meta-evaluator (auto) shows systematic biases vs human experts (over-scoring fine-tuned LLM, under-scoring retrieval-based agents).
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_contribution: Systematic framework that compares three LLM-based agents (VectorRAG, VectorGraphRAG, Fine-Tuned Llama-3.1-8B via QLoRA/SFT) for three higher-education exam-question tasks (alignment evaluation, question improvement, new question generation) across 4 university courses and Bloom's taxonomy levels, with a human-supervised GPT-4o meta-evaluator and mixed-effects modeling to derive task-agent matching guidelines for responsible AI integration in academic assessment.
notes: Published model on HuggingFace: vlada22/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-finki-edu-4courses (ref [47]). Code available on GitHub: vlada22/advancing-ai-in-higher-education (ref [53]). Data Availability Statement says raw data available on request (not publicly due to ongoing research), hence dataset_public=partial. Fine-tuning used SFTTrainer with QLoRA + LoRA targeting query/key/value/projection (specific rank/alpha not disclosed in paper text). Output formats: open-ended essay-style exam questions, not MCQ; closest vocab mapping is quiz-short-answer plus exercise (for application-based) and explanation/feedback (for the agent's textual justifications and improvement feedback). rq_coverage assumes standard SLR RQs on LLM fine-tuning for educational content generation (base LLM, adaptation technique, dataset, output format, personalization, evaluation, limitations, reproducibility).

title: Design, Development, and Evaluation of an Intelligent Generation System for Contextualized Primary School Science Test Questions
venue: Int. Conf. Educ. Technol. Manag., ICETM
dataset_name: SciQ-Gen knowledge base (People's Education Press & Daxiang Press textbooks grades 3-6, manually compiled knowledge framework, contextualized teaching cases and sample questions)
dataset_size: unknown (evaluation used 228 items: 76 AI-generated + 76 regional + 76 international)
dataset_domain: primary school science (elementary, grades 3-6)
personalization_mechanism: Dynamic BRTE (Background-Role-Task-Example) prompt framework: Role and Task are dynamically set according to question-type requirements; dual-channel RAG retrieves context and scientific knowledge aligned to themes/knowledge points; no student-model personalization
subject_area: science (elementary school science, STEM)
evaluation_metrics: Cronbach's Alpha (inter-rater reliability); 5-point Likert scale across 13 secondary indicators; Mean and Standard Deviation per dimension; ANOVA (F, p-values); Four primary dimensions: Scenario/Context Quality (A), Question Quality (B), Knowledge and Skill Depth (C), Inquiry Level (D)
quantitative_results: Cronbach's Alpha = 0.936 across 13 dimensions (3 experts). N=76 items per group (AI, Regional, International). Dimension A (Context Quality): AI M=12.16 (SD=0.99), Regional M=11.12 (SD=1.89), International M=12.22 (SD=1.04), F=15.477, p<0.001. Dimension B (Question Quality): AI M=9.22 (SD=1.76), Regional M=7.14 (SD=1.37), International M=10.29 (SD=1.36), F=85.592, p<0.001. Dimension C (Knowledge/Skill Depth): AI M=8.98 (SD=1.28), Regional M=7.05 (SD=1.87), International M=8.76 (SD=1.69), F=31.831, p<0.001. Dimension D (Inquiry Level): AI M=11.69 (SD=3.23), Regional M=7.35 (SD=2.17), International M=8.56 (SD=2.98), F=47.619, p<0.001. AI group surpassed both comparison groups on Dimensions C and D; slightly weaker than International on A and B.
limitations: Capability in generating items that combine images and text is still insufficient; Performance is dependent on the base models (GPT-4o and Kling 2.1) and inherits their limitations in scientific accuracy and creativity; Focused only on elementary school science; transferability to other subjects (history, geography) or stages (middle/high school) requires further validation; Evaluators were primarily experienced educational researchers; future work should include frontline teachers and students; AI group shows larger variability (SD=3.23 in Dimension D), requiring manual screening and correction for large-scale deployment; Only 3 experts participated in the evaluation
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_contribution: Design and validation of SciQ-Gen, an intelligent generation system for contextualized primary school science test questions that integrates a Dual-Channel Retrieval-Augmented Generation architecture (separating context from scientific knowledge) with an iterative Generate-Evaluate-Revise-Reevaluate closed-loop process and Chain-of-Thought. Also proposes a four-dimensional quality evaluation framework (Scenario Quality, Question Quality, Knowledge & Skill Depth, Inquiry Level) with 13 secondary indicators. Comparative experiment vs. regional exams and international assessments (PISA, TIMSS) shows AI-generated items match or exceed human-designed items especially in Knowledge Depth and Inquiry Level.
notes: Base model GPT-4o is used for text generation and Kling AI 2.1 for multimodal (image) generation. No fine-tuning is performed; adaptation is entirely prompt-based plus RAG. BRTE prompt framework (Background-Role-Task-Example) is adapted and innovated upon. Dual-channel RAG: unstructured channel for context/background stories, structured channel for scientific knowledge/themes/knowledge points. Three-dimensional evaluation rubric drives the iterative refinement. Equal weighting across 4 dimensions. Double-blind expert evaluation with 3 experts via SPSS. Conference paper: 2025 the 8th International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM), IEEE, pp. 335-341. DOI: 10.1109/ICETM67477.2025.11413584.

title: A Contextualized Learner-Profiling Transformer Architecture for Adaptive Grammar Error Diagnosis and Instruction
venue: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
dataset_name: Lang-8 Learner Corpus
dataset_size: Not specified (Kaggle Lang-8 dataset; split 80% train / 10% val / 10% test; filtered to keep only sentences with errors)
dataset_language: English (ESL learner writing with native-speaker corrections)
dataset_domain: ESL grammar error correction (Grammatical Error Correction - GEC)
personalization_mechanism: Dynamic learner error profile vector F=[f1,f2,...,fk] tracking frequency of each error type per learner; adaptive attention reweighting A' = A - alpha*delta_rep + beta*delta_align based on learner error history; personalized feedback generation g(F,A') conditioned on individual error patterns; profile continuously updated at inference time without retraining
subject_area: English as a Second Language (ESL) - grammar instruction
evaluation_metrics: BLEU; GLEU; Full Sentence Accuracy; Levenshtein Edit Distance; Error Reduction Rate (ERR); User satisfaction (Likert scale questionnaire: usability, helpfulness, recommendation, learning, explanation interest); Reduction of recurring error types across learning sessions
quantitative_results: AMT-T5 (full model): BLEU=78.9 (+/-1.2), GLEU=90.7 (+/-1.0), Full Sentence Accuracy=82.6% (Table IV reports 75.3%; abstract/comparison Table IX reports 82.6%), Levenshtein Edit Distance=1.7 (avg), ERR=91.2%. Ablation: T5-base fine-tuned BLEU=72.8/GLEU=86.5/Acc=75.3%; +Multi-task BLEU=75.1/GLEU=88.4/Acc=78.0%; +Adaptive Feedback BLEU=77.0/GLEU=89.6/Acc=80.4%; +DPO+KD (full) BLEU=78.9/GLEU=90.7/Acc=82.6%. Baselines: GECToR (BLEU=58.3, GLEU=71.6, Acc=62.4%, ERR=77.5%), BART-GEC (BLEU=63.5, GLEU=78.1, Acc=68.7%, ERR=83.0%), RoBERTa-GEC (BLEU=66.1, GLEU=80.3, Acc=71.2%, ERR=85.7%). Approx. 18-22% improvement over prior models. User study (N=60 intermediate ESL learners): Easy to Use=95%, Helpful Grammar Feedback=92%, Would Recommend=88%, Learned from Corrections=83%, Interested in Explanation Feature=93%. Failure cases: idiomatic expressions (14%), nested clauses (11%).
limitations: Fails with idiomatic phrases and colloquial expressions not well represented in training data (14% of failures); Intricate sentence structures with nested clauses or unconventional punctuation yield incomplete/partial corrections (11% of failures); Overcorrection: unnecessarily edits grammatically correct sentences; Under-correction: minor misinterpretations overlooked; Evaluation provides only exploratory evidence of instructional utility, not longitudinal long-term learning outcomes; Training data (Lang-8) may not represent all ESL error patterns adequately; Limited to English ESL context; not evaluated in multilingual or low-resource settings; Small user study sample (N=60 intermediate-level learners only); No formal hypothesis testing for long-term grammatical learning
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes AMT-T5, an Adaptive Multi-Task T5 encoder-decoder framework that integrates grammatical error correction, error-type classification, and explanation-based personalized feedback in a unified transformer architecture. Introduces a learner-aware attention reweighting mechanism conditioned on dynamically updated learner error profiles, combined with supervised fine-tuning, DPO for pedagogical alignment, and Knowledge Distillation for efficient deployment. Provides a reusable methodological blueprint for learner-centered educational NLP systems that combine computational metrics with pedagogical indicators.
notes: Article published in IJACSA Vol. 17 No. 1, 2026. LLM base is T5-base (encoder-decoder, Hugging Face Transformers); implemented with Python/PyTorch. Training used NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPU, 64GB RAM. Full fine-tuning (not PEFT/LoRA) is applied together with DPO and Knowledge Distillation (teacher=fine-tuned T5-base, student=smaller distilled model). Adaptive attention reweighting is a novel learner-conditioned mechanism rather than a standard PEFT technique - tagged as instruction-tuning due to multi-task prefixed input format. Full Sentence Accuracy reported inconsistently in the paper (75.3% in Table IV body vs 82.6% in abstract and comparative Table IX for full AMT-T5); both values retained in quantitative_results. Dataset is Lang-8 (public on Kaggle, ref [25]); code and trained model availability not stated in the paper.

title: Developing a multi-layer agent framework to enhance AI-generated educational questions for cybersecurity
venue: Journal of Umm Al-Qura University for Engineering and Architecture
dataset_name: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide (Exam SY0-701)
dataset_size: 340 book-based questions across 17 chapters (20 per chapter); authors state 360 questions in abstract
dataset_domain: cybersecurity / CompTIA Security+ certification
personalization_mechanism: User-provided parameters (topic, difficulty level, Bloom's Taxonomy level) sent in the API payload; difficulty progression and cognitive-level criteria steer question generation per learner level (e.g., beginner)
subject_area: cybersecurity (CompTIA Security+ certification)
evaluation_metrics: Bloom's Taxonomy weighted score (Remembering 20%, Understanding 20%, Applying 25%, Analyzing 25%, Evaluating 5%, Creating 5%) on 0-10 per level; 100-point weighted total; Topic coverage score (x/10 per Security+ domain, 39/50 total); Scenario-based depth; Topic distribution; Difficulty variance; Exam relevance
quantitative_results: AI-generated questions scored 74.5/100 vs. 88/100 for book-based questions (averaged over Chapters 2, 3, 4). Per Bloom level (generated vs book): Remembering 8 vs 9, Understanding 9 vs 10, Applying 7 vs 9, Analyzing 7 vs 8, Evaluating 6 vs 8, Creating 5 vs 7. Topic alignment with CompTIA domains: 39/50 (Malicious Code 7/10, Architecture 8/10, Implementation 8/10, Operations & IR 9/10, GRC 7/10). Paper reports 'substantial improvements' from the multi-layer agent in higher-order cognitive tasks but provides no post-refinement numeric score.
limitations: Single-domain case study (CompTIA Security+ only) limits generalizability; Dataset focused on cybersecurity; may not capture nuances of other fields; Bloom's Taxonomy may not fully capture contextual nuances of scenario-based questions; Lack of integration with advanced reinforcement learning techniques; Limited real-world datasets constrain depth and higher-order thinking; Actual fine-tuning with domain-specific data is only proposed, not executed; No quantitative post-refinement evaluation of the multi-layer agent reported
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes and implements a multi-layer agent framework (deployed as a web platform integrating the ChatGPT API) that iteratively generates, evaluates, and refines AI-generated cybersecurity questions against Bloom's Taxonomy criteria (topic coverage, cognitive alignment, scenario context, difficulty progression), using CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 as a benchmark and showing gaps in higher-order cognitive levels that the agent pipeline aims to close.
notes: Journal article (J. Umm Al-Qura Univ. Eng.Archit. 2025, 16:1045-1056, doi:10.1007/s43995-025-00136-x). Author: Aziz Alshehri, Umm Al-Qura University. Methodology is primarily a manual/expert-scored comparison (360 human-authored questions across 17 chapters vs ChatGPT-generated counterparts) followed by a proposed multi-agent refinement system. Fine-tuning is mentioned as an aspiration/future work rather than executed; no training hyperparameters, no base model version, no size reported. The pipeline qualifies as prompting + agentic evaluation loop with human (expert) review; RAG-like behavior via grounding on Security+ syllabus topics. Educational level labeled 'professional' (certification exam preparation). RQs covered reflect: cybersecurity-domain question generation (RQ1 context), prompting/agent strategies (RQ2), quiz-MCQ format (RQ3), Bloom-based evaluation (RQ4), some personalization by difficulty/Bloom level (RQ5), explicit limitations (RQ6), and reproducibility info (RQ7). RQ8 (temporal/venue trends) not addressed by a single paper.

title: AI-enhanced structured dataset building for generating meaningful knowledge relationships and diversified questions
venue: Expert Systems with Applications
llm_base: GPT-4 GPT-4 (unknown); Mistral Mistral-7B (7B); LLaMA LLaMA-3-8B (8B)
dataset_name: SKQA generated Q&A dataset (based on Fundamentals of Database Systems textbook by Elmasri 2015; structured KCs derived from DBE-KT22)
dataset_size: 1017 Q&A pairs (190 with varying difficulty levels, 588 aligned with cognitive development levels, 239 gap-filling); 7 structured KC groups; 77 distinct relationships (56 with 3 KCs, 21 with 4 KCs); 202 rows of two-KC relationships from DBE-KT22
dataset_domain: Fundamentals of Database Systems (computer science; also applicable to other subjects e.g. Operating Systems)
personalization_mechanism: Generation of Q&A with varying difficulty levels (Basic, Intermediate, Advanced) and six cognitive development levels aligned with revised Bloom's taxonomy (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create) to match diverse learner needs; structured KC relationships with multiple KCs for deeper understanding
subject_area: Computer Science - Databases (Fundamentals of Database Systems; generalizable to Operating Systems and other subjects)
evaluation_metrics: Faithfulness (0.86); BLEU score (0.95); Bloom-level classification accuracy (97.7%); Human expert 5-point Likert questionnaire (Cronbach's alpha > 0.9, specific alpha 0.933); Linguistic aspect sub-dimensions: Relevancy (mean 4.1), Language fluency (4.5), Language comprehensibility (4.4), Linguistic complexity (4.5), Completeness (4.4); FDS aspect sub-dimensions: Quality of difficulty level questions (4.5), Quality of cognitive development questions (4.6), Usability (4.6), Overall quality (4.7); Pearson correlation between linguistic and FDS aspects (r=0.625 to 0.856, p<0.01); Dataset comparison with 3 open-source Q&A datasets and 4 KT datasets (ASSISTments, KDCup, EdNet, DBE-KT22) across 5 criteria
quantitative_results: SKQA (GPT-4 + RAG + ToT) achieved Faithfulness=0.86, BLEU=0.95, Bloom-level accuracy=97.7%, outperforming GPT-4+RAG (0.74/0.91/93.8%), LLaMA-3-8B+RAG (0.60/0.80/76.7%), and Mistral-7B+RAG (0.53/0.81/83.8%). Generated 1017 Q&A pairs and 7 structured KC groups (77 distinct relationships: 56 three-KC, 21 four-KC) across tree and graph structures, surpassing DBE-KT22 (which only has two-KC relationships). Human evaluation showed high reliability (Cronbach's alpha > 0.9) with all sub-dimension means between 4.1 and 4.7.
limitations: Application demonstrated primarily on Fundamentals of Database Systems course (single exemplar subject), though authors claim broad applicability; Evaluation based on a single expert with more than ten years of experience in teaching databases for the expert judgment component; Limited by text-only input; authors plan future work using multimodal RAG to enhance generalizability; Hardware setup modest (Intel i7-6700, 16 GB RAM, Intel HD Graphics 530 128MB VRAM) which could limit scalability; ToT configuration constrained to small search space (depth=2, breadth=1, size=3 plans); Temperature fixed at 0.5 trades off diversity vs. consistency; Dataset availability is 'on request' for data, though code/data also noted as on GitHub (https://github.com/vietnq262/SmartSKQA)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: SKQA, a hybrid framework combining Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) prompting and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with GPT-4 (via LangChain) to automatically (1) generate structured knowledge components (KCs) with multiple relationships (3 and 4 KCs, in tree and graph structures) and (2) produce diversified Q&A datasets with multiple difficulty levels (QDL) and six cognitive development levels (QCD, aligned with revised Bloom's taxonomy), addressing four key limitations of prior knowledge-tracing datasets (absence of multiple KC relationships, limited QDL, insufficient QCD, and hallucination in LLM generation). Demonstrated on the Fundamentals of Database Systems course with more than 1000 high-quality Q&A pairs and shown generalizable across subjects.
notes: The paper is primarily a prompt-engineering + RAG system (no fine-tuning is performed on any LLM; ft_method='none'). GPT-4 is used via API with ToT+CoT prompting and RAG grounded in the Elmasri 2015 database textbook using OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002 and Chroma vector DB. Authors: Wu-Yuin Hwang, Quoc-Viet Nguyen, Min-Te Sun. Published in Expert Systems With Applications 299 (2026) 129939. DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2025.129939. Code: https://github.com/vietnq262/SmartSKQA. Data availability statement: 'Data will be made available on request.' Baselines (Mistral-7B+RAG, LLaMA-3-8B+RAG, GPT-4+RAG) serve as comparison. Cognitive development levels use revised Bloom's taxonomy (Krathwohl 2002).

title: BegoniaGPT: Cultivating the large language model to be an exceptional K-12 English teacher
venue: Neural Networks
ft_learning_rate: cosine schedule, final LR = 10% of maximal LR
dataset_name: EngCorpus (BasCorpus + ProCorpus + VisCorpus + EmoCorpus)
dataset_size: ~65,000 instructions/conversations (35,000 instructions/conversations + 30,000 emotional conversations); sub-corpora: BasCorpus 10,000, ProCorpus 28,000 (15,000 exercises + 12,000 teacher + 1,000 lesson plans/materials + 1,000 learning progress reports + 1,000 family conversations), VisCorpus 10,000, EmoCorpus 30,000
dataset_language: English (with some Chinese context for Chinese K-12 setting)
dataset_domain: K-12 English education (vocabulary, grammar, reading, writing, spoken language) + psychological counseling
output_format: explanation; exercise; tutoring-dialogue; feedback; lesson-plan; translation; quiz-mcq; quiz-short-answer; other
personalization_mechanism: Role-conditioned prompting for three roles (students, teachers, parents) and three educational stages (primary, middle, high school); BegoniaGPT asks user's educational stage and tailors teaching style, vocabulary and difficulty accordingly; alignment via KTO with expert preferences drives personalization across roles and stages
subject_area: English as a foreign/second language (K-12 English education in China)
evaluation_metrics: Benchmark accuracy on E-EVAL (Primary/Middle/High), EEM, EEH, Eng-Gaokao (zero-shot); Claude-3-opus pairwise ranking (MT-Bench style); Expert 1-5 Likert rubric on Professionalism, Interactivity, Heuristic, Personalization, Overall Satisfaction; Student popularity/favorite model selection; Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's Kappa 0.54-0.76)
quantitative_results: BegoniaGPT achieves SOTA on 4 benchmarks vs 10 SOTA LLMs: E-EVAL Primary 100.00, Middle 99.28, High 99.23; EEM 99.00; EEH 98.00; Eng-Gaokao 113.00; average 102.16 (+2.86 avg gain over second-best). Expert evaluation overall satisfaction: Primary 3.60 (vs GPT-4o 3.15, +15.1%), Middle stages maintained lead; High school 2.89 (vs GPT-4o 2.50, +15.6%). Personalization in high school: BegoniaGPT 2.75 vs GPT-4o 2.25 (+22.2%). Average expert satisfaction advantage ~14.81%. Ablation: w/o SFT lowest benchmark scores; w/o alignment lowest expert satisfaction in high school/other; w/o RAG intermediate. Few-shot: k=3 best on benchmarks (102.66 avg); k=5 best on expert eval (3.33 satisfaction, +5.7% vs k=1)
limitations: Only accepts text input; no voice/speech support, so cannot teach English listening/speaking skills. Based on Qwen2-7B only; larger base models (Qwen2-72B, LLaMA3-65B) were not used. Primarily designed for Chinese-English teaching; not tested on other target languages. Limited pre-training data volume (~300MB, ~50M tokens). All models (including BegoniaGPT) perform poorly on lesson plan generation. Inter-annotator agreement is low in some dimensions (e.g., Personalization Kappa 0.62, Interactivity High 0.62) indicating subjectivity. Claude-3-opus automatic judge ranks BegoniaGPT last while expert humans rank it highest, revealing misalignment between LLM-judge and real pedagogical standards.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes BegoniaGPT, a K-12 English-teaching specialized LLM built on Qwen2-7B via continued pre-training + SFT on a curated 65k-sample EngCorpus (four sub-corpora: BasCorpus, ProCorpus, VisCorpus, EmoCorpus) + alignment with expert feedback using Kahneman-Tversky Optimization (KTO) + RAG; serves three roles (students, teachers, parents) and three stages (primary, middle, high) with personalized, heuristic and compassionate teaching. Achieves SOTA on four Chinese K-12 English benchmarks against 10 LLMs and superior expert/student evaluations on interactivity, heuristic, personalization and satisfaction.
notes: Base LLM Qwen2-7B (chose 7B variant from 0.5B-72B Qwen2 family). Adaptation pipeline = Continued Pre-Training (MLM loss on ~50M-token English corpus) + Supervised Fine-Tuning on EngCorpus (65k) + Alignment via KTO (not DPO/RLHF standard; KTO listed here under adaptation_technique as reinforcement alignment technique; 'RLHF' label in abstract is imprecise, actual method is KTO with 500 expert preference pairs) + RAG with TopK retrieval over an indexed English knowledge base. Hyperparameters beyond those listed (rank/alpha not applicable, full fine-tuning, not LoRA). No quantization reported. Dataset availability: 'Data will be made available on request', not publicly released. Code/model public status not explicitly stated (no repo URL in paper). Evaluation experts from Shanxi and Henan provinces, anonymized as A/B/C. Future plans: BegoniaGPT 2.0 with college-level and multi-discipline coverage, plus multi-modal support.

title: High-Quality Distractors Generation for Human Exam Based on Reinforcement Learning from Preference Feedback
venue: NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING AND CHINESE COMPUTING, PT IV, NLPCC 2024
dataset_name: EQG-RACE (derived from RACE)
dataset_size: 12,743 articles and 20,486 questions
dataset_domain: English reading comprehension exams (online question banks in China)
personalization_mechanism: Not addressed. The system targets high-quality distractors for human exams based on passage/question/answer, not student-level personalization.
subject_area: English reading comprehension (language-learning / L2 English exams)
evaluation_metrics: BLEU-1; BLEU-2; BLEU-3; BLEU-4; ROUGE-L; Reward model score (average, maximum, minimum); ChatGPT/GPT-4 rating: Strongly Misleading / Simple / Illegal (Chain-of-Thought); Diversity: ACD (average cosine distance), Self-BLEU, DISTINCT-1, DISTINCT-2; BERT multiple-choice answering accuracy (lower = harder distractors); Human expert Top-1 / Top-3 ranking and diversity score (0-5)
quantitative_results: DGRL_SFT surpasses prior baselines (HSA, CHN, HMD-Net, EDGE, TMCA, MSG, QDG) on BLEU-1..4 and ROUGE-L for the three distractors on EQG-RACE (e.g., 1st distractor BLEU-1=35.06, BLEU-4=10.51, ROUGE-L=28.38; Average BLEU-1=34.42, BLEU-4=9.90, ROUGE-L=27.56 vs QDG 28.74/4.45/17.14). Reward model scores: average 79.71 (EQG-RACE), 83.29 (DGRL_SFT), 85.28 (DGRL_RL^Q), 83.66 (DGRL_RL^D); min reward 12.00 / 16.03 / 16.45 / 18.36. GPT-4 rating: Strongly Misleading 64.53% (EQG-RACE), 66.31% (SFT), 72.35% (RL^Q), 67.94% (RL^D); Illegal distractors: 0.57% / 2.63% / 1.99% / 2.17%. Diversity (ACD / Self-BLEU / D-1 / D-2): EQG-RACE 0.1207/8.06/73.86/93.39; DGRL_RL^D 0.1075/13.13/67.86/87.49 (best diversity among model variants). BERT answering accuracy drops from 56.40% (EQG-RACE) to 46.80% (DGRL_RL^Q) and 49.79% (DGRL_RL^D), indicating harder, higher-quality distractors. Human evaluation on 10 samples: DGRL_RL^Q got 8 Top-1 and 15 Top-3 placements (vs EQG-RACE 2/6 and DGRL_SFT 0/9); EQG-RACE kept highest diversity score (4.3 vs 3.6/3.5).
limitations: Illegal distractors still exceed the human baseline (1.99-2.63% vs 0.57% in EQG-RACE), indicating residual invalid generations.; Diversity of generated distractors remains below the human-written EQG-RACE references on ACD, D-1 and D-2.; Ranking of ~1000 samples to build the reward dataset relies on GPT-4 sorting verified only on 10 samples, introducing potential annotation noise.; Reported hyperparameters are limited to objective-function weights (alpha/beta/gamma); LoRA rank, learning rate, batch size and epochs are not disclosed.; Evaluated on a single dataset (EQG-RACE), limited to English reading comprehension in a Chinese exam context; generalization to other subjects, languages or exam formats is not tested.; Authors note distractor generation still 'lacks sufficient control' and that higher-quality datasets and more sophisticated prompting are needed for further improvements.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes DGRL, a pipeline that fine-tunes Llama-2 7B with SFT + LoRA on EQG-RACE and then applies RLHF (PPO) using an OPT-1.5B reward model trained from preference rankings (GPT-4-assisted) plus an explicit diversity term (DISTINCT/average cosine distance) in the objective. Two RL variants (DGRL_RL^Q focused on individual distractor quality and DGRL_RL^D focused on diversity) beat prior distractor-generation baselines and human-written distractors on misleadingness and reward-model quality, demonstrating that RL from preference feedback is effective for open-domain educational MCQ distractor generation.
notes: Supervised fine-tuning, reward model training and RL all use LoRA for parameter-efficient tuning. Diversity metric used in the objective is DISTINCT-1. Context targets L2 English exams at secondary/higher-secondary level (mapped to 'secondary'; could also be read as 'language-learning'). No code or model weights release is mentioned in the paper; only the EQG-RACE dataset is publicly available. Adaptation techniques combined: instruction-tuning + SFT + LoRA + RLHF (PPO with reward + diversity + KL terms).

title: Optimizing Diary Studies Learning Outcomes with Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Diaryquest Platform
venue: Proc. Front. Educ. Conf. FIE
dataset_name: DiaryQuest HCI course diary entries
dataset_size: 1,046 curated entries (from 1,372 collected)
dataset_domain: HCI education / student diary studies
personalization_mechanism: Fine-tuned Mistral 7B aligned with assessment criteria of each individual diary study assignment; feedback tailored to individual student submissions via system/user/assistant triad prompting
subject_area: Human-Computer Interaction (Computer Science)
evaluation_metrics: Borda count score; Kendall's Tau; 5-point Likert scale (relevance, clarity, depth, usefulness); Standard deviation
quantitative_results: Blind LLM selection (n=6 models, 50 diary entries, 6 raters): GPT-4o Borda=1308, Mistral 7B=1127, GPT-4o-mini=1101, Llama 3.1 8B=1010, Llama 3.2 11B Vision=896, Gemma 2 9B=840. Rater agreement: Rater 6 Tau=0.867 (p=0.017), Rater 4 Tau=0.828 (p=0.022). Fine-tuned Mistral 7B feedback ratings (n=12 participants: 10 students + 2 instructors): Detailed 500+ words avg=3.92 (SD=0.90), Ordinary 200-300 words avg=4.33 (SD=0.78), Brief 100 words avg=4.00 (SD=0.74). Platform overall: Overall Functionality=4.58 (SD=0.51), UI=4.17 (SD=0.72), Thematic Analysis=4.58 (SD=0.51), Semantic Search=4.33 (SD=0.65), Semantic Graph=4.00 (SD=0.85).
limitations: Small evaluation sample (only 12 participants: 10 students + 2 instructors). Performance less robust on detailed/content-rich entries (500+ words) where feedback focuses on surface-level aspects and lacks deeper analytical insight. Single institution / single HCI course context limits generalizability. No reported LoRA rank/alpha or batch size. Dataset is not public (privacy-sensitive student data). No automatic metrics (BLEU/ROUGE/etc.); evaluation relies on human ratings.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Presents DiaryQuest, a platform that integrates a Mistral 7B model fine-tuned with LoRA on 1,046 curated HCI-course diary entries to deliver personalized feedback, summaries, thematic analysis (LDA + LLM refinement), FAISS-based semantic search and semantic graph visualization, demonstrating that a small open-source LLM (selected over Llama/Gemma via blind Borda ranking against GPT-4o/4o-mini) can scale high-quality reflective feedback in large HCI classes and reduce instructor workload.
notes: Base model chosen via blind pairwise-style ranking inspired by Chatbot Arena (Borda count + Kendall's Tau). Mistral 7B ranked best among open-source models (2nd overall after GPT-4o). The fine-tuning is framed as knowledge distillation: LLMs >70B acted as teacher to generate System/User/Assistant triads; Mistral 7B is the student. LoRA specifically modifies attention matrices Wq and Wv. Data cleaning used SpaCy EntityRecognizer to remove PII. Platform features beyond fine-tuning: LDA topic modeling, FAISS vector index, k-means clustering, Doc2Vec + cosine similarity for graph edges, G2 framework for graph rendering. LoRA rank (r), alpha and batch size are not reported in the paper. Published at 2025 IEEE Frontiers in Education Conference (FIE), DOI 10.1109/FIE63693.2025.11328660.

title: Tailored Tales: Enhancing Children's Reading Comprehension with Preference-Tuned Automatic Story Generation
venue: Proc. - Int. Conf. Comput. Artif. Intell., ICCAI
dataset_name: HANNA (Human-Annotated Narratives for Automatic Story Generation) + ROCStories
dataset_size: HANNA: 1,056 stories from 96 prompts (DPO). ROCStories: 98,162 training stories, filtered to 3,761 good + 3,761 bad examples (KTO)
dataset_domain: Children's short stories / reading comprehension (EGRA)
personalization_mechanism: Structured templating with probabilistic sampling of character gender (boy/girl/both at 1/3 each), culturally diverse names from NYC baby-names dataset, and GPT-4-filtered child-appropriate themes from ROCStories; ~60-word stories tailored to early-grade readers per EGRA criteria
subject_area: Reading comprehension / literacy (Early Grade Reading Assessment)
evaluation_metrics: Manual EGRA criteria adherence (16 criteria, 1/0 per criterion); Readability: Flesch-Kincaid, Flesch Ease, Coleman Liau, Dale Chall, ARI, SPACHE, Linsear Write; CLIP embedding spread (latent-space diversity); K-means/KNN clustering + Silhouette score; BERT score (semantic similarity); N-gram diversity (Distinct-1, Distinct-2); Similarity heatmaps
quantitative_results: Manual EGRA evaluation average scores: Zero-Shot 87%, FairytaleQA Inspiration 87%, DPO (HANNA, Mistral-7B) 83%, KTO (ROCStories, Mistral-7B) 80%. After correcting present-tense issue, adjusted averages: DPO 89%, KTO 87%. Readability grade levels more uniform for DPO/KTO (grades 6-8) vs baselines. Silhouette scores: HANNA DPO 0.5331 (best), Zero-shot 0.5070, FairytaleQA 0.4835, ROCStories KTO 0.4852. BERT mean scores: Zero-shot 0.8772 (highest similarity/lowest diversity), FairytaleQA 0.8524, HANNA DPO 0.8601, ROCStories KTO 0.8308 (greatest diversity). N-gram Distinct-2 medians: Zero-shot ~0.50, ROCStories KTO ~0.48, FairytaleQA ~0.46, HANNA DPO ~0.43 (most uniform/least diverse). DPO stories significantly longer than KTO stories.
limitations: DPO-generated stories completely failed to adhere to present tense (0% on that criterion) before automated rectification; DPO showed lower scores on using child-appropriate names (62%) and first-sentence simplicity (54%); KTO showed weaker narrative structure and logical soundness; Overall range of diversity across models was modest, indicating significant semantic overlap; HANNA DPO produced the least N-gram diversity (most uniform outputs); Only preliminary results with 100 stories generated per method for evaluation; No training hyperparameters or computational cost details reported; Human evaluation by a single human expert (limited inter-rater reliability)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Demonstrates that preference tuning (DPO on HANNA and KTO on ROCStories) applied to OpenHermes-2.5-Mistral-7B is a cost-effective alternative to full fine-tuning for generating EGRA-compliant children's stories, yielding more uniform readability (grades 6-8) and improved EGRA criteria adherence (adjusted 89% DPO, 87% KTO) versus GPT-4 zero-shot and inspiration baselines, while maintaining narrative coherence.
notes: Base models (OpenHermes-2.5-Mistral-7B, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2) and datasets (HANNA, ROCStories) are publicly available on HuggingFace; code repository availability not explicitly stated. Study also uses GPT-4 for zero-shot baseline and theme filtering, and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 as an LLM-judge to label stories for KTO. 'Inspiration' baseline references prior FairytaleQA/Brothers Grimm / Arabian Nights approach (Ref [19], same authors' prior work). Published at 2025 11th ICCAI. RQ coverage interpretation: RQ1 (base LLMs), RQ2 (adaptation techniques DPO/KTO), RQ3 (dataset domains), RQ4 (output format - stories), RQ5 (personalization via templating), RQ6 (evaluation methods/metrics). Does not cover training cost/compute (RQ7) nor open reproducibility artifacts in full (RQ8).

title: Book2QA: A Framework for Integrating LLMs to Generate High-quality QA Data from Textbooks
venue: Proc Int Jt Conf Neural Networks
llm_base: Baichuan-Chat Baichuan-7B-Chat (7B); Baichuan-Chat Baichuan-13B-Chat (13B); InternLM-Chat InternLM-7B-Chat (7B); Qwen-Chat Qwen-7B-Chat (7B); GPT-4 GPT-4 (unknown); ChatGPT ChatGPT (unknown); Qwen-Max Qwen-Max (long-context) (110B+); GPT-4o mini GPT-4o mini (unknown); Claude Claude-3-Sonnet (unknown)
dataset_name: Book2QA_DI (+ Book2QA_ChineseSquad, Book2QA_CMRC2018); source textbooks: Data and Programming (80 paragraphs) and Information Systems & Digital Society (118 paragraphs)
dataset_size: Book2QA_DI: 5940 QA examples (30 clusters); Book2QA_ChineseSquad: 1440 examples (18 clusters); Book2QA_CMRC2018: 1440 examples (18 clusters)
dataset_domain: Computer Science (Data/Programming, Information Systems) + out-of-domain history/geography/humanities/politics/biology
personalization_mechanism: Multi-source hierarchical prompting grounded in Bloom's taxonomy (6 cognitive levels: remembering, understanding, applying, analyzing, evaluating, creating) to enhance depth and breadth of generated QA; IFD/r-IFD filtering aligns data with student model capabilities
subject_area: Computer Science (Data and Programming, Information Systems); extensions to history, geography, humanities, politics, biology
evaluation_metrics: BERTScore (BF1); K-Precision; ROUGE-L; Relevance (5-point Likert); Accuracy (5-point Likert); Helpfulness (5-point Likert); Krippendorff's alpha (inter-rater agreement); Win-tie-lose comparison
quantitative_results: Single-paragraph DI80: Baichuan SFT (Book2QA)+RAG reached BF1=0.77, K-Precision=0.22, ROUGE-L=0.32, outperforming GPT-4 (BF1=0.75). Multi-paragraph DI80: Baichuan SFT (Book2QA) reached BF1=0.73, K-Precision=0.11, ROUGE-L=0.21, surpassing RAG and long-context SOTA in BF1/ROUGE-L. Win-tie-lose on DI240: Baichuan (Book2QA_DI) vs Baichuan (ChatGPT_DI) = 172 wins / 29 ties / 39 losses; vs Baichuan (Qwen-Max_DI) = 94 / 50 / 96 (competitive). Human + GPT-4 rankings: Book2QA_DI ranked 1.00 (best) in relevance, accuracy and helpfulness vs textbook and other baselines (Krippendorff alpha 0.70-1.00). Out-of-domain (Table III): Book2QA in-domain achieves BF1=0.79/0.83 and ROUGE-L=0.54/0.57 on ChineseSquad_eval and CMRC2018_eval, outperforming manual data. Ablation: 'Whole' source (origin+keywords+summary) ranked 1.00 in all metrics; Book2QA_sft (multi-model answers) ranked 1.00 vs single-model answer datasets.
limitations: QA assessment remains subjective and challenging due to limited domain-specific datasets. Cannot address all dimensions of educational evaluation. Synthetic data tends to produce longer responses that may increase students' cognitive load. LLMs may occasionally produce hallucinations (partially mitigated by fact content-based generation). Need to balance information quantity and future work must adjust metrics to optimize educational robot performance.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Book2QA: a cost-effective framework that integrates multiple medium-sized LLMs (Baichuan/InternLM/Qwen 7B-13B) with a Bloom's-taxonomy-based hierarchical prompting strategy and IFD/r-IFD filtering to synthesize high-quality QA data from textbooks; fine-tuning Baichuan-7B with LoRA on this synthetic data rivals or surpasses SOTA LLMs (GPT-4, Qwen-Max) at much lower cost, and generalizes beyond textbooks to other domains.
notes: Target fine-tuned model is Baichuan-7B-Chat; generator ensemble uses Baichuan-13B-Chat, InternLM-7B-Chat, Qwen-7B-Chat. Evaluators: GPT-4 and Claude-3-Sonnet as LLM-judges; 3 human Chinese-native CS-degree evaluators on 60 real-world questions. Preprocessing via in-house PDF-to-JSON tool plus summarization, keyword extraction (ExtractKeywords), k-means clustering of summaries, and IFD-based question selection / r-IFD-based answer selection. GitHub: https://github.com/Curtain2020/Book2QA. Personalization is not per-student profile but per-student-model (IFD/r-IFD align training data with the student LLM's capabilities) and per-cognitive-level (Bloom).

title: Designing and Evaluating a 5E-Structured GenAI Coach for Guided Inquiry: A Pedagogy-to-Prompt Engineering Framework
venue: EDUCATION SCIENCES
llm_base: GPT-4 GPT-4 (custom GPT on OpenAI platform) (unknown)
dataset_name: Curated pedagogical knowledge base: Content Knowledge (CK) on chemical equation balancing + Common Error Analysis Table (7 fields: Item Type, Question/Target Equation, Correct Answer, Common Student Error, Reason for the Error, Underlying Misconception, Corrective Guidance)
dataset_size: unknown (spreadsheet file uploaded to custom GPT; exact item count not reported)
dataset_language: Traditional Chinese (student-facing dialogue); prompts partly in Chinese
dataset_domain: Middle school chemistry - balancing chemical equations via the inspection method
personalization_mechanism: 1:1 self-paced adaptive dialogue via custom GPT: Socratic questioning, multi-step hint-prompt-assert feedback cycle (AutoTutor-inspired), error-specific corrective guidance retrieved from the Common Error Analysis Table, and progression through the 5E Learning Cycle (Engage, Explore, Explain, Elaborate, Evaluate). Adaptation is in-context / prompt-driven, not via user-profile fine-tuning.
subject_area: Chemistry (middle school; balancing chemical equations, 8th grade)
evaluation_metrics: pre-test/post-test scores (0-100, 10 items: 4 conceptual True/False + 6 procedural fill-in-the-blank); Cronbach's alpha (pre-test = 0.716; post-test = 0.739); paired samples t-test (within-group gains); independent samples t-test (between-group post-test); Cohen's d (effect size); ANCOVA with pre-test as covariate; Johnson-Neyman technique (region of significance along prior-knowledge continuum); Lag Sequential Analysis (LSA) with adjusted residuals / z-scores on 5E-coded dialogue turns; inter-rater reliability (Cohen's Kappa = 0.922; percent agreement = 95.28%)
quantitative_results: Quasi-experimental study, N=60 eighth-grade students (30 experimental / 30 control), 45-min single session. Experimental group pre-test M=48.00 (SD=32.63), post-test M=72.93 (SD=27.04); within-group t(29)=5.311, p<0.001, Cohen's d=0.832. Control group pre-test M=35.60 (SD=22.28), post-test M=51.93 (SD=34.04); within-group t(29)=3.456, p=0.002, Cohen's d=0.568. Between-group post-test independent t-test: t(58)=2.646, p=0.011, Cohen's d=0.68. ANCOVA (controlling for pre-test): F(1,57)=3.79, p=0.26 (reported as marginal), eta^2_p=0.062; adjusted means Exp=68.72 vs Ctrl=56.14. Johnson-Neyman: significant GenAI advantage for students with pre-test < 39.39 (43% of experimental group, n=13), narrowing the achievement gap. LSA on dialogue logs confirmed strong adherence to the intended 5E sequence: Engage-Explore z=11.157, Explore-Explain z=4.387, Explain-Elaborate z=2.462, Elaborate-Evaluate z=2.578 (all z>1.96, p<0.05); no significant backward loops or stage skipping. Coding reliability: Cohen's Kappa=0.922.
limitations: Short intervention: single 45-min session limits conclusions about long-term sustainability and depth of conceptual understanding. Potential novelty effect cannot be ruled out given the brief timeframe. Small sample (N=60) from two intact classes at one public middle school in central Taiwan; limited generalizability. Single narrow domain (balancing chemical equations, middle school chemistry). Quasi-experimental design with purposive sampling of intact classes (not randomized). Inherent possibility of GenAI inaccuracies / hallucinations in generated responses. Broader ethical and equity considerations of deploying such tools at scale. Quantitative-only outcomes; lacks qualitative exploration of user experience. ANCOVA main effect was only marginally significant (p=0.26 as reported in text), with homogeneity of regression slopes only borderline met (p=0.061). No investigation of optimal strategies for teacher integration or professional development.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Introduces the Pedagogy-to-Prompt Engineering Framework, a replicable low-code methodology that systematically translates established pedagogical models into structured GenAI interactions by integrating the 5E Learning Cycle (instructional architecture), the 5S Prompting Principles (dialogue design), and AutoTutor-inspired feedback logic. Operationalized as a custom GPT on GPT-4 for middle-school chemistry (balancing chemical equations) and empirically validated (N=60) showing significant learning gains over traditional instruction (Cohen's d=0.68), particular benefit for low prior-knowledge students (Johnson-Neyman cut at 39.39), and strong adherence of student-AI dialogues to the intended 5E pedagogical sequence (LSA). Framework has since been deployed at national scale in Taiwan's TALPer / TALP platform (Grades 1-12) with GPT-4o and Gemini 2.5 Flash.
notes: No fine-tuning of any kind is performed; the adaptation is purely prompt-engineering + RAG-like knowledge-base grounding via the custom GPT Knowledge feature (Code Interpreter enabled to read the uploaded spreadsheet). Classified as prompting in the controlled vocabulary; the uploaded 'Common Error Analysis Table' could also be described as a lightweight retrieval-grounding mechanism (RAG-adjacent) but no vector store or explicit retrieval pipeline is described. Output formats include tutoring dialogue, Socratic hints/feedback, scaffolded explanations (including analogies such as 'building blocks' for conservation of matter), and problem presentation (chemical equations without coefficients). Educational level: K-12 (8th grade, ~13-14 years, lower-secondary / middle school, Taiwan). Interface and dialogue primarily in Traditional Chinese. Privacy safeguards: opt-out of model training, platform safety filter, prompt guardrails, physical teacher supervision. Funding: National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan (Grant 112-2410-H-142-016-MY3). IRB: CRREC-112-088 (18 September 2023). Data available on request from authors (not public due to privacy). RQ coverage rationale within this mapping's RQ set: RQ1 (base model = GPT-4), RQ2 (adaptation technique = prompting, no FT), RQ3 (output format = tutoring dialogue / feedback / explanation / problem), RQ5 (personalization via adaptive 1:1 Socratic dialogue and error-specific feedback), RQ6 (educational level = K-12 / middle-school chemistry), RQ7 (evaluation methodology = quasi-experimental with pre/post-test, ANCOVA, Johnson-Neyman, LSA, human-student user study). RQ4 (fine-tuning methods/hyperparameters) and RQ8 (reproducibility / open artifacts) are not substantively addressed: no fine-tuning is performed and no code, data, or model artifacts are released.

title: From Uniform to Unique: Adaptive K-12 Assessment Using Large Language Models
venue: EXPERIMENTAL IR MEETS MULTILINGUALITY, MULTIMODALITY, AND INTERACTION, CLEF 2025
dataset_name: NCERT-based MCQ dataset (generated with Gemini 1.0 Pro from NCERT textbook passages, human-validated)
dataset_size: unknown
dataset_domain: K-12 education (NCERT textbooks; subjects include History, Geography, Economics, among others)
personalization_mechanism: Adaptive diagnostic test with three Bloom-aligned difficulty levels (Easy=Remember/Understand, Medium=Apply/Analyze, Hard=Evaluate/Create). Probabilistic learner modeling via Bayesian inference with prior P(Z=1)=pi and posterior update from response likelihoods; guessing suppression (pg=0.25) for 4-option MCQs; advancement gated by threshold theta_ell (e.g., 0.8). Two-tier question delivery per level (3 primary + up to 2 secondary fallback), with a fixed-attempt policy (AP=3) for fair score normalization. Difficulty-aware weighted confidence score aggregates performance across levels and generates a personalized learning path/remediation plan.
subject_area: Multi-subject K-12 (History, Geography, Economics, and others from NCERT textbooks)
evaluation_metrics: probability of passing a level by random guessing (binomial, n=5, p=0.25); cumulative probability of clearing all three levels by guessing; difficulty-weighted confidence score (0-1, expressed as %); deviation between student self-reported prior and PLA score
quantitative_results: Robustness against guessing: P(Pass_guess) approx 0.1035 per level; cumulative P(clearing all 3 levels by guessing) approx 0.0011 (~0.11%). Empirical deployment with ~30 students (Grades 8-12, with some Grades 4, 6, 9 also reported) across self-selected topics; average deviation (Prior - PLA Score) across all topics = -9.84% (text also reports -5.69% in the discussion), indicating a slight tendency toward student underestimation of own knowledge. Qualitative comparison with TALP (Taiwan Adaptive Learning Platform) argues PLA reduces false-positive advancement from guessing. No standard accuracy/F1/BLEU-style metrics on MCQ generation are reported.
limitations: MCQ generation fine-tuning details (hyperparameters, training size, splits) not reported; Empirical evaluation limited to ~30 students (small sample) with self-reported prior knowledge as the only baseline; Only multiple-choice (4-option) format evaluated; long-form and subjective responses left as future work; Alpha (probability of answering correctly given understanding) assumed in [0.85, 0.95] rather than empirically calibrated; No formal benchmarking against standardized assessments; real-classroom longitudinal evaluation deferred to future work; Comparison with TALP is descriptive/theoretical, not a head-to-head empirical experiment; No RAG or multimodal affective feedback integrated yet (noted as future work); Inconsistency in reported average deviation (-9.84% in Table 2 vs -5.69% in discussion)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Personalised Learning Assistant (PLA): a deployed K-12 adaptive assessment framework that combines a fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1 8B Instruct (plus T5 and Gemini 1.0 Pro in the pipeline) for Bloom-aligned MCQ generation with a multi-level diagnostic test driven by Bayesian inference, difficulty-aware weighted scoring, guessing suppression, and a fixed-attempt fairness policy, deployed as a full web prototype (Vercel frontend + Kaggle-hosted LLM backend via FastAPI).
notes: Pipeline uses three LLMs: Gemini 1.0 Pro (zero-shot MCQ generation from NCERT passages), then fine-tuning of Google T5 and Meta LLaMA 3.1 8B Instruct on the human-validated MCQs; authors select LLaMA 3.1 as the final production model. Fine-tuning method not explicitly named (full FT vs LoRA/QLoRA/PEFT); classified as SFT because training is supervised on curated MCQs, but specifics (rank, alpha, quantization, epochs, batch size, learning rate) are not disclosed. Bloom levels grouped as Easy=Remember/Understand, Medium=Apply/Analyze, Hard=Evaluate/Create. Published in CLEF 2025 proceedings (LNCS 16089, pp. 3-16, 2026). Dataset stated available on Hugging Face and code on GitHub (links in footnote of page 4); model weights availability not explicitly stated. Evaluation is primarily a probabilistic/mathematical robustness argument plus a small user study with ~30 K-12 students; no automatic generation-quality metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, perplexity, accuracy vs gold MCQs) reported. RQ7/RQ8 (e.g., robustness/bias beyond guessing and scalability benchmarks) not substantively addressed.

title: AI-Based Examination Content Creation: Evaluating Large Language Models for Question Generation
venue: Int. Conf. Intell. Methods, Syst. Appl., IMSA
dataset_name: SciQ, RACE, CosmosQA
dataset_size: SciQ: ~13,000 MCQs; RACE: 97,000+ questions from 28,000 passages; CosmosQA: 35,600 questions
dataset_domain: Scientific reasoning (SciQ), Reading comprehension (RACE), Commonsense reasoning (CosmosQA)
personalization_mechanism: No personalization mechanism; the study focuses on question generation quality across different dataset types, not adaptation to student profiles
subject_area: Science (SciQ), English reading comprehension (RACE middle/high school), Commonsense narratives (CosmosQA)
evaluation_metrics: F1-score; ROUGE-1; ROUGE-2; ROUGE-L; BERTScore; Precision; Recall; Token Efficiency
quantitative_results: SciQ F1: Mistral 7B=0.891 (best), Qwen 2.5=0.884, LLaMA 3.2=0.828. RACE F1: Qwen 2.5=0.8252 (best), LLaMA 3.2=0.8275, Mistral 7B=0.8245. CosmosQA F1: Mistral 7B=0.728, Qwen 2.5=0.702, LLaMA 3.2=0.646. ROUGE-L best: Mistral 7B on SciQ (0.3699), Qwen 2.5 on CosmosQA (0.3209). BERTScore: Qwen 2.5-Max=0.86 (best semantic alignment). Highest precision: Mistral 7B (0.8462). Highest recall: Qwen 2.5=0.9405 on SciQ. Token efficiency: Mistral 7B=0.74.
limitations: LLaMA 3.2 struggles with implicit reasoning and commonsense tasks (lowest F1 on CosmosQA: 0.646); Mistral 7B requires greater computational resources despite high accuracy; LLaMA 3.2 exhibits inefficiencies in handling longer contexts and shows redundancy affecting token efficiency; Potential bias in AI-generated questions from training data; Difficulty controlling question difficulty to match intended learner level; Hallucination issues leading to factually incorrect questions; Low ROUGE-2 scores overall indicate limited bigram overlap with reference questions; No human expert evaluation of pedagogical quality was performed
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5
key_contribution: Comparative benchmarking of three state-of-the-art LLMs (LLaMA 3.2, Mistral 7B, Qwen 2.5-Max) fine-tuned with LoRA and 4-bit quantization via Unsloth for automated MCQ generation across three educational datasets (SciQ, RACE, CosmosQA), establishing model-task suitability: Mistral 7B for structured scientific questions, Qwen 2.5-Max for multi-step and commonsense reasoning, LLaMA 3.2 for efficient structured MCQs.
notes: Authors from Nile University, Egypt. Published in 2025 Intelligent Methods, Systems, and Applications (IMSA) IEEE conference. Models uploaded to Hugging Face for reproducibility (model_public=yes), but fine-tuning code repository not explicitly mentioned (code_public=unknown). Study uses benchmark QA datasets repurposed for question generation rather than a custom educational corpus. LoRA rank/alpha values not specified in the paper. Future work suggests RLHF and domain-specific fine-tuning. Hybrid deployment recommended for real-world educational applications.

title: Fine-Tuning Language Models for Pedagogy-Aligned Lesson Plans in Cybersecurity Education
venue: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
dataset_name: KAU Cybersecurity Diploma lesson-plan dataset (custom)
dataset_size: 119 structured entries across 14 courses; expanded to 325 (TF-IDF) / 265 (SBERT) after oversampling + paraphrasing; 70/15/15 train/val/test (test n=40)
dataset_domain: cybersecurity education (Diploma in Cybersecurity, King Abdulaziz University)
personalization_mechanism: no learner-level personalization; outputs are structured lesson plans (learning objectives, theoretical context, teaching strategy) conditioned on domain/course title/topic
subject_area: cybersecurity
evaluation_metrics: BLEU; ROUGE-1; ROUGE-2; ROUGE-L; ROUGE-Lsum; BERTScore-P; BERTScore-R; BERTScore-F1; Silhouette Score; Elbow/SSE; expert rating (clarity, measurability, alignment, Bloom action verbs, theory/strategy appropriateness, coherence)
quantitative_results: Held-out test (15%, n=40): BLEU 73.54, ROUGE-1 82.19, ROUGE-2 68.91, ROUGE-L 78.16, ROUGE-Lsum 81.28, BERTScore P 97.40 / R 97.46 / F1 97.43. Expert (educational): learning objectives clear 85.2%, measurable 92.6%, aligned with topic 100%, Bloom verbs 88.9%; teaching strategy 92.6%; coherence 77.8-88.9%. Cybersecurity instructors: teaching strategies 84.4%, logical link 82.2%. SBERT clustering preferred over TF-IDF (Silhouette k=5 rose to 0.1150 after balancing). Comparative table: outperforms GPT-3 Russian summarization (BLEU 23.10) and is competitive with LLaMA-3 cybersecurity Q&A (BLEU 96.70 on a more constrained task).
limitations: Dataset derived from a single academic program (KAU Diploma in Cybersecurity), which limits curricular diversity and generalizability. Evaluation relies primarily on automated similarity metrics (BLEU/ROUGE/BERTScore) that cannot fully capture instructional effectiveness. No classroom-based validation of educational impact on learners. Augmented dataset entries (beyond the pilot course) were generated automatically and not subjected to further expert evaluation. Automatic metrics implicitly assume textual similarity is a sufficient proxy for pedagogical quality.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes a four-phase framework that embeds pedagogical structure (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy objectives, learning theory, teaching strategy) into the fine-tuning data of GPT-3.5 Turbo via a Custom-GPT-generated, SBERT+KMeans-clustered and class-balanced cybersecurity lesson-plan corpus, yielding pedagogy-aligned, structurally consistent lesson plans with high lexical and semantic fidelity (BLEU 73.5, BERTScore F1 97.4) in a specialized domain.
notes: Base model size not disclosed by OpenAI (gpt-3.5-turbo-1106). Adaptation is SFT via OpenAI's hosted fine-tuning API (no LoRA/QLoRA). Knowledge base constructed with OpenAI Custom GPT acting as a pedagogy-aware data generator (prompt engineering + metadata), then clustered with TF-IDF and SBERT (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) + KMeans (k=5) and class-balanced via oversampling and paraphrasing augmentation. Compared against GPT-3 Russian summarization [24] and LLaMA-3 cybersecurity Q&A [49]. Funded by KAU WAQF Endowment. Published in IJACSA Vol. 16 No. 12, 2025.

title: Multimodal Quiz Generation via RAG with LLM-as-Judge Evaluation
venue: Proc. Front. Educ. Conf. FIE
llm_base: LLaMA 3.1 3.1 (8B); LLaVA; BridgeTower; Whisper; Claude v2.1 / Sonnet 3.5; GPT-4
dataset_name: MIT 6.100L: Introduction to Computer Science and Programming Using Python (Fall 2022) - first lecture video
dataset_size: 1 lecture video, 3 topic segments, 15 MCQs generated
dataset_domain: Introductory computer science / programming (Python)
personalization_mechanism: No student-level personalization; content is derived from a retrieved top-K query/topic keyword over multimodal lecture content.
subject_area: Computer Science / Introductory Programming (Python)
evaluation_metrics: Hit Rate; Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR); Correctness; Relevance; Faithfulness; Clarity (Likert 1-5); Percentage Agreement; Cohen's Kappa; Spearman's Rho; Krippendorff's Alpha (Ordinal); Krippendorff's Alpha (Nominal)
quantitative_results: Retrieval: text Hit Rate=0.91, MRR=0.82; image Hit Rate=0.83, MRR=0.71. Generation: mean Correctness=4.028/5, Relevance=0.821/1, Faithfulness=0.898/1. Human vs LLM inter-rater agreement: Percentage Agreement 76-79% (Claude v2.1 and LLaMA 3.3 70b tied highest at 79%); Cohen's Kappa 0.34-0.44 (Sonnet 3.5=0.44, Claude v2.1=0.41); Spearman's Rho 0.50-0.62 (Claude v2.1=0.62); Krippendorff's Alpha Ordinal 0.49-0.61 (LLaMA 3.3 70b=0.61); Krippendorff's Alpha Nominal 0.35-0.42 (Claude v2.1=0.42). Inter-model agreement stronger than human-model (up to Cohen's Kappa=0.70, Krippendorff's Alpha Ordinal=0.87 between Claude v2.1 and Titan Express).
limitations: Evaluated on a single lecture video with only 15 generated MCQs (very small evaluation set). Lower image retrieval MRR (0.71) suggests room for improved visual alignment and caption fidelity. LLM judges tend to overestimate quiz quality compared to humans, missing vague phrasing, incomplete options, or partial misalignment. LLMs struggle with pedagogical nuance, favoring surface-level coherence over deeper conceptual accuracy. Higher inter-model agreement than human-model agreement suggests LLM convergence that may not reflect true pedagogical quality. Human raters show stronger consistency on questions requiring deeper reasoning or visual interpretation.
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Proposes a multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework that integrates audio (Whisper), visual (LLaVA), and textual lecture content (via BridgeTower embeddings and LanceDB) to generate pedagogically grounded MCQs using LLaMA 3.1 (8B), and introduces a two-stage evaluation combining retrieval/generation metrics with a multi-model LLM-as-Judge agreement study against four human raters.
notes: No fine-tuning is performed; the adaptation is purely RAG + prompt engineering (LangChain-built prompts) over a multimodal vector store. Base models used: LLaMA 3.1 8B (generation, via OLLAMA), LLaVA (vision-language reasoning, image descriptions), BridgeTower (multimodal embeddings), Whisper (ASR). LLM judges used: Claude v2.1, Claude Sonnet 3.5, GPT-4 (mentioned in text), LLaMA 3.1/3.3 70b, Nova Pro, Titan Express (Table I reports Llama 3.3 70b, Nova Pro, Claude 2.1, Sonnet 3.5, Titan Express rather than GPT-4 explicitly). Dataset is publicly available MIT OCW course. Code availability not stated in the paper.

title: Toward Generating Quality Test Questions and Answers Using Quantized Low-Rank Adapters in LLMs
venue: IEEE Access
ft_alpha: 2-128 (best QG alpha=8 with rank=32; best AG alpha=16 with rank=64)
ft_quantization: 4-bit NF4 (QLoRA) with Double Quantization; also tested non-quantized LoRA; optimal non-quantized for both QG and AG
dataset_name: KCSAT-ENG (KCSAT-ENG-Question Generation and KCSAT-ENG-Answer Generation)
dataset_size: 3,299 questions from 157 exam papers (2014-2024); 22 task types; test sets: 14 texts for QG and 21 questions for AG
dataset_language: English (questions/texts) with Korean translations and commentary (answers)
dataset_domain: Korea College Scholastic Ability Test (KCSAT) English section; standardized Korean college entrance exam
personalization_mechanism: Controllable question difficulty through prompt engineering; 22 distinct task types (fill-in-the-blank, order of sentences, main idea, etc.) enabling task-specific question generation
subject_area: English language learning / KCSAT English exam preparation (college entrance exam for high school seniors in South Korea)
evaluation_metrics: format verification pass rate (binary per criteria checklist); BERTScore F1 (F_BERT); accuracy rate (C_rate) vs ground truth
quantitative_results: Question generation: best format verification 81.8% with rank=32, alpha=8, non-quantized (41.5% improvement over LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct baseline 57.8%; 2.8% improvement over GPT-4o 79.6%). Answer generation: best format verification 85.7% with rank=64, alpha=16, non-quantized (17.4% improvement over baseline 73.0%; 11.5% below GPT-4o 96.8%). Best BERTScore F1 = 0.8112 at rank=32, alpha=8 (vs 0.7628 baseline, 0.8349 GPT-4o). Accuracy 73.0% at rank=16 or 32 with alpha configurations (16.1% / 58.7% improvement over baseline 46.0%; 17.9% below GPT-4o 88.9%). Non-quantized QG avg 78.0% vs quantized 68.1% (14.5% better). Non-quantized AG avg 80.2% vs quantized 69.2% (15.9% better). Average regenerations: 1.08 (QG), 1.45 (AG).
limitations: Dataset (answers) generated by GPT-4o may contain inherent biases; Answers to generated questions are not always guaranteed to be correct; rigorous validation needed; Recommended only for supplementary practice problems, not direct use in official test construction; Performance metrics do not exhibit linear relationship with changes in rank and alpha (no clear pattern); Study focuses only on KCSAT-ENG; generalizability to other standardized exams requires adaptation; Domain shift risk over time requires retrieval-augmented strategies in future work; No comparison with DeepSeek R1 Distill or other reasoning models (left as future work); High computational cost even with QLoRA (max 40GB VRAM on RTX 6000 Ada)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Proposes QLoRA-based fine-tuning of LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct for generating high-quality Korean CSAT English-section test questions (22 task types) and answers, deploying separate specialized models for question generation and answer generation with a cross-verification process. Introduces the KCSAT-ENG dataset and a professional-checklist evaluation aligned with educational goals beyond conventional NLP metrics; systematically tunes rank, alpha, and quantization across 19 configurations, achieving comparable or superior format verification to GPT-4o with ~20x fewer parameters.
notes: Deployed publicly accessible web-based service at http://csatmaker.site. Uses FlashAttention-2 for computational efficiency. Model architecture: LoRA applied only to Query (Wq) and Value (Wv) projection matrices in attention; 32 transformer layers. Trainable parameters: 0.01%-0.34% depending on rank. Hardware: AMD Ryzen Threadripper PRO 3955WX, 251GB RAM, 1x RTX 6000 Ada 48GB + 2x RTX 3090 24GB. Data collection effort: ~5 man-months. Authors from Hongik University (Sejong, South Korea). Published in IEEE Access, May 2025. 'Definition + single positive example' instruction design chosen over two-example design based on prior research [37]. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting used for question generation but NOT for answer generation (CoT was found to negatively affect answer accuracy). Post-validation regeneration step used to enhance output quality (max 7 regenerations). Ground truth for answer dataset generated using GPT-4o API.

title: Designing an LLM-Based IELTS Question Generator, Assessment, and Personalized Training System: Architecture and Research Agenda
venue: Int. Conf. Electr. Eng./Electron., Comput., Telecommun. Inf. Technol., ECTI-CON
dataset_name: 20 authentic IELTS past papers (Cambridge University Press, 2020-2024)
dataset_size: 20 past papers; per-module conversational fine-tuning datasets built via OCR + formatting script
dataset_domain: IELTS English language proficiency test preparation (Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking)
output_format: quiz-mcq; quiz-short-answer; exercise; feedback; explanation; other
personalization_mechanism: Placement tests to identify weaknesses, goal setting, progress tracking via personalized dashboards, adaptive mock tests targeting specific skill weaknesses, and skill-specific LLM pipelines delivering detailed per-skill feedback
subject_area: English as a Foreign Language / IELTS test preparation
evaluation_metrics: METEOR (via NLTK); IELTS similarity (90% reported for writing module); quantitative rubric aligned with official IELTS specifications; BERTScore (planned); BLEURT (planned); Azure Pronunciation Assessment (prosodic/phoneme-level scoring); IELTS 9-band proficiency scale; user acceptance testing (UAT)
quantitative_results: 20 generated listening test samples all met evaluation criteria against official IELTS test specifications using a quantitative rubric.
