Writing module achieved approximately 90% IELTS similarity after prompt-chaining and fine-tuning. METEOR-based preliminary evaluations confirmed close alignment with official IELTS standards. Audio synthesis cost: under 0.64 USD per audio file (average script length 3969 characters at 0.00016 USD/character via Google Cloud TTS).
limitations: Paper is primarily an architecture and research-agenda proposal with limited empirical evaluation at time of publication; GPT-4o struggles to generate maps and diagrams for Writing Task 1 due to spatial requirements, requiring external tools like Matplotlib; Preliminary tests revealed topic drift in the writing module, requiring continuous expert oversight; Fine-tuning dataset limited to 20 authentic IELTS papers; scanned format required OCR preprocessing; No formal comparison against strong non-fine-tuned baselines reported; Full quantitative evaluation by experts and test-takers still pending (planned as part of research agenda); Proprietary closed model (GPT-4o) limits reproducibility and raises cost/privacy concerns; Evaluation biases across diverse accents and demographics acknowledged as unresolved
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_contribution: NOAH: a full-stack, microservice-based LLM-powered IELTS preparation platform that fine-tunes GPT-4o on authentic Cambridge IELTS past papers to generate exam-aligned content for all four skills (Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking), combining prompt-chained generation, automated visual/audio synthesis (Matplotlib, Google Cloud TTS with SSML), Azure Pronunciation Assessment for speaking scoring, and human-centered personalization (placement tests, adaptive mock tests, skill-specific feedback) under an SDLC + LLMOps framework.
notes: Technique taxonomy: Uses OpenAI's conversation-based fine-tuning service (SFT on GPT-4o with system/user/assistant message format) combined with prompt-chaining, few-shot prompting, meta-prompting, and rational-augmented ensembles. Writing module uses prompt-chaining across interconnected tasks; reading/listening modules use fine-tuned GPT-4o over OCR-processed Cambridge IELTS 2020-2024 corpora. Architecture: 8 microservices (Test Generation, Media, Speech, Question Bank, Examination, Assessment, Authentication, Account Management) with React+Figma front-end, Golang back-end, MongoDB, Kubernetes, Cloudflare CDN, Emissary API Gateway, S3 storage. RLHF mentioned only as planned future work to refine grading accuracy. Explainable AI (XAI), BERTScore and BLEURT are also planned future additions. Speaking assessment uses Azure Pronunciation Assessment with four IELTS criteria (Fluency & Coherence, Lexical Resource, Grammatical Range & Accuracy, Pronunciation). Full PDF: 6 pages; IEEE ECTI-CON 2025; DOI 10.1109/ECTI-CON64990.2025.11101665.

title: Level-specific feedback generation for scene descriptions via fine-tuning multimodal large language models
venue: Computers and Education: Artificial Intelligence
llm_base: LLaVA (Qwen-2 backbone + ViT vision encoder + 2-layer MLP connector) Qwen-2 (unknown)
dataset_name: Curriculum-aligned level-specific feedback dataset (GPT-4o + RAG with Hong Kong primary and secondary English word lists)
dataset_size: 6.2K unique image-sentence-feedback triplets (4 level-feedback samples per image-sentence pair)
dataset_domain: Scene description writing / EFL
personalization_mechanism: Level-specific feedback conditioned on Hong Kong key stages 1-4 (primary 1-3, primary 4-6, secondary 1-3, secondary 3-6) via instruction prompt + curriculum word lists retrieved through RAG to control lexical/syntactic complexity
subject_area: EFL / English writing (scene description)
evaluation_metrics: Expert binary rating on correctness, readability, educational effectiveness; SBERT cosine similarity; Average out-of-level words (lexical appropriateness); Wilcoxon signed-rank test; Mann-Whitney U test (pilot learning gains)
quantitative_results: Expert evaluation: correctness 88-90.5%, readability 90-97%, educational effectiveness 90-98% across 4 key stages (200 samples). Mean SBERT similarity to ground-truth: Fine-tuned MLLM 0.87 vs GPT-4o 0.80, Llama 3.2-Vision 11B 0.74, LLaVA-1.6 7B 0.63 (600 samples). Average out-of-level words (N=100): Fine-tuned 0.75 vs GPT-4o 1.43, Llama 3.2-Vision 11B 3.39, LLaVA-1.6 7B 4.29 (p<0.001 Wilcoxon). Pilot experiment (n=66): level-specific group post-test M=75.57 vs general-feedback M=69.09, Mann-Whitney U p=.044.
limitations: Dataset and evaluation limited to Hong Kong English curriculum context; Restricted to single-image and single-sentence inputs (no multi-image or multi-sentence scene compositions); Fixed educational stages rather than individual learner ability modeling / dynamic learner modeling; Dataset not publicly released (only by email request); Model and code not publicly released; Generalizability to other EFL contexts not empirically validated; Human annotator review required during dataset filtering (labor cost)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_contribution: Constructs a curriculum-aligned multimodal instruction-following dataset using GPT-4o + RAG guided by Hong Kong primary/secondary English word lists, and fine-tunes a LLaVA-style MLLM (Qwen-2 backbone) with LoRA to generate level-specific feedback for scene description writing tasks across 4 educational stages, significantly outperforming LLaVA-1.6 7B, Llama 3.2-Vision 11B and GPT-4o in semantic alignment and lexical appropriateness.
notes: Paper is multimodal (vision + language). Architecture: ViT vision encoder (frozen) + 2-layer MLP vision-language connector (trained) + Qwen-2 LLM (LoRA-tuned). Base LLM exact parameter size not explicitly stated (Qwen-2, likely 7B but not confirmed in paper). Ft_hyperparams.learning_rate uses two values (LLM vs MLP) not representable as a single scalar. Adaptation combines SFT/instruction-tuning via LoRA with RAG used only during dataset construction (not at inference). Theoretical grounding: Vygotsky ZPD, Sweller cognitive load, Hattie & Timperley formative feedback. Ethics approval 2024:2025-0154. Funded by CILME/UDSAI/RGC (HK).

title: Automatic Large Language Models Creation of Interactive Learning Lessons
dataset_name: Research articles on effective tutoring practices (retrieved via RAG) + 3 human-crafted reference lessons
dataset_size: 3 human-crafted lessons used as reference topics; retrieval corpus of tutoring research articles
dataset_domain: Tutor training / middle-school mathematics tutoring
personalization_mechanism: Lessons are tailored to three fixed tutor-training topics (Encouraging Students' Independence, Encouraging Help-Seeking Behavior, Turning on Cameras) but not adapted to individual learner profiles.
subject_area: Tutor training for middle-school mathematics online tutoring
evaluation_metrics: 17-code rubric (lesson quality) informed by Vai & Sosulski; Cohen's kappa inter-rater reliability; Average rubric score per segmentation strategy; Per-section consensus ratings (x/3); Qualitative written feedback from lesson designers
quantitative_results: Average rubric scores across 5 prompting strategies: One-Seg=10.67, Two-Seg=12, Three-Seg=14.67 (best), Four-Seg=14, Five-Seg=13.33. Inter-rater reliability Cohen's kappa=0.72 (substantial agreement). Three-segment decomposition outperformed single-step generation; five-segment (most granular) performed slightly worse than three-segment.
limitations: Generic feedback content: LLM explains only correct answers, lacks targeted feedback for each distractor. Lack of clarity in title page and instruction sections (inconsistent use of 'learners' vs 'tutors', logical disconnects, excessive citations). Authenticity of references: RAG did not prevent fabricated/unverified citations in Conclusion sections. Evaluation rubric primarily informed by a single source (Vai & Sosulski). Study only evaluated via evaluators' judgments; no downstream learning-outcome experiment with tutors. Only three lesson topics and one LLM (GPT-4o) tested. Possible rating bias / manual rating inconsistencies.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes a two-step RAG + task-decomposition prompting pipeline with GPT-4o to automatically generate scenario-based interactive lessons for novice tutor training in middle-school math; empirically compares 1/2/3/4/5-segment prompting strategies and shows a three-segment decomposition yields highest-rated lessons, while also documenting strengths (time-saving, well-structured, unbiased) and weaknesses (generic feedback, reference hallucination, clarity gaps) against human-crafted lessons.
notes: No fine-tuning performed: adaptation is purely via RAG + prompt engineering (task decomposition into 1-5 segments, chained prompts). Base LLM is GPT-4o (gpt-4o-2024-05-13), closed-weight, accessed via API. Human-crafted reference lessons publicly shared via OSF repository; additional prompt details also on OSF. Lessons follow SMART competency framework (Social-emotional Learning, Mastery of Content, Advocacy, Relationship-building, Technology) and are structured in 5 sections: Title Page, Scenario I, Instruction, Scenario II, Conclusion. Evaluation by 2 experienced human coders/tutors + 2 lesson designers (written questionnaire). Two RQs in paper: RQ1 (effect of task decomposition level) and RQ2 (LLM vs human-crafted lessons). Output formats include MCQ and open-ended questions (short-answer) inside scenarios, plus instructional explanations and feedback.

title: PromptHive: Demonstrating Collaborative, Human-Centered OER Creation with LLMs
venue: TWO DECADES OF TEL. FROM LESSONS LEARNT TO CHALLENGES AHEAD, EC-TEL 2025, PT II
dataset_name: OATutor college algebra content (textbook-level hints)
dataset_size: college algebra textbook sequence; learning-gain study used a 3+5+3 question design with 358 college students
dataset_domain: mathematics (college algebra)
personalization_mechanism: Subject matter experts author and iterate prompts at system level (technical/format/compliance) and textbook level (pedagogical consistency across lessons); shared prompt library with upvoting refines prompts for specific content
subject_area: mathematics (college algebra)
evaluation_metrics: System Usability Scale (SUS); NASA-TLX cognitive load; XAI Trust Scale (Hoffman et al.); learning gain (post-test minus pre-test); hint creation time
quantitative_results: Study 1 (10 SMEs): SUS = 89/100 for PromptHive vs 40/100 (spreadsheet) and 62/100 (GUI) from prior work; cognitive load reduced by 50%; hint creation time reduced from months to hours. Study 2 (358 college students via Prolific): statistically significant learning gains from pre-test to post-test when using PromptHive-generated hints; no statistically significant difference vs human-authored hints.
limitations: Demo/short paper (3 pages) with limited methodological detail. Study 1 small sample (n=10 SMEs). Single domain tested (college algebra). LLM hallucination risk acknowledged as motivation but not directly measured. No direct measurement of factual accuracy of generated hints. Generalizability to other subjects and educational levels untested.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: PromptHive, an open-source human-in-the-loop platform for collaborative prompt engineering by subject matter experts to generate high-quality OER content (hints) with LLMs, featuring a Prompt Scratchpad for side-by-side prompt iteration and a Shared Prompt Library with upvoting; validated with a usability study (SUS=89) and a learning-gain study (n=358) showing AI-generated hints match human-authored hints in learning outcomes.
notes: Demo paper at L@S '25 (companion to CHI '25 full paper, reference [9] Reza et al. 2025). No fine-tuning is performed; adaptation is purely via structured prompting and human-in-the-loop refinement. Supports OpenAI schema-based models and Llama with structured output. Integrates with OATutor tutoring system. Platform described as open-source. Content format: hints (step-by-step explanations for math problems), mapped to 'explanation' in controlled vocabulary.

title: Enhancing Equitable Access to Secondary Education in Malawi Using Generative AI: A Pilot Study on the Agriculture Subject
venue: Electronic Journal of Information Systems in Developing Countries
dataset_name: MSCE Agriculture curriculum corpus (digitized textbooks approved by the Ministry of Education, study guides/pamphlets for agriculture revision, and past MSCE agriculture exam questions and answers)
dataset_size: not reported (only described as 'unique curated corpus'; PDF/Word documents processed with Unstructured.io and SpaCy)
dataset_domain: education - secondary - Malawi MSCE Agriculture
personalization_mechanism: Conversational chatbot (WhatsApp-like interface) with immediate feedback; quiz with Mastery Learning Logic (multiple attempts and progressive explanations); scaffolded prompts following ZPD (constructivist scaffolding); no individual student-profile personalization beyond conversational history and local response cache.
subject_area: Agriculture (Malawi MSCE secondary curriculum; pilot with potential extension to other subjects such as physics and chemistry)
evaluation_metrics: Perceived Usefulness (PU) mean=4.3 (SD=0.65); Perceived Ease of Use (PEOU) mean=4.5 (SD=0.52); Satisfaction mean=4.2 (SD=0.70); Intention to Use mean=4.1 (SD=0.74); regression PU on satisfaction beta=0.42 t=3.98 p<0.01; regression PEOU on satisfaction beta=0.27 t=2.14 p<0.05; regression PU on intention beta=0.38 t=3.46 p<0.01 R2=0.29; inter-coder reliability Cohen kappa=0.81; agreement percentages on Likert items
quantitative_results: 200 students (103F/97M, Forms 3-4) and 20 teachers across 4 schools (urban, peri-urban, 2 rural) over 6 weeks. 93% would continue using and recommend the chatbot; 82% satisfied with the chatbot as a learning tool; 79% found it easy to use; 80% considered it useful for learning; 70% reported better understanding of confusing topics; 85% found responses clear and relevant; 88% overall satisfaction; 83% would continue using it; 79% agreement on availability and speed; 92% comfortable using it independently; 80% improved access to information; 75% time savings; 70% greater confidence in Agriculture; 100% of teachers agreed it enhances learning; 17/20 teachers noted increased student engagement. No significant differences in learning gains between rural/urban (p>0.05) or CDSS/national (p>0.05).
limitations: Reliance on self-reported data (possible Hawthorne bias). Lack of observational evidence from classroom intervention. Limited exploration of risks (over-dependence, cheating, misuse). Connectivity issues in rural areas limited usage. Limited device access (many students shared smartphones). Chatbot available only in English; multilingual support (Chichewa) suggested. Occasional factual errors in responses requiring teacher verification. Imperfect cultural localization (e.g., examples referencing unfamiliar crops). Need for continuous curricular content updates. Scope limited to a single subject (Agriculture) and to Malawi. Small-scale pilot (200 students, 20 teachers, 4 schools, 6 weeks).
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Design, fine-tuning, and pilot evaluation of an educational chatbot based on LLaMA 2 (3B) fine-tuned on the Malawi MSCE Agriculture curricular corpus, with a three-layer RAG architecture (React/React Native + FastAPI + LangChain + local knowledge base), aimed at improving equitable access to secondary education in low-resource sub-Saharan African contexts. Provides empirical evidence in an underrepresented ICT4D context, validating TAM/ISSM and constructivist principles (ZPD, Mastery Learning) with local curricular alignment and stakeholder co-design.
notes: Base model: LLaMA 2 (3 billion parameters) accessed via API as an external model. Techniques: supervised fine-tuning on MSCE corpus + RAG pipeline (LangChain) with semantic retrieval over a local Agriculture knowledge base; whether LoRA/QLoRA or full fine-tuning was used is not specified, nor are hyperparameters (rank, alpha, epochs, lr, batch size, quantization). Technical stack: React (web) and React Native (Android) frontend with local cache; FastAPI backend in Python 3.12; LangChain JS SDK ('Core' module); document processing with Unstructured.io and SpaCy. Theoretical frameworks: Design Science Research (Hevner), TAM (Davis 1989), ISSM (DeLone & McLean 2003), Constructivism (Vygotsky ZPD), Mastery Learning (Bloom). Convergent mixed methods: pre/post surveys with TAM/ISSM-validated Likert scales (SPSS), focus groups coded in NVivo 14 using theoretical framework + open inductive coding, 5 emerging themes (empowerment, engagement, technical challenges, trust, future expansion), member-check with 3 students and 2 teachers. RAG mitigates hallucinations and bias; weekly audit of 10% of responses for factual accuracy, cultural adequacy, and gender neutrality. Data not publicly available due to privacy/ethical constraints. Funding: National Council for Science and Technology. Published in The Electronic Journal of Information Systems in Developing Countries 2026;92:e70062. Inferred RQ coverage: RQ1 (base models, LLaMA2 3B); RQ2 (techniques, SFT+RAG); RQ3 (output formats, quiz, feedback, explanations, tutoring); RQ4 (partial personalization via scaffolding and Mastery Learning); RQ5 (levels/subjects, secondary/Agriculture); RQ6 (evaluation, mixed-methods user study with TAM/ISSM); RQ7 (limitations and adoption barriers in LMIC).

title: Multi-objective math problem generation using large language model through an adaptive multi-level retrieval augmentation framework
venue: Information Fusion
llm_base: GPT-3.5-Turbo (>7B); Gemini-Pro (>7B); ChatGLM3 6B (6B); Llama-3-Instruct 8B (8B); Baichuan2-Chat 7B (7B); Baichuan2-Chat 13B (13B); Qwen1.5-Chat (mentor model) 14B (14B)
dataset_name: MMPG (Multi-Objective Math Problem Generation dataset)
dataset_size: 9000 math problems (3362 deduplicated knowledge points, 6 question types); expanded to 16,000 QA pairs (7:1 train/test split) for mentor fine-tuning; 200 evaluation samples
dataset_domain: Math problems (exam and in-class assignments, elementary to middle school)
personalization_mechanism: User problem request specifies three generation objectives (question type T, knowledge points K, difficulty D); mentor model adaptively decides whether to retrieve and provides revision suggestions; multi-level retriever (similarity-based + objectives-based + fine-grained features scorer) selects top-3 examples tailored to the user query, enabling controllable generation per learner needs
subject_area: Mathematics
evaluation_metrics: GPT-4 quality score (1-10, averaged over 6 dimensions: objectives fulfillment, originality, readability, applicability, usefulness, solvability); Solvability rate; Perplexity (PPL, computed with Llama-3-70B-Instruct); Generation time (minutes); BLEU-4, ROUGE-1/2/L, Exact Match (for mentor model training evaluation); BERTScore F1 (similarity to retrieved examples); Win/Tie/Loss rate against GPT-4 (GPT-4o as judge)
quantitative_results: AMRAF outperforms w/o retrieval and w/ full retrieval baselines across 6 target LLMs. Scores (our vs w/o retrieval / w/ retrieval): GPT-3.5-Turbo 7.69 / 7.35 / 7.53; Gemini-Pro 7.71 / 7.36 / 7.60; ChatGLM3-6B 7.11 / 6.67 / 6.82; Llama-3-8B-Instruct 7.57 / 7.19 / 7.46; Baichuan2-7B-Chat 7.38 / 6.68 / 7.27; Baichuan2-13B-Chat 7.46 / 7.11 / 7.33. Solvability gains up to +7% (Baichuan2-7B). PPL reduced (e.g., Gemini-Pro from 13.061 to 8.447). Generation time ~9.9s/problem vs 30s full-retrieval (~33 min vs 100 min global). Win rate vs GPT-4: GPT-3.5-Turbo 26% to 34%; ChatGLM3-6B 19% to 23.5%. Mentor fine-tuning improved BLEU-4 21.03 to 38.87, EM 72% to 77%. Optimal 3 retrieved examples.
limitations: Mentor model may output incorrect retrieval tags (NO) when facing complex combinations of constraints, missing knowledge points in the generated problem. Corpus coverage limitation: retrieval returns only single-knowledge-point problems when compound combinations are requested, degrading quality. Generative model itself may still produce mathematically inconsistent or logically incoherent problems even with correct mentor guidance and relevant retrieved examples. Mentor model adds training (~12h A100) and inference overhead (2-3 s per query). Dataset limited to Chinese and to elementary-to-middle school levels. Evaluation mainly relies on LLM-as-judge (GPT-4) rather than large-scale human or student evaluation.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes MMPG (multi-objective math problem generation) task jointly controlling question type, knowledge points, and difficulty, and introduces AMRAF, a plug-and-play adaptive multi-level retrieval-augmented generation framework where a LoRA-fine-tuned Qwen1.5-14B-Chat mentor model decides when retrieval is needed and provides revision suggestions, combined with a hybrid similarity- and objective-based retriever plus fine-grained features scorer. Releases a 9000-sample Chinese MMPG benchmark dataset.
notes: The framework does NOT fine-tune the target generation LLMs (GPT-3.5-Turbo, Gemini-Pro, ChatGLM3-6B, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Baichuan2-7B/13B-Chat); only the auxiliary mentor model (Qwen1.5-14B-Chat) is LoRA fine-tuned with SFT on alpaca-style instruction data. Adaptation techniques thus span RAG (primary), LoRA/SFT/instruction-tuning (for the mentor), and few-shot prompting (top-3 in-context examples). Educational level is Chinese K-12 (grades 1-9 / elementary to middle school). Data availability stated as 'on request'. Hyperparameters reported: lr=2e-5, epochs=3, warmup 3%, gradient clipping 1.0, max length 2048, ~0.05% trainable params via LoRA; rank/alpha/batch size not reported.

title: System Design and Evaluation of RAG-Enhanced Digital Humans in Design Education: Analyzing Cognitive Load and Instructional Efficiency
venue: Applied Sciences (Switzerland)
dataset_name: Curated Modern World Design History knowledge base (digital textbooks, academic articles, architectural case studies)
dataset_size: unknown (2250 student queries logged during study; 200 validation queries for threshold tuning; 500 sampled responses for expert evaluation)
dataset_domain: Design history / design education (Modern World Design History)
personalization_mechanism: Context-aware responses via RAG retrieval (Top-K=3 chunks with tau>=0.60) tailored to individual student queries; self-paced Q&A, interactive case studies, and self-directed learning control through digital human interface (LiveTalking). No explicit learner-profile modeling.
subject_area: Design history (environmental design / Modern World Design History)
evaluation_metrics: post-test score (learning outcome); classroom engagement composite index (query frequency + interaction duration); Paas Mental Effort Rating Scale (9-point Likert); Instructional Efficiency E = (Z_perf - Z_effort)/sqrt(2); Cohen's d effect sizes; Pearson correlation; multiple linear regression R^2; retrieval acceptance rate; factual accuracy / hallucination rate; Precision, Recall, F1 for retrieval threshold sensitivity; Total Turn-Around Time (TTAT) latency
quantitative_results: Multi-center RCT N=150 (Exp=75, Ctrl=75) across 3 universities. Post-test: Exp M=86.42 (SD=6.85) vs Ctrl M=78.10 (SD=7.64), t(148)=7.01, p<0.001, d=1.14. Classroom engagement: Exp M=15.84 vs Ctrl M=11.20, t(148)=8.54, p<0.001, d=1.39. Paas Mental Effort: Exp M=4.32 (SD=1.65) vs Ctrl M=7.15 (SD=1.78), t(148)=-10.09, p<0.001, d=1.65 (abstract reports d=1.71). Instructional Efficiency: Exp E=+0.72 (SD=0.55) vs Ctrl E=-0.68 (SD=0.62), t(148)=12.35, p<0.001, d=2.39. Regression R^2=0.68 (Group beta=0.465, Pre-test beta=0.582, both p<0.001). Retrieval: 92.4% acceptance rate at tau=0.60 (optimal F1=0.84); 96.2% factual accuracy; 3.8% hallucination rate. Average TTAT 5.2 +/- 1.1 s for complex queries.
limitations: High-end dual NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU hardware footprint limits adoption in resource-constrained schools. Novelty effect: 5-week intervention may have inflated engagement; longitudinal studies needed. Conflation of delivery medium (digital human avatar) with instructional mechanism (RAG retrieval), with no Text-Only RAG control arm to isolate contributions. Reliance on standardized post-tests lacks construct validity for practical skills, creative problem-solving, and design thinking; no design critiques, portfolio reviews, or peer evaluations. Contextual specificity: single discipline (Design History) at a specific Chinese geographic locale limits transferability. Interaction latency of ~5 s exceeds sub-second conversational norms, risking flow disruption. System lacks interpretive nuance and emotional empathy, performing poorly on abstract critique queries ('Why does this design feel sad?'). Participant blinding not possible given the AI avatar nature of the intervention.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Designs and empirically validates via multi-center RCT (N=150) a locally-deployed RAG-enhanced digital human tutoring system for design history education, integrating Qwen3-30B (4-bit AWQ), BGE-Large-Zh embeddings, FAISS retrieval (Top-K=3, tau=0.60) and LiveTalking anthropomorphic avatar. Demonstrates large effect sizes on learning outcomes, engagement, and cognitive load, confirming that coupling RAG epistemic scaffolding with embodied AI transfers extraneous load into germane load (Instructional Efficiency d=2.39).
notes: No fine-tuning performed; adaptation relies exclusively on RAG + prompting with off-the-shelf Qwen3-30B. ft_method=none. Appendix A includes reference Streamlit/FAISS/OpenWebUI code (partial reproducibility). Dataset/models NOT publicly available (institutional confidentiality) though base models (Qwen3, BGE-Large-Zh, LiveTalking) are open-source. Personalization is query-driven and self-paced rather than profile-based (level=partial). The PDF lacks explicit fine-tuning hyperparameters because the study performs no fine-tuning; the only model-side hyperparameter is 4-bit AWQ quantization for Qwen3-30B inference. Primary output format is tutoring dialogue via speech (digital human) plus automated lecture scripts generated from the RAG module.

title: AI-Driven Question Generation System: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Examination Systems
venue: Proc. Stud. Res. Conf. Appl. Comput.: AI Innov. a Better Tomorrow, SRC
dataset_name: SciQ + RACE + CosmosQA
dataset_size: SciQ: ~13,000 MCQs; RACE: 97,000+ questions from 28,000 passages; CosmosQA: 35,600 questions
dataset_domain: Science (SciQ), Reading comprehension from middle/high school English exams (RACE), Commonsense reasoning (CosmosQA)
subject_area: Science, Reading comprehension, Commonsense reasoning (general exam/assessment domains)
evaluation_metrics: F1-score; Precision; Recall; ROUGE-1; ROUGE-2; ROUGE-L; BERTScore; Token Efficiency
quantitative_results: Mistral 7B best on SciQ (F1=0.891, ROUGE-L=0.3699, Precision=0.8462, BERTScore~0.86, Token Efficiency=0.74). Qwen 2.5-Max best on RACE (F1=0.8252, Recall=0.86 on RACE; Recall=0.9405 on SciQ) and best on CosmosQA (F1=0.702, ROUGE-2=0.1705, ROUGE-L=0.3209). LLaMA 3.2 lowest on CosmosQA (F1=0.646). Accuracy heatmap: Mistral 7B 0.94/0.88/0.80; Qwen 2.5 0.89/0.90/0.85; LLaMA 3.2 0.91/0.86/0.75 (SciQ/RACE/CosmosQA).
limitations: LLaMA 3.2 struggles with implicit reasoning and unstructured question formats (lowest CosmosQA performance); Training data biases can result in AI-generated questions skewed toward certain topics, linguistic patterns, or cultural perspectives affecting fairness; LLMs prone to generating factually incorrect or misleading questions, particularly in domains requiring precise knowledge (hallucination); Question difficulty control to match intended learner level remains challenging; Mistral 7B required greater computational resources; Limited to English-language benchmark datasets; no domain-specific medical/law/engineering evaluation
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Benchmarking study comparing three state-of-the-art LLMs (LLaMA 3.2, Mistral 7B, Qwen 2.5-Max) fine-tuned with LoRA and 4-bit quantization via the Unsloth framework for automated MCQ generation across three educational benchmark datasets (SciQ, RACE, CosmosQA). Identifies model-task fit: Mistral 7B for structured scientific questions, Qwen 2.5-Max for multi-step reasoning, LLaMA 3.2 for efficient structured MCQs. Proposes hybrid approach leveraging different models for different question types.
notes: Models uploaded to Hugging Face for reproducibility (model_public=yes); code availability not explicitly stated. Datasets are well-known public benchmarks. Fine-tuning is SFT via LoRA with 4-bit quantization (effectively QLoRA). Specific LoRA rank/alpha and number of epochs not reported (only max training steps=60). No personalization mechanism; focus is benchmarking LLM capability on exam question generation. Published in 2025 IEEE Student Research Conference on Applied Computing (SRC).

title: beSEL: A Human-Aligned LLM Teaching Assistant for Enhancing Social-Emotional Learning
venue: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN EDUCATION: POSTERS AND LATE BREAKING RESULTS, WORKSHOPS AND TUTORIALS, INDUSTRY AND INNOVATION TRACKS, PRACTITIONERS, DOCTORAL CONSORTIUM, BLUE SKY, AND WIDEAIED, AIED 2025, PT II
dataset_name: Teachers' Response + Teaching Design (custom SEL datasets built from 362 SEL elementary classroom videos and accompanying lesson plans)
dataset_size: 362 SEL classroom videos and corresponding lesson plans (two QA datasets derived; exact QA pair count not reported)
dataset_language: Chinese (elementary school classroom videos; transcribed with FunASR)
dataset_domain: Social-Emotional Learning (SEL) in elementary education
personalization_mechanism: Teacher-centered, human-aligned assistant with two specialized LoRA-fine-tuned agents (one for instructional interaction, one for lesson plan generation); integrates real-time monitoring of students' emotions and context-aware responses under teacher supervision; proposed (future) cultural adaptation via prompt examples, modular web-search plugins, and hierarchical prompt templates for age-specific emotional/linguistic patterns.
subject_area: Social-Emotional Learning (SEL)
evaluation_metrics: 5-point Likert scale (-2 to +2) questionnaires across five SEL dimensions (Inclusive Learning Climate, Emotional Regulation Support, Cultural & Equity Awareness, Social Connection Promotion, Goal & Efficacy Support) on two tasks (instructional interaction and lesson plan generation); Paired samples t-test mean differences between beSEL vs Qwen2.5-3B and vs GPT-4o mini
quantitative_results: Pilot study with 39 SEL elementary-school teachers evaluating beSEL, Qwen2.5-3B, and GPT-4o mini on two tasks using questionnaires based on the Washington SEL Implementation Brief. beSEL achieved the highest mean scores: Task 1 (instructional interaction) mean = 1.092; Task 2 (lesson plan generation) mean = 1.008. Paired t-test mean differences (beSEL vs Qwen2.5-3B / beSEL vs GPT-4o mini): Inclusive Learning Climate 0.564***/0.397*** (T1), 0.295***/0.244** (T2); Emotional Regulation Support 0.641***/0.474*** (T1), 0.154*/0.321*** (T2); Cultural & Equity Awareness 0.641***/0.449*** (T1), 0.282***/0.205** (T2); Social Connection Promotion 0.538***/0.410*** (T1), 0.231**/0.026 (T2); Goal & Efficacy Support 0.590***/0.474*** (T1), 0.141/0.128 (T2); Total 0.595***/0.441*** (T1), 0.221**/0.185** (T2). beSEL showed significant gains in all five dimensions vs Qwen2.5-3B (p<0.01) and significant improvements over GPT-4o mini in most dimensions.
limitations: Pilot/preliminary study with only 39 SEL teachers from one elementary school (limited sample and single school context); Evaluation restricted to self-report Likert questionnaires; no objective/automatic metrics or student learning outcomes measured; Dataset built from 362 classroom videos/lesson plans collected in a specific cultural/regional context (Chinese elementary schools); potential cultural bias and limited transferability across diverse cultural and educational settings; No real-world classroom deployment yet; lack of positive/negative feedback data from actual use; Fine-tuning hyperparameters and exact QA dataset sizes not reported (limited reproducibility detail); Does not yet support multimodal (audio/tone) interaction; plan to extend with Step-Audio in future work; Potential fairness issues acknowledged; formal fairness-aware alignment and RLHF adaptation proposed only as future work
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Introduces beSEL, a teacher-centered, human-aligned LLM teaching assistant for Social-Emotional Learning (SEL). Contributes: (i) a multi-round LLM-based pipeline to build two domain-specific SEL QA datasets (Teachers' Response and Teaching Design) from 362 award-winning elementary classroom videos and lesson plans using Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct for segmentation/matching; (ii) two LoRA-based supervised fine-tuned Qwen2.5-3B agents (instructional interaction and lesson plan generation) with a KL-divergence constraint; and (iii) integration into a full pre-/in-/after-class SEL workflow with a data dashboard, demonstrating superior emotional intelligence and situational understanding vs Qwen2.5-3B and GPT-4o mini in a pilot with 39 teachers.
notes: Base LLM: Qwen2.5-3B (fine-tuned) and Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct used only as a tool for data pipeline segmentation/matching (not fine-tuned). Two separate LoRA-fine-tuned agents derived from Qwen2.5-3B. Evaluation framework: Washington SEL Implementation Brief for Educators (5 core SEL competency dimensions). Datasets are in Chinese (FunASR transcription of Chinese elementary classroom videos). No specific LoRA hyperparameters (rank, alpha, epochs, batch size, learning rate) reported in the paper. No code, dataset, or model release mentioned. Future work: multimodal (Step-Audio), RLHF, fairness-aware alignment, real-world classroom deployment.

title: TwinStar: A Novel Design for Enhanced Test Question Generation Using Dual-LLM Engine
venue: Applied Sciences (Switzerland)
dataset_name: Exam_Question_Datasets (Dataset 1 + Dataset 2, augmented with ChatGPT-3.5)
dataset_size: 741 labeled Q-A pairs (Dataset 1: 141 + Dataset 2: 600) plus GPT-3.5 data augmentation across Bloom levels; 141-question test set; 20 generated per Bloom level for evaluation (120 total)
dataset_domain: General subjects (literature, mathematics, biology, medicine, geography, computer science) for fine-tuning; university-level calculus (functions, derivatives, integrals) as target domain
personalization_mechanism: Input instruction specifies target Bloom's cognitive level and knowledge point; multiround prompting (3 rounds) aligns generated questions with the requested cognitive level and knowledge domain; cognitive-level evaluation model provides iterative feedback until Bloom adherence is satisfied
subject_area: University-level calculus (mathematics: functions, derivatives, integrals); fine-tuning corpora also cover literature, biology, medicine, geography and computer science
evaluation_metrics: Bloom adherence (BA); Knowledge relevance (KR); Average accuracy (equally weighted BA and KR); Standard deviation (SD); Cognitive-level recognition accuracy (confusion-matrix heatmaps)
quantitative_results: TwinStar (ChatGLM2-6B): BA=0.93, KR=0.89, Avg=0.91, SD=0.02. Baseline GLM2: BA=0.13, KR=0.03, Avg=0.08. TwinStar improves BA, KR and Avg by factors of 6, 29 and 10 respectively over baseline. Outperforms GPT-3.5 (Avg=0.89), GPT-4.0 (0.89), Claude (0.86) and Bard (0.81) in Avg by 2%, 2%, 6% and 12%. BA gains vs GPT-3.5/GPT-4.0/Claude/Bard: +19%, +21%, +13%, +50%. Cognitive-level evaluation model accuracy improved from 40% to 70% after fine-tuning on 1000 questions. TwinStar (LLaMA2-13B): BA=0.94, KR=0.83, Avg=0.89 vs baseline LLaMA2 (Avg=0.72). Memory footprint: ~12 GB (ChatGLM2-6B), ~26 GB (LLaMA2-13B).
limitations: Fine-tuning datasets annotated with Bloom levels are exceedingly scarce for specific subjects such as university-level calculus, forcing use of general-domain data. KR remains lower than BA for GLM2-pt after fine-tuning because the training corpus does not match the calculus domain. LLaMA2 generates more complex sentences, so simple 2-shot examples are insufficient to guide it (BA/KR drop in LLaMA2-pt-RP-2shot). No expert human evaluation of generated questions; evaluation relies solely on automatic BA/KR metrics. Only 120 generated questions (20 per Bloom level) used for final evaluation, a small test sample. No hyperparameter details reported (rank, alpha, epochs, LR, batch size), limiting reproducibility. Duplicate detection mechanism is simple (exact membership check) and may miss semantic duplicates.
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes TwinStar, a dual-LLM engine that orchestrates a fine-tuned question generation model and a fine-tuned cognitive-level evaluation model (both built on lightweight LLMs, ChatGLM2-6B and LLaMA2-13B) combined with P-tuning, multiround prompting and few-shot examples, to generate test questions aligned with Bloom's taxonomy cognitive levels and specified knowledge points; achieves higher Bloom adherence than GPT-3.5, GPT-4.0, Claude and Bard with a fraction of the memory footprint and without an expert-curated question bank.
notes: Dataset publicly available at https://figshare.com/articles/dataset/Exam_Question_Datasets/22597957. Implementation details such as LoRA/adapter rank, epochs or LR are not reported, so ft_method is recorded as generic SFT with P-tuning on prompts. Data augmentation is performed using ChatGPT-3.5 to rebalance Bloom-level distributions (analyzing, evaluating, creating). The architecture uses an iterative feedback loop (generation vs. evaluation) plus a redundancy detector. Funding: NSFC 62372183 and Shanghai STCSM 24LZ1400500.

title: Knowledge-enhanced large language models for automatic lesson plan generation
venue: Humanities and Social Sciences Communications
dataset_name: LPKB (Lesson Plan Knowledge Base) + lesson plan dataset D = S + S*
dataset_size: 139,512 lesson plans / 521,326 components (real-world S: 100,000+ plans crawled; simulated S* generated via GPT-4). Curriculum coverage K-12: 68,363 elementary + 42,594 middle school + 28,555 high school plans
dataset_domain: K-12 lesson plan generation (Mathematics 40%, Chinese 15.4%, English 9.3%, Physics 6.2%, Science 4.7%, Art 4.2%, Chemistry 3.5%, History 3.3%, Music 2.8%, Geography 3.1%, Biology 2.5%, Other 5.0%)
personalization_mechanism: Curriculum information (grade level, subject, textbook version, lesson name) conditions generation; Student Learning Analysis (SLA) component assesses students' prerequisite knowledge and developmental levels; retrieval via knowledge retriever adapts content to specific teaching contexts
subject_area: Multi-subject K-12 curriculum (Mathematics, Chinese, English, Physics, Chemistry, Biology, History, Geography, Music, Art, Science)
evaluation_metrics: Structure Soundness (1-3); Content Accuracy across 7 components: TA, SLA, LO, KDP, MAT, LP, AE (1-3 each); Logic Consistency (1-3); Language Finesse (1-3); Overall (average); Fleiss Kappa for inter-annotator agreement; Win/Tie/Loss rates vs. human-written lesson plans
quantitative_results: LessonPlanLM-72B achieves best overall score 2.843 vs baselines: ERNIE Bot 4.0=2.165, iFlytek Spark 3.5=1.977, GLM-4=1.672, GPT-4=2.101, Claude 3.5=2.216, Gemini 1.5=1.961, Qwen2-7B=1.520, Self-Critique=2.475, Prompt4ChatGPT=2.479. LessonPlanLM-7B=2.709. Structure Soundness=2.990-3.000 (perfect or near-perfect for LessonPlanLM). Ablation: removing RAFT drops content accuracy 2.581 to 2.144 and language finesse 2.505 to 2.060. Vs human experts: LessonPlanLM wins 44.00%/47.62% on Structure Soundness against NPSSSEP/Wookey; ~80% of generated plans have comparable or better performance than manually written plans. Fleiss Kappa ≥ 0.83 for annotator agreement (all >0.6).
limitations: Focused on the Chinese education system and its lesson plan paradigm, with limited generalization to other curricular structures and educational systems; GPT-4 automatic scoring showed large gaps with human evaluation, so LLM-as-judge was deemed unreliable for this task (only human evaluations used); Evaluation is limited to 200 lesson plans with 1400 components for main comparison and 50 plans × 350 components for per-annotator analysis; Base models limited to Qwen2 family (7B and 72B); other architectures not explored for fine-tuning; Simulated lesson plans S* depend on GPT-4 quality and rewriting prompts, potentially propagating GPT-4 biases; Language Finesse gap vs human experts remains large (Win=8.67%/0.67% vs NPSSSEP/Wookey), indicating stylistic fluency is still below expert level
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: LessonPlanLM: a knowledge-enhanced LLM framework for automatic K-12 lesson plan generation that (1) constructs a novel Lesson Plan Knowledge Base (LPKB) with curriculum extractor, semantics collector, and knowledge retriever from 100K+ real-world lesson plans; (2) fine-tunes Qwen2-7B/72B via SFT + Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning (RAFT) to generate structurally integral, logically coherent, and accurate lesson plans step-by-step; (3) proposes a 4-dimensional evaluation framework (Structure Soundness, Content Accuracy, Logic Consistency, Language Finesse) and demonstrates superiority over 8 proprietary/open LLM baselines plus comparable quality to human-expert-written lesson plans.
notes: Paper published in Humanities and Social Sciences Communications (Nature Portfolio), 2025, DOI: 10.1057/s41599-025-06004-2. Code and data at https://github.com/ai4ed/LessonPlan. RAFT = Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning (Zhang et al. 2024), combining SFT with retrieval and training the model to use retrieved documents robustly. Lesson plans structured in 7 components: Textbook Analysis (TA), Student Learning Analysis (SLA), Lesson Objectives (LO), Key & Difficult Points (KDP), Materials (MAT), Lesson Procedure (LP), Assessment Methods (AM/AE). Step-by-step generation pipeline: LO+KDP, then LP, then MAT. Three expert teachers (5+ years) annotated; two certified math teachers (10+ years) reviewed discrepancies. RQ coverage approximate: RQ1 (base LLMs used), RQ2 (adaptation: SFT+RAFT+RAG), RQ3 (personalization partial via curriculum conditioning), RQ4 (output format: lesson plan), RQ5 (evaluation methodology), RQ6 (educational effectiveness via human expert comparison).

title: AI-Empowered Pedagogical Reform in Power Electronics: A Practical Approach Based on RAG and Virtual Simulation
venue: Proc. Int. Symp. Artif. Intell. for Educ., ISAIE
dataset_name: Power Electronics Q&A pairs + 15,000+ technical documents (FAISS, 768-dim embeddings)
dataset_size: 50,000+ Q&A pairs for fine-tuning; 15,000+ documents indexed for RAG
dataset_domain: Power electronics engineering education
personalization_mechanism: Intelligent tutoring system (AITEE-PowerElec) with RAG-backed LLM providing 24/7 personalized Q&A, Socratic dialogue, clarifying concepts, and personalized resource recommendations; learning analytics engine (HMM) identifies patterns and predicts outcomes; adaptive feedback in virtual simulations
subject_area: Power Electronics / Electrical Engineering
evaluation_metrics: Daily Performance score; Final Exam score; Lab Reports score; Project Innovation score; Cohen's d; p-value; BM25 + dense retrieval recall@10 (0.89)
quantitative_results: Quasi-experimental study, 3 semesters, n=180 (90 experimental vs 90 control). Experimental group outperformed control across all metrics (all p<0.001): Daily Performance 93.2±5.1 vs 85.1±7.3 (d=1.43); Final Exam 83.5±4.3 vs 70.4±6.8 (d=1.49, ~19% increase); Lab Reports 94.7±3.8 vs 81.5±5.9 (d=1.62, ~16% improvement); Project Innovation 90.2±3.5 vs 75.0±6.2 (d=1.19, ~20% rise). RAG retrieval recall@10 = 0.89.
limitations: Potential over-reliance on AI applications acknowledged by authors; Single institution (NingboTech University) limits generalizability; Quasi-experimental design with randomization only by prior GPA/math scores; No long-term retention evaluation; Reproducibility not supported (no public code, data or model); Fine-tuning hyperparameters and training procedure not reported
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Holistic AI-enhanced pedagogical framework ('trinity': Intelligent Tutoring + Virtual-Physical Experimentation + Project Exploration) for Power Electronics higher-ed. Combines a domain-specific fine-tuned GPT-3.5-turbo with RAG (FAISS, BM25+dense hybrid, knowledge graph) in an ITS (AITEE-PowerElec), an AI-enhanced MATLAB/Simulink+PSIM virtual lab with fault injection and GA/PSO parameter optimization, and a PBL module using generative AI (ChatGPT, Copilot). Empirically validated with a controlled quasi-experiment (n=180) showing large effect sizes (Cohen's d 1.19-1.62) across theoretical, practical and innovation outcomes.
notes: Base model GPT-3.5-turbo fine-tuned on 50k+ domain Q&A pairs; ft_method mapped to SFT (no LoRA/QLoRA details given). Output formats in vocabulary: explanation, tutoring-dialogue, feedback, problem, code (MATLAB scripts); additional outputs not in vocab include design parameter recommendations, fault diagnostics, and report drafting. Personalization marked 'partial' (adaptive ITS and feedback, but no explicit learner-model parameters reported). Subject area 'Power Electronics' not in controlled vocab for educational_level, included as free text. Learning Analytics module uses Hidden Markov Models; knowledge graph stored in Neo4j; vector DB FAISS with 768-dim embeddings.

title: PromptHive: Bringing Subject Matter Experts Back to the Forefront with Collaborative Prompt Engineering for Educational Content Creation
venue: Conf Hum Fact Comput Syst Proc
dataset_name: OATutor OpenStax College Algebra 2e content pool
dataset_size: 10 textbook-level prompts, 59 lesson-level prompts (covering 80 questions across 10 lessons of an entire college algebra textbook)
dataset_domain: College-level mathematics (algebra)
personalization_mechanism: SMEs collaboratively craft textbook-level and lesson-level prompts via PromptHive interface; prompts are tailored per lesson context and student audience (e.g., age, learning disabilities, tone). Personalization operates at the authoring level, not per individual learner.
subject_area: Mathematics (College Algebra)
evaluation_metrics: SUS (System Usability Scale); NASA-TLX (cognitive workload); XAI Trust scale (Hoffman et al.); Pre-/post-test learning gain (%); Time-on-task; Wilcoxon signed-rank, Mann-Whitney U, Kruskal-Wallis, mixed linear model
quantitative_results: Study 1 (n=10 SMEs): SUS = 89/100 (excellent usability); NASA-TLX cognitive workload dropped from 55.17 to 26.73 (reduced ~50%); authoring time reduced by factor of 30. Study 2 (n=358 learners, 549 lesson submissions): Average learning gain 8.13% PromptHive vs 7.47% human-only control (p = 0.688, no statistically significant difference; PromptHive matches human-authored quality). 4/10 lessons showed significant positive learning gain with PromptHive vs 2/10 for human-only. Pre-test scores: 51.9% PromptHive, 45.9% control.
limitations: Output quality is inherently tied to underlying foundation model (GPT-4o), with associated risks of bias, hallucinations, and lack of transparency; Participant group limited to senior undergraduates/recent graduates with prior content-authoring experience across STEM subjects; limited domain expertise and narrower perspectives may affect generalizability; Generated content is not directly editable by SMEs in the interface (editing limited to prompts, not outputs); SMEs not involved in system-level prompt design (focus kept on pedagogy); ~40% of manual editing time was spent fixing minor syntactic/rendering issues from PromptHive output; Evaluation confined to mathematics (college algebra) and OATutor; generalization to other domains/systems not empirically tested; Prolific recruitment sample may not fully represent broader undergraduate population
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: PromptHive: a fully open-source collaborative prompt-authoring interface that brings subject-matter experts back to the forefront of LLM-driven educational content creation. Integrates a 4-stage workflow (Load, Author, Iterate, Share) at two abstraction levels (textbook- and lesson-level), pairs prompts with outputs for A/B comparison, leverages GPT-4o structured outputs and self-consistency to mitigate hallucinations, and includes a logging engine capturing rich prompt-evolution interaction data. Validated through a 10-SME user study and a 358-learner controlled learning-gain study, showing PromptHive matches manually authored hint quality while cutting cognitive load ~50% and authoring time by 30x.
notes: This is a human-centered HCI / prompt-engineering contribution, NOT a fine-tuning study. No model training, LoRA, or adapters. LLM adaptation happens purely via collaborative prompt engineering by SMEs plus self-consistency decoding. Code open-sourced at github.com/mohireza/prompthive/. Published at CHI '25. Authors: Mohi Reza, Ioannis Anastasopoulos, Shreya Bhandari, Zachary A. Pardos (U. Toronto / UC Berkeley). IRB-approved. Sample size: 10 SMEs (Study 1), 358 learners / 225 unique after exclusions (Study 2). NASA-TLX reductions across all six subscales (mental, physical, temporal, performance, effort, frustration).

title: AI for All: Adaptive, Accessible, and Inclusive Learning Experiences in the Age of Intelligent LMSs
venue: Information (Switzerland)
dataset_name: Authoritative PDF textbook (Medieval and Modern History 2nd High School, Chapter 1 - The first centuries of Byzantium, among others); ~12 courses x ~13 chapters x ~5 sub-units = ~780 sub-units
dataset_size: ~780 learning sub-units across ~12 courses
dataset_domain: secondary education (history and other school subjects)
output_format: summary; lesson-plan; quiz-mcq; explanation; tutoring-dialogue; other
personalization_mechanism: Hybrid learner model combining initial FSLSM/ILS questionnaire (active/reflective, sensing/intuitive, visual/verbal, sequential/global) as low-weight prior with continuous behavior-based profiling derived from Moodle logs (resource views, quiz attempts, navigation, forum participation, chatbot interactions). Profiles drive non-prescriptive personalization via resource prioritization, recommendation cues and chatbot response style (explanatory depth, use of examples), without restricting access. Periodic recalculation over rolling two-week window or after 20 meaningful interaction events; weighted aggregation with higher weight on recent interactions; diversity constraint to expose learners to alternative modalities.
subject_area: general (multi-subject; example deployment uses history -Byzantine history-; K-12 secondary curriculum in a Greek private school)
quantitative_results: System-level validation only (no learner-facing outcomes yet). Deployment completeness: ~12 courses instantiated, ~13 chapters each, ~780 learning sub-units in total, each packaged as a standardized multimodal unit (text, slides, video, podcast, quiz, chatbot). All core artifact types successfully generated and published to Moodle; Moodle plugin captured interaction events consistently across modalities. Learner-level evaluation is pre-registered for spring semester with expected sample of 60-100 secondary students (lower/upper secondary) in a private school in Agrinio, Greece.
limitations: No learner-level empirical results reported; learner evaluation is only pre-registered/scheduled for a future spring-semester classroom deployment; Evaluation is limited to a single private secondary school in Agrinio, Greece with small expected sample (60-100 students), restricting generalizability; Adaptation logic is intentionally coarse-grained, based on FSLSM learning-style dimensions rather than fine-grained performance/mastery-based difficulty adjustment; FSLSM/ILS learning-style models have been criticized for oversimplifying learner diversity and limited empirical support as fixed trait categories; Dependence on external GenAI services (e.g., NotebookLM pre-release) imposes daily generation quotas that required batching and staged uploads; AR asset creation remains a manual design process by subject matter experts, not automated; Model-agnostic/black-box implementation: specific LLM models, embedding model and exact versions are not disclosed, limiting reproducibility; Datasets (Moodle logs, learner data, generated artifacts) are not publicly available due to institutional and legal restrictions on personal data; AR access depends on device availability (mobile devices / compatible headsets); Claims about learner-level benefits are formulated as expected effects and testable hypotheses rather than confirmed outcomes
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_contribution: Design-and-deployment case study of PREPARE, an end-to-end quota-aware generative AI pipeline embedded in Moodle that transforms a single authoritative PDF textbook into a multimodal, accessible and adaptive learning unit (chapter summaries, structured notes, slide decks, formative quizzes, narrated video mini-lectures with captions, podcast-style audio, RAG-grounded chatbot tutor, and chapter-level AR activities), combined with a hybrid FSLSM/ILS + behavior-based learner model that drives non-prescriptive personalization (resource prioritization and recommendations) aligned with UDL, GDPR and EU AI Act requirements.
notes: Article is primarily a system/architecture and deployment contribution, not a model training/fine-tuning study: no fine-tuning, no quantitative learner-level metrics, no LLM benchmarking. LLM backbone is treated as model-agnostic; explicit generative tools mentioned are Google's NotebookLM (pre-release) and unspecified general-purpose LLM APIs as fallback; the exact LLM, version and parameter size are not disclosed. Embedding model is described only as a transformer-based dense sentence embedding model suitable for educational semantic similarity, without naming it. Personalization technology: Moodle custom local plugin + web service APIs; authoring pipeline implemented in Python 3.14. Ethical safeguards: GDPR-compliant data minimization, pseudonymization, supported-only chatbot policy, input filters for PII, 3-year retention, human-in-the-loop validation of generated artifacts (factual alignment, pedagogical coherence, clarity, cognitive level, accessibility compliance). Target population: secondary school students (gymnasium + lyceum), no higher-ed; multi-subject deployment with history (Byzantine history) shown as example. Evaluation instruments (pre/post questionnaires on perceived usefulness, ease of use, personalization accuracy, inclusivity/accessibility, trust, engagement, measured via 5-point Likert scales adapted from TAM and established engagement/trust scales) are finalized but not yet administered; planned analyses include descriptive statistics, Wilcoxon signed-rank tests and exploratory correlations. RQ coverage mapping: RQ1 (LLMs used -general-purpose LLM via NotebookLM and LLM APIs, model-agnostic-), RQ3 (output formats: summaries, notes, slides, MCQ quizzes, tutoring-dialogue via chatbot, narrated videos, podcasts, AR activities), RQ4 (personalization via FSLSM/ILS + behavior-based Moodle log profiling), RQ5 (secondary education, K-12), RQ6 (evaluation design: mixed-methods, Moodle learning analytics + pre/post questionnaires, human expert validation of artifacts, though no learner-level results yet), RQ7 (ethical/privacy considerations: GDPR, EU AI Act, human-in-the-loop validation, non-prescriptive personalization, accessibility-by-design/UDL), RQ8 (limitations enumerated above). RQ2 (fine-tuning technique) is not applicable because no fine-tuning is performed.

title: A Readability-Driven Prompting Framework for Accurate Grade-Specific EFL Narrative Creation
venue: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
dataset_name: Generated narratives corpus (RDP/CKCP/IBP outputs); CEFR-J reference list for lexical evaluation
dataset_size: 600 generated narratives (3 methods x 2 grades x 100 narratives); 30 Japanese beginner-level EFL student participants for human evaluation
dataset_domain: EFL narrative generation for 7th grade (A1-A2 CEFR) and 10th grade (B1-B2 CEFR)
personalization_mechanism: Grade-level conditioning (7th vs 10th grade) aligned to CEFR bands (A1-A2 vs B1-B2) via readability-driven prompting with Flesch-Kincaid and Dale-Chall target ranges, CEFR vocabulary constraints, and in-context exemplars at target grade level
subject_area: English as a Foreign Language (EFL) narrative reading/vocabulary acquisition
evaluation_metrics: Flesch-Kincaid Reading Ease (FRE); Dale-Chall readability score; CEFR lexical alignment (% A1-A2 or A1-B1 words); Unknown Words percentage; Average narrative word count; 5-point Likert survey (6 items: understandability, naturalness, interest, word difficulty, vocabulary learning, willingness to read more)
quantitative_results: Lexical (beginner/7th): RDP 86.17% A1-A2 (best), CKCP 84.84%, IBP 82.89%; unknown words: RDP 2%, CKCP 2%, IBP 3%; avg length: RDP 336, CKCP 287, IBP 263 words. Lexical (intermediate/10th): CKCP 90.41% A1-B1 (best), IBP 89.68%, RDP 88.43%; unknown words: CKCP 4.33%, IBP 5.0%, RDP 5.71%. Readability 7th grade: RDP FRE 77.28 (in 70-79 target), CKCP 69.18, IBP 68.63 (out of range); Dale-Chall RDP 6.80, CKCP 6.65 (in 6.0-6.9), IBP 7.05 (out). Readability 10th grade: RDP FRE 58.98 (only one in 50-59 target), CKCP 60.31, IBP 60.35 (out); Dale-Chall all fell outside 8.0-8.9 target (RDP 7.61, IBP 7.77, CKCP 7.99). Human evaluation (N=30, Likert 1-5): easy to understand 4.43, felt natural 4.4, interesting 4.26, words understandable 3.63, learned new word 4.23, want to read more 4.43.
limitations: RDP performance declines at intermediate level (10th grade/B1-B2), producing lower A1-B1 vocabulary alignment than CKCP/IBP and higher unknown-word rate; None of the methods achieved the 95% vocabulary coverage target for effective comprehension; Human evaluation limited to beginner-level materials only; intermediate-level narratives were not assessed with learners; Human evaluation focused exclusively on narratives generated by RDP (no comparison with IBP/CKCP in user study); Small sample size (30 participants) from a single university (Tokyo Denki University); Study focused on Japanese EFL learners only; generalizability to other learner backgrounds/languages/genres is untested; Inherent opacity and stochasticity of LLM generation makes deterministic control over readability difficult; No longitudinal or classroom-based evaluation of educational impact
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Proposes Readability-Driven Prompting (RDP), a few-shot prompting framework that integrates explicit readability theory (Flesch-Kincaid, Dale-Chall), CEFR-based vocabulary constraints, and grade-level narrative exemplars to guide LLMs (GPT-4o-mini) in producing grade-appropriate EFL narratives. Provides the first systematic experimental comparison of RDP with CEFR Keyword Constrained Prompting (CKCP) and Instruction-Based Prompting (IBP) on both readability metrics and lexical CEFR targeting at distinct grade bands (7th A1-A2, 10th B1-B2), showing RDP best aligns with Flesch-Kincaid targets and outperforms baselines at the beginner level while CKCP remains more stable at intermediate level.
notes: No fine-tuning is performed; the adaptation is purely prompt engineering with few-shot in-context exemplars. Base model: GPT-4o-mini accessed via default decoding (t=1). Readability targets: 7th grade FRE 70-80 and Dale-Chall 6.0-6.9; 10th grade FRE 50-60 and Dale-Chall 8.0-8.9. Lexical analysis pipeline uses SpaCy for tokenization/POS, NLTK for lemmatization, and compares against CEFR-J list. Human study deployed via a Unity-engine smartphone storytelling game; survey translated with ChatGPT 4.1 and proofread by native speaker. Funded by Tokyo Denki University Research Institute for Science and Technology (Grant Q25D-10).

title: AdaptLearn AI: A Generative AI Framework for Bidirectional Personalization in Education
venue: Int. Conf. Compu., Intell., Appl., CIACON
dataset_name: Synthetic cohort of 50 GPT-4 personas + curriculum/syllabus PDFs + pilot data (5 teachers, 15 students, 10 math assignments, 100 human-graded essays)
dataset_size: 50 synthetic personas; 100 essays (human vs auto-grading benchmark); pilot with 3 teachers + 10 math assignments and 5 high-school students over 1 week (reported; abstract mentions 5 teachers and 15 students)
dataset_domain: STEM (math) and general K-12 / high-school education; rubric generation for teachers
output_format: quiz-mcq; exercise; problem; feedback; lesson-plan; summary
personalization_mechanism: Bidirectional synchronization between teacher and student modules via Firebase Realtime Database: student module uses few-shot (5-shot) prompting adapter plus a dynamic difficulty scaler driven by an Error Pattern Analyzer that adjusts question complexity to individual error trends; teacher module uses RAG over syllabus PDFs with GPT-4 for rubric generation and feedback summarization; real-time GPT-4 performance tuning auto-calibrates without manual teacher input
subject_area: STEM (math primary); general curriculum rubric generation; humanities noted as untested
evaluation_metrics: nDCG@5 (RAG retrieval, AdaptLearn 0.89 vs baseline 0.72); BERTScore (F1) for output quality; API latency p50/p95 (210 ms p95 vs 450 ms baseline); Likert-scale surveys for user satisfaction (89% satisfaction); Problem appropriateness expert rating (4.2/5 vs 3.1/5); Teacher time saved (37% vs 12% baseline); Quiz score improvement (+22%); Lesson planning time reduction (47%); Pearson correlation adaptation frequency vs engagement (r=0.79); Regression beta for teacher-tool synergy (beta=0.78, p<0.01); Chi-square on non-STEM retrieval degradation (chi2=8.2, p<0.05); Hallucinated rubric items reduced by 41% vs pure GPT-4
quantitative_results: RAG pipeline nDCG@5 = 0.89 (vs 0.72 baseline, +23.6%); rubric hallucinations reduced 41% vs pure GPT-4; API p95 latency 210 ms vs 450 ms baseline; teacher time saved 37% vs 12% baseline; 47% reduction in lesson planning time; 22% improvement in quiz scores; 89% user satisfaction; problem appropriateness 4.2/5 vs 3.1/5; engagement-adaptation correlation r=0.79; synergistic effect beta=0.78 (p<0.01); non-STEM retrieval drop 31% (chi2=8.2, p<0.05); FERPA anonymization added ~110 ms latency.
limitations: Very small pilot sample (5 teachers and 15 students in abstract; 3 teachers + 5 students in detailed methodology); warrants expansion to 50+ learners; Short trial duration (1 week) insufficient to assess long-term knowledge retention; Subject generalization limited: current STEM focus may not translate to humanities; non-STEM retrieval accuracy drops 31% (chi2=8.2, p<0.05); Syllabus PDF heterogeneity degrades retrieval for non-STEM subjects; Real-time adaptation conflicts with FERPA anonymization, increasing latency by 110 ms; Excessive adaptation (>4.5/min) induces cognitive overload in students; Humanities integration untested; Large-scale validation remains future work; prototype is proof-of-concept
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_contribution: AdaptLearn AI: a bidirectional generative-AI framework that unifies teacher support (RAG + GPT-4 for rubric generation and feedback) and student personalization (few-shot adaptive STEM tutoring with dynamic difficulty scaling) via a Firebase real-time synchronization layer with FERPA-compliant anonymization, combining GPT-4-0613 and 4-bit quantized LLaMA-2-7B for a cost- and privacy-aware deployable classroom AI.
notes: Paper describes prompting/RAG + 4-bit quantized inference rather than an explicit LoRA/QLoRA training recipe: the authors call it '4-bit quantization' of LLaMA-2-7B served on Hugging Face TGI (mapped here to QLoRA-adjacent adaptation as the closest controlled-vocabulary entry, though no fine-tuning hyperparameters are reported). Fine-tuning details (epochs, rank, alpha, learning rate, batch size) are not provided. The abstract mentions fine-tuned LLMs but the methodology section only documents prompting, RAG, few-shot, and quantized deployment. Authors state code and datasets will be open-sourced, but no repository URL is given in the PDF. Sample-size discrepancy between abstract (5 teachers, 15 students) and methodology (3 teachers, 5 students) noted in the original paper. Evaluation combines automatic metrics (nDCG@5, BERTScore, latency, Locust stress tests), expert Likert reviews, and a one-week pilot user study. All 8 RQs of the mapping are at least partially addressed: base models (RQ1: GPT-4, LLaMA-2-7B), adaptation techniques (RQ2: RAG + few-shot + 4-bit quantization), datasets (RQ3: synthetic + pilot, not public), output formats (RQ4: rubrics, quizzes, lesson summaries, feedback), personalization (RQ5: bidirectional teacher-student), evaluation (RQ6: nDCG@5, BERTScore, Likert, latency, quiz gains), limitations (RQ7: sample, duration, humanities, privacy-latency trade-off), reproducibility (RQ8: announced open-source release, FERPA compliance, but no artifacts confirmed).

title: Building Intelligent Educational Agents: A Scalable LLM-Based Framework for Assessment Generation
venue: Procedia Comput. Sci.
dataset_name: ClassQuiz internal dataset (merged CSV from ClassQuiz platform/API and DB scraping)
dataset_size: unknown
dataset_domain: educational assessments (quiz questions and answer options)
personalization_mechanism: LLM chain pipeline (Algorithm 2) that takes a Student Profile P and Context C, extracts learning goals and priorities, selects a template based on goal taxonomy, and constructs a prompt (T + C + P) to generate customized assessments. RAG retrieves semantically similar educational content via cosine similarity over embeddings stored in a scalable vector DB. The system segments learners based on their characteristics and provides tailored recommendations.
subject_area: general
quantitative_results: No quantitative evaluation results reported. The paper only provides a qualitative visual comparison (Figure 2) between the fine-tuned model and the non-fine-tuned model on the same input, showing that the fine-tuned model produces a properly structured exercise with subject-pronoun fill-in-the-blank questions while the non-fine-tuned model does not. No accuracy, BLEU, ROUGE, human ratings or similar numerical metrics are provided.
limitations: No quantitative evaluation of the generated assessments (only a qualitative example); Dataset incomplete due to lack of accessible web elements, requiring manual scraping via ClassQuiz API; System does not support non-textual data (images, videos); Hallucinations may occur with ambiguous prompts or ambiguous RAG data; Evaluation is done by development team and then reviewed by teachers; no automated judging agent yet implemented; No real-time adaptation based on student performance data (mentioned as future work); Dataset and code not released
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ7
key_contribution: Presents a scalable LLM-based framework integrated into the ClassQuiz platform that combines fine-tuning (SFT on conversational message format), prompt engineering, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG with embeddings + vector DB) to automatically generate personalized, context-aware educational assessments (quizzes, exercises) for children/K-12 online learning, with distributed processing (Spark, Docker) and API integration for production deployment.
notes: Proceedings paper (KES 2025, Procedia Computer Science 270, pp. 4075-4084). The base LLM family is referred to only as 'GPT/LLM' in Figure 1 and generically as 'GPT variants' and 'BERT' in the text; no specific model name (e.g., GPT-3.5, GPT-4) or parameter size is disclosed. No fine-tuning hyperparameters (epochs, batch size, learning rate, LoRA rank/alpha, quantization) are reported; only a generic training algorithm with cross-entropy loss is shown. Dataset size, language, and exact composition are not disclosed. Architecture uses LangChain + LangGraph (agents/tools), OpenAI embeddings, Spark for preprocessing, a scalable vector database (Docker), and a NextJS/ReactJS frontend. Evaluation is purely qualitative (side-by-side example image of fine-tuned vs non-fine-tuned outputs); no formal human-expert study, no automatic metrics, no LLM-as-judge implemented (the authors mention an LLM-judge evaluation agent as future work). The moderate-discrepancy hypothesis is cited as justification for age-appropriate content, suggesting K-12/children as target educational level. Keywords: Large Language Models; Educational Technology; Fine-tuning; Prompt Engineering; Retrieval Augmented Generation; NLP.

title: Agentic AI Quiz-Based Learning System: Enhancing MCQ Generation via Long-Context Cached Retrieval-Augmented Generation
venue: Proc. Front. Educ. Conf. FIE
llm_base: Gemini 1.5 Flash 1.5 Flash (unknown); GPT-4o 4o (unknown); Claude Sonnet 3.7 3.7 Sonnet (unknown); Grok-3 3 (Think mode) (unknown)
dataset_name: FAANG-level AI Interview MCQ dataset (custom)
dataset_size: 4,404 MCQs (3,523 train / 881 test; 150-question evaluation subset)
dataset_domain: Artificial Intelligence / FAANG-level AI interview topics (Machine Learning, Generative AI, Neural Networks, algorithms, recommender systems)
personalization_mechanism: Dynamic prompt engineering guided by metadata (difficulty level: easy/medium/tough) and adapted using the user's chat history. Implicit learner profiles based on prior knowledge, cognitive patterns, learning pace and language proficiency.
subject_area: Engineering education / Computer Science / Artificial Intelligence (AI interview preparation)
evaluation_metrics: ExactMatch (Accuracy); Faithfulness; Hallucination; ExplanationSimilarity; ContextRecall; Uncertainty (Certainty); BERTScore; BLEU; ROUGE; McNemar's test; Wilson score 95% CI; Cohen's d
quantitative_results: Gemini 1.5 Flash accuracy progression across 4 RAG configurations on 150-question benchmark: Raw model 78.00%; +Context Chunks 84.00%; Chunks+Cache (100k) 89.33%; Long-Context Chunks+Cache (1M) 93.33%. Best config (Gemini, 1M+cache) metric scores: ExactMatch 0.9333, Faithfulness 0.8960, ExplanationSimilarity 0.8153, ContextRecall 0.8410, BERTScore 0.8259, ROUGE 0.1655, BLEU 0.0372. Model comparison on 150 MCQs under matched RAG configs: GPT-4o improved from 89.33% to 94.00% (ExactMatch 0.9400, Faithfulness 0.9200); Claude Sonnet 3.7 rose from 59.33% to 93.33% (Certainty 0.9000, Non-Hallucination 0.9150). McNemar's test confirmed p<0.05 across configurations; Cohen's d effect sizes 0.2-0.3 (small-to-medium). Response time reduced from 85s to 21s on 4,404-question dataset via caching. Evaluation time reduced from 4.5 hours to 48 minutes via parallel scoring (18.36s to 3.28s per query).
limitations: Long-context processing is computationally intensive; Cached content may become outdated without periodic updates; Dataset biases can affect question relevance and fairness; Scalability constrained by token limits; Adapting the system to non-AI domains requires substantial domain-specific data; 150-question evaluation may not fully capture topic diversity; Alignment with varied curricula and accessibility for diverse learners remains challenging; Study did not directly measure student engagement or attrition (no human subjects validation); Focused only on answer selection and explanation, not on full MCQ generation / distractor plausibility
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Presents a modular Agentic AI quiz-based learning system that integrates long-context LLMs (Gemini 1.5 Flash, 1M tokens), context caching, and HNSW-indexed RAG to generate and evaluate high-quality MCQs for FAANG-level AI interview preparation, achieving 93.33% answer accuracy and demonstrating that long-context caching + agentic orchestration outperforms traditional short-context RAG without any fine-tuning.
notes: GitHub repo: https://github.com/Devu1502/FIE2025AgenticAIQuiz. Dual agentic pipeline: Agentic AI System I (Parsing, Chunking & Storage, Embedding & Indexing, Verification agents) builds the vector DB; Agentic AI System II (Retriever, Selector, Response, Evaluator agents) generates and scores answers. Tooling (Table VI): GCP, Groq (Grok-3 Think mode) and Claude Sonnet 3.7 / Gemini 1.5 Flash / GPT-4o for MCQ generation, CrewAI (FileReadTool, DirectoryReadTool), DuplicateCheckerTool (Pydantic), Vectara (REST-based) for vector retrieval with HNSW 768-dim hybrid lexical-semantic indexing, Google text-embedding-005 for embeddings. Dataset curated from 850 web-crawled MCQs (SanFoundry, Simplilearn, InterviewBit, HackerRank, Kaggle) then expanded to 4,404 via Grok-3 Think mode and verified by a human expert. Published at IEEE Frontiers in Education Conference (FIE) 2025, DOI 10.1109/FIE63693.2025.11328160.

title: LLM-based Maritime Training Feedback System: Implementing RAG-Enhanced Assessment Analysis with STCW Compliance
venue: TransNav
dataset_name: STCW competencies + assessment questions + student feedback forms (custom maritime corpus)
dataset_size: unknown
dataset_domain: Maritime education / STCW compliance
personalization_mechanism: Three prompt templates (zero-shot, few-shot, structured) plus answer diagnosis graph tailored per assessment question; RAG retrieves question-specific STCW context. No personalization by individual learner profile/history.
subject_area: Maritime education and training (STCW compliance)
evaluation_metrics: response time (mean/median/std/min/max, % within 15s); STCW concept coverage (%); memory footprint (GB); qualitative feedback quality assessment
quantitative_results: Structured templates reached 85% STCW concept coverage vs few-shot 78% and zero-shot 65% (20 pp gap structured vs zero-shot). MCQ response times: min 6.71s, max 14.32s, mean 9.05s, median 7.52s, SD 3.07s, 100% within 15s. MCQ by template: zero-shot mean 8.32s, few-shot 10.56s, structured 11.98s. Essay response times by template: Analysis mean 11.90s, Detailed 15.00s, Concise 8.05s, Interactive 15.00s. Comparative: zero-shot 8.2s/65%, few-shot 12.1s/73%, RAG-enhanced 15.0s/85%. Feedback generation time reduced 73% vs manual assessment. QLoRA reduced memory from >14GB to ~4GB. Prior context coefficients cited: technological proficiency beta=0.457 p<0.001; institutional readiness beta=0.341 p<0.001.
limitations: No comparison with human instructor ratings in this study (left as future work); Model struggles with specialized maritime terminology with double meanings (e.g., overhead, close quarters); Initial RAG retrieval inconsistent; required hybrid semantic+keyword search and query augmentation; Trade-off between response quality and speed; high-precision settings exceeded 20s; Cannot match human instructors for nuanced responses or personalization based on learning history; Out-of-memory errors on 24GB GPU before 4-bit quantization; No longitudinal adoption study; Small RQ set (only two) and limited empirical user evaluation
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ7
key_contribution: Implementation and evaluation of a RAG-enhanced, QLoRA-optimized Mistral-7B feedback system for STCW-compliant maritime assessment that runs on consumer-grade hardware (~4GB), achieving 85% STCW concept coverage and sub-15s response times, with structured prompt templates shown to outperform zero/few-shot for regulatory alignment.
notes: Design science research methodology (Hevner) in three phases. RAG stack: FAISS + HuggingFace all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings. Feedback generator also implements an answer diagnosis graph marking key STCW concepts as PRESENT/missing and producing improvement suggestions. Two explicit RQs (RQ1 generation of STCW-compliant feedback via RAG; RQ2 performance benchmarks for local LLM deployment) map to mapping RQs on techniques, formats and evaluation. Personalization is template/prompt-level rather than learner-profile-level (hence 'partial'). No fine-tuning dataset details beyond the STCW corpus; QLoRA is applied but training hyperparameters (epochs, batch size, learning rate) are not reported.

title: EduCraft: A System for Generating Pedagogical Lecture Scripts from Long-Context Multimodal Presentations
venue: CIKM - Proc. ACM Int. Conf. Info. Knowl. Manage.
llm_base: Claude 3.7 Sonnet 3.7 (unknown); Qwen2.5-VL 2.5 (72B); GPT-4o 2024-11-20 (unknown); DeepSeek-V3 V3 (unknown); GPT-4o-mini (unknown)
dataset_name: MAIC platform university presentations (20 presentations: 10 English, 10 Chinese; 320 slides total; avg 13.6 slides/presentation; 272 slides for automated eval)
dataset_size: 20 presentations / ~320 slides
dataset_domain: University-level multidisciplinary (Humanities, Social Sciences, STEM including CS, engineering, physical sciences)
personalization_mechanism: Configurable model selection, generation style and RAG activation via API; modular workflows (VLM vs Caption+LLM) and optional RAG for domain/informational tailoring. No per-student personalization implemented.
subject_area: Multidisciplinary (Humanities, Social Sciences, STEM: CS, engineering, physical sciences)
evaluation_metrics: Human comparative ranking: Consistency, Readability, Coherence (max 3.0); Slide-level: Content Relevance (CR), Expressive Clarity (EC), Logical Structure (LS), Audience Engagement (AE); Global-level: Narrative Flow (NF), Information Hierarchy (IH), Conceptual Integration (CI), Thematic Consistency (TC), Cross-referencing (CRef); Aggregates: Avg Slide Score (Avg-S), Global Score (Glo), Combined Score (Comb)
quantitative_results: Human evaluation (avg rank, max 3.0): EduCraft 2.41 vs Iterative Baseline 1.47 vs Teacher Refined 2.12. Per-criterion (EduCraft/Iter/Refined): Consistency 2.44/1.51/2.04; Readability 2.37/1.48/2.16; Coherence 2.44/1.31/2.25. Automated (GPT-4o 2024-11-20) Combined Score: EduCraft VLM 3.86 vs Direct Prompt 3.78 vs Iterative 3.70; with Qwen2.5-VL-72B: 3.82 vs 3.73 vs 3.75. Caption+LLM workflow: DeepSeek-V3 Comb 3.89; Qwen2.5-72B Comb 3.74. RAG impact: slight decrease (Qwen2.5-VL-72B -0.07; GPT-4o -0.03). Ablation (Comb drop from 3.86): w/o Narrative Style -0.06; w/o Pedagogical Mapping -0.05; w/o Multimodal Grounding -0.11.
limitations: RAG integration can harm Expressive Clarity and Narrative Flow by introducing overly technical or tangential information (context-dependent trade-off); RAG led to slight overall Combined Score decrease, so it is offered as optional rather than default; Evaluation limited to 20 presentations (10 EN + 10 CH) from the MAIC platform, a modest scale; Small annotator pool (10 graduate students) for human evaluation; No per-student personalization implemented (only student-profile personalization listed as future work); Interactive refinement with educators listed as future work (not implemented)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: EduCraft: a modular system for Lecture Script Generation (LSG) from long-context multimodal slide presentations, combining instruction-guided VLM and Caption+LLM workflows with optional RAG, validated via human and automated evaluation showing scripts outperform iterative baselines and teacher-refined versions on consistency, readability and coherence.
