notes: No fine-tuning performed; system is prompting/orchestration over off-the-shelf VLMs/LLMs (Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4o, Qwen2.5-VL-72B, DeepSeek-V3, GPT-4o-mini) with RAG over Wikipedia-derived KB. Code open-sourced at https://github.com/wyuc/EduCraft. Output type is a pedagogical lecture script (oral-delivery narrative per slide), which is the closest controlled-vocab match is 'explanation' / 'lesson-plan'. Personalization is at system/configuration level (model, style, RAG), not per-student. Automated global coherence judged by Claude 3.7 Sonnet (LLM-as-judge). CIKM'25 paper (doi: 10.1145/3746252.3761578).

title: EduAgent: A Multimodal Chatbot Framework for Enhancing Interactive Learning in Electrical and Electronics Engineering Education
venue: International Journal of Modern Education and Computer Science
adaptation_technique: LoRA; QLoRA; SFT; prompting; few-shot; RAG
dataset_name: EduAgent conversational dataset + EEE image dataset
dataset_size: 596 four-turn conversations (547 training / 49 testing; 2188 QA pairs; 519 training / 28 validation examples) + 118 EEE images
dataset_domain: Electrical and Electronics Engineering (EEE) education; 10 topics: Electronic Components, Power Electronics, Amplifiers and Oscillators, Digital Electronics, Transistors, Rectification and Diode Circuits, Electronic Instruments, Miscellaneous Topics, Logic Gates, Adders
personalization_mechanism: Pedagogically enhanced prompts enforce step-by-step explanations, mention prior knowledge required, penalize verbose/shallow answers; multimodal retrieval of related images from knowledge base (RAG) adapts response with visual context; currently no per-learner adaptation (authors note this as future work)
subject_area: Electrical and Electronics Engineering (EEE)
evaluation_metrics: BERTScore (F1); BLEU (sacrebleu); correctness (1-5); completeness (1-5); conciseness (1-5); engagement (1-5); relevance (image, 1-5); understandability (image, 1-5); win-rate (image retrieval); Krippendorff's alpha (inter-rater)
quantitative_results: Automatic (BERTScore/BLEU across 4 turns): Llama-3.1-8B best via One-Shot/Three-Shot prompting (e.g., turn 4: One-Shot BERT=0.7453, BLEU=29.37; Three-Shot BERT=0.7426); fine-tuning BERT=0.7293 / BLEU=31.65 at turn 4. Llama-3.2-3B best via Three-Shot (turn 3: BERT=0.7183, BLEU=28.81); fine-tuning underperforms (turn 3: BERT=0.7056, BLEU=23.93). Few-shot prompting outperformed fine-tuning in most cases. Image retrieval win-rate: TF-IDF=0.82, BM25-stopwords-removed=0.76, BM25=0.75. Human evaluation (20 raters, 45 conversations): Llama-3.1-8B few-shot highest medians on correctness, completeness, conciseness, engagement vs GPT-4o and Llama-3.1-8B fine-tuned. Dataset quality Krippendorff alpha: correctness=0.671, completeness=0.536, conciseness=0.569. Live evaluation (8 learners): Llama-3.1-8B three-shot consistently scored 4.0-5.0 on all metrics, outperforming GPT-4o baseline.
limitations: Dataset limited to 10 foundational EEE topics; does not cover advanced areas; Small image database (only 118 images) limits retrieval performance; No individualization per learner (no adaptation to user interests, strengths, weaknesses, learnability); LLMs still fall behind on mathematical calculations / math-heavy content; Fine-tuning underperformed few-shot prompting, suggesting limited benefit of SFT in conversational scenarios with this dataset size; Subjectivity of human metrics yielded only moderate inter-rater agreement (alpha 0.54-0.67); Assumes all learners benefit from short, stepwise, prerequisite-first responses
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: EduAgent, a multimodal chatbot framework for Electrical and Electronics Engineering education combining fine-tuned open-source LLMs (Llama-3.1-8B and Llama-3.2-3B via LoRA/QLoRA with Unsloth), iterative prompt engineering with few-shot in-context learning, and a BM25/TF-IDF retrieval-augmented image knowledge base; contributes a synthetic 596-conversation EEE dataset and 118-image corpus, and empirically shows that few-shot prompting outperforms fine-tuning for pedagogical response generation in small-data conversational scenarios.
notes: Dataset generation used GPT-4o as answering agent and questioning agent with iterative prompt refinement to mitigate harmful intent and shallowness bias. Llama-3.1-8B used to generate topic descriptions; InternLM used to generate image explanations; BM25 (rank_bm25) used for QA retrieval in few-shot example selection. GitHub: https://github.com/hyadess/EduAgent. Published in IJMECS Vol 18(2026) Issue 1, DOI 10.5815/ijmecs.2026.01.07.

title: Personalized Assessment in Education: Enhancing MCQ Generation Using Advanced AI and Contextual Learning Models
venue: Int. Conf. Circuits, Controls Commun., CCUBE
dataset_name: Curated MCQ knowledge base (instructor-designed + student-created MCQs)
dataset_size: unknown (sample of 11 MCQs shown in Table 1; ratio 5 instructor:1 student)
dataset_domain: Computer Science / general education (pathfinding, data structures, networking, physics, geography, chemistry, electronics)
personalization_mechanism: Adaptive feedback loop: after students interact with generated MCQs, performance data and feedback adjust difficulty level of subsequent questions, refine distractors to address misconceptions, and enhance alignment with learning outcomes. Difficulty categorized via Bloom's taxonomy (easy/hard).
subject_area: Computer Science and general STEM (math-based, concept-based, coding-based questions)
evaluation_metrics: Grammatical Fluidity; Answerability; Diversity; Complexity; Relevance
quantitative_results: Human evaluation (3 subject-matter experts), Math-Based: Gram.Fluidity 4.5, Answerability 3.8, Diversity 3.7, Complexity 3.6, Relevance 4.7; Concept-Based: 4.8/4.5/4.0/4.2/4.9; Coding-Based: 4.6/4.3/4.1/4.0/4.8. Machine evaluation, Math-Based: 4.6/4.1/3.8/3.9/4.8; Concept-Based: 4.9/4.7/4.2/4.4/4.9; Coding-Based: 4.7/4.5/4.3/4.1/4.9. Scores on a 5-point scale. Highest performance in Concept-Based and Coding-Based MCQs; Math-Based weakest in Answerability and Complexity.
limitations: Weak performance on Math-Based MCQs, especially in answerability and complexity; Limited diversity and cognitive complexity of generated questions; No fine-tuning: relies only on prompt engineering and RAG over a small curated knowledge base; Dataset size and composition not specified quantitatively; Validation mechanisms need strengthening for math-heavy domains; Narrow domain focus required for better results; No detailed reproducibility artifacts (code, dataset, models not released); Human evaluation limited to 3 subject-matter experts
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: PAMGen framework: automated personalized MCQ generation combining GPT-4, Retrieval-Augmented Generation over a dual-source knowledge base (instructor-designed + student-created MCQs), and prompt engineering techniques (Chain-of-Thought reasoning and Self-Refine prompting), with an adaptive feedback loop for difficulty and distractor calibration.
notes: The paper refers to Self-Refine prompting (Madaan et al. 2023) as an iterative self-critique mechanism, classified here under 'prompting' and 'chain-of-thought' within the controlled vocabulary; the self-refine aspect does not map to any single term. No model training or fine-tuning is performed; PAMGen is a prompt+RAG pipeline on top of GPT-4. Dataset is a curated MCQ bank, not a training corpus. Evaluation compares 3 human experts vs LLM-as-judge ('advanced language models') on 5-point scale across 3 question types. Adaptive personalization is described conceptually (feedback loop) but not empirically validated with students in this paper. Venue: 4th Int. Conf. on Circuits, Controls and Communication (CCUBE 2025), IEEE, DOI: 10.1109/CCUBE66458.2025.11340281.

title: Research on the construction and application of intelligent tutoring system for english teaching based on generative pre-training model
venue: Systems and Soft Computing
dataset_name: Daily Mail, XSum, CNN/Daily Mail; BNC and COCA corpora mentioned as public English corpora; learner data from online platforms, offline tests and questionnaires
dataset_size: unknown
dataset_domain: English language teaching / English text summarization
personalization_mechanism: Learner profiles built from multi-channel data (English proficiency test scores, learning history, self-reported preferences, online platform records, offline test results, questionnaires). System dynamically adjusts exercise difficulty and type based on learner profile and practice results.
subject_area: English as a Foreign Language (EFL)
evaluation_metrics: ROUGE-1; ROUGE-2; ROUGE-L; MAE; RMSE; learner progress (listening, speaking, reading, writing); learning satisfaction; engagement; motivation
quantitative_results: Proposed model on Daily Mail: ROUGE-1=51.852, ROUGE-2=24.396, ROUGE-L=49.176 (vs BART 51.408/24.228/48.648, Pegasus 51.66/24.288/48.804, T5 50.844/23.964/47.64, FASUM 46.56/20.676/42.84, BART+TA 51.432/23.124/48.192); improvements of +0.16/+0.09/+0.20 over best baseline. UNILM-LSTM on CNN/DailyMail: ROUGE-1=43.45%, ROUGE-2=20.72%, ROUGE-L=40.62% (+0.31%/+0.70%/+0.73% over UNILMv2). Application results: avg +30% progress in English listening/speaking/reading/writing, +40% learning satisfaction, reading comprehension improved ~35%, writing skills ~30%, speaking fluency/accuracy ~25%, engagement +40%, motivation +30%.
limitations: English data for special fields may not be enough in general corpus; Student learning data difficult to cover all groups; Lag in data updating, difficult to reflect emerging language phenomena and new learning needs in timely manner; Differences in educational resources (lack of hardware in some areas); Differences in students' basic foundations make it difficult for weak-foundation students to adapt to preset paths; Differences in cultural backgrounds limit universal applicability; Large-scale training is very time-consuming; training time grows more than linearly with data volume; Real-time feedback requires frequent training of small batches which increases system burden; Fine-tuning results did not reach the level of the original paper due to computing power limitations
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Construction and application of an intelligent tutoring system for English teaching based on generative pre-trained models (UniLM, BERT, BART variants), integrating learner portraits from multi-channel data to automatically generate personalized exercises, dynamically adjust difficulty, and provide instant feedback on grammar, vocabulary and coherence. Introduces semantic perceptual attention and knowledge graph modules, and a UNILM-LSTM fusion that improves abstract generation quality on standard benchmarks while yielding measurable learning gains (+30% across four skills, +40% satisfaction) versus traditional teaching.
notes: The paper is conceptually oriented and lacks many rigorous implementation details: no specific LLM parameter sizes, no fine-tuning hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, epochs, optimizer), no clearly defined educational dataset, no human-expert or student-cohort evaluation protocol description (sample size, control group, statistical tests). The reported +30%/+40% improvements are stated without methodological detail. Some figures (Fig. 1 on speaker anonymization, Fig. 4 on crime prediction, Fig. 5 DomainNet/Office-31, Fig. 6 EXATAS/basted, Fig. 7 PARSeq/ABINet, Fig. 8 AE/AAE SuperEncoder qubits, Fig. 9 CGU/MSMT17/Market/Duke, Fig. 10 EEGVIT) appear unrelated to English teaching and suggest figure/caption inconsistencies or reuse from other works. Base models are generative pre-trained (GPT-family mentioned, UniLM used as central example, BART, BERT, Pegasus, T5 as comparison). Adaptation combines pre-training on large English corpus + supervised fine-tuning on downstream English tasks (text summarization, classification, translation, sentiment analysis). Covers RQ1 (base LLMs), RQ2 (SFT/pre-training), RQ3 (text outputs: summaries, exercises, feedback), RQ4 (personalization via learner profiles), RQ5 (EFL, language-learning), RQ6 (ROUGE + self-reported gains). Does not clearly address RQ7 (limited reproducibility) or RQ8 (ethics/biases beyond cultural mention).

title: Intelligent teaching design assistant for primary mathematics: A large language model-driven framework with retrieval-augmented generation and problem-chain pedagogy
venue: International Electronic Journal of Mathematics Education
llm_base: unspecified generic LLM (accessed via Coze platform)
dataset_name: Multi-dimensional knowledge bases: Curriculum Knowledge Base, Teaching Strategy Base, Student Learning Style Base (primary math, fifth grade, unit 'Multiplying Decimals by Whole Numbers')
dataset_size: unspecified (knowledge fragments ingested via Coze splitter); 34 teachers x 2 lesson plans evaluated
dataset_domain: primary school mathematics (fifth grade, 11-12 years old)
personalization_mechanism: Student profile knowledge base and student simulator agent; teacher inputs knowledge points and student learning information processed by Teaching Analysis Module; problem-chain pedagogy adapts scaffolding but no per-learner profile adaptation
subject_area: mathematics
evaluation_metrics: Likert 8-point lesson-plan rubric across 10 dimensions / 19 items (teaching objectives, situation/textbook analysis, problem chain, activities, knowledge content, methods/strategies, evaluation, usability, range, overall); Paired-sample t-tests (Task 1 generic LLM vs Task 2 Intelligent Teaching Design Assistant); Inter-rater reliability: ICC(C,1)=0.885, ICC(C,K)=0.958; Cronbach alpha = 0.881 (usage questionnaire, 14 items, 5-point Likert: perceived ease of use, perceived usefulness, technology acceptance, satisfaction); Back-end platform analytics (active users, dialogues, session duration, retention)
quantitative_results: N=34 fifth-grade math teachers; 3 expert raters. Overall lesson-plan score Task 2 (assistant) M=5.83 vs Task 1 (generic LLM) M=3.67, t=-8.006, p<0.01. All 10 dimensions significantly higher for Task 2 (all p<0.01): teaching objectives 6.26 vs 4.19 (t=-5.896); situation/textbook analysis 7.22 vs 4.79 (t=-6.328); problem chain 6.05 vs 3.58 (t=-8.938); teaching activities 5.69 vs 3.60 (t=-10.218); knowledge content 4.40 vs 3.29 (t=-7.916); methods/strategies 5.99 vs 4.69 (t=-6.455); evaluation 5.01 vs 3.58 (t=-5.820); availability 6.00 vs 3.00 (t=-9.781); knowledge scope 5.50 vs 3.84 (t=-5.119). Usage survey (N=34): perceived ease of use M=4.72 (SD=0.39), perceived usefulness avg 4.47, technology acceptance 3.94-4.35, satisfaction M=4.29. Platform: 39 registered users, 914 dialogues, avg 10.2 dialogues/user, 8.8 min avg duration, 3.7 rounds/session, 3:55 min/session.
limitations: Small sample size (N=34 teachers) limits generalizability; Single subject and grade level (fifth-grade primary mathematics, 'Multiplying Decimals by Whole Numbers'); No formal statistical assumption checks for paired t-tests (normality, homogeneity of variances) nor effect sizes reported; Weak support for interdisciplinary knowledge integration (M=4.39 knowledge content); Evaluation templates overemphasize summative assessment, neglect formative strategies (M=5.02 teaching evaluation); Dependency on manually pre-constructed knowledge bases that require ongoing updates to align with curriculum reforms; Risk of teacher overreliance on AI suggestions dampening creative exploration; High technical threshold for frontline teachers to apply LLMs effectively; Variability in technology acceptance (SD up to 0.82) suggests self-efficacy influences adoption
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes an Intelligent Teaching Design Assistant for primary mathematics built on the Coze platform, integrating a generic LLM with retrieval-augmented generation over three knowledge bases (curriculum standards, teaching strategies, student profiles) and a multi-agent architecture (process planner + student simulator) grounded in socio-constructivist theory, cognitive load theory and problem-chain pedagogy. A quasi-experiment with 34 fifth-grade math teachers shows the assistant significantly outperforms a generic LLM in lesson-plan quality across all 10 evaluation dimensions, with high teacher ease-of-use, usefulness and satisfaction.
notes: No fine-tuning is performed: adaptation is exclusively via RAG (Coze splitter/embedding of knowledge fragments) + structured prompting within a multi-agent chatbot workflow. The base LLM is not named (described generically as 'general-purpose large language model' accessed via web interface for control Task 1 and as the backbone of the Coze agent for Task 2). Quasi-experimental within-subjects design (Task 1 baseline generic LLM vs Task 2 assistant, same teachers). Output is a Word-exportable lesson plan; problem-chain pedagogy scaffolds teacher design but the system generates lesson plans, not per-student materials. Ethics approval waived by Anhui Normal University ethics committee (Doc AHNU-ERB-2024-0105). Data available on request; code/model not released.

title: RAG Technology to Develop and Evaluate an Automatic Generation System for Similar Problems with Adjustable Difficulty
venue: Proc. - Int. Conf. Inf. Technol., InCIT
dataset_name: Background Knowledge DB + Conforming Problems and Subjective Evaluations DB (built from first-year university information security course materials; 15 source problems used in experiments)
dataset_size: 15 source problems x 7 difficulty levels = 105 combinations; 315 analogous problems generated in Preliminary Experiment 2
dataset_language: Japanese/English (unspecified; course at University of Electro-Communications, Japan)
dataset_domain: higher-education information security / information science (first-year university introductory course)
personalization_mechanism: User provides a 7-point subjective difficulty evaluation of a preexisting problem; system iteratively collects 'conforming problems' (those whose generated difficulty matches user judgment) and feeds them as few-shot examples to the LLM via RAG to calibrate subsequent generations to the user's subjective sense of difficulty
subject_area: Information security / information science (introductory university course)
evaluation_metrics: Conforming problem rate (difficulty-agreement rate); Problem validity rate (Correct Answers, Clarity, Format criteria); Quality scores on 5-point scale (Similarity, Adherence to Scope, Naturalness, Overall quality); Success rate for scope adherence and naturalness
quantitative_results: Preliminary Exp 2: conforming problem rate rose from 38.1% (diagnostic) to 80.3% (exercise); problem validity 90.5% (285/315); quality on 5-point scale: similarity 4.6, adherence to scope 5.0, overall 4.5. Main Exp (3 students): conforming rate improved from 38.1% to 81.0%. Preliminary Exp 1: optimal cosine similarity threshold = 0.5; max validity 92.4% at threshold 0.5; scope and naturalness 100% across all thresholds; thresholds 0.1-0.2 exceeded token limit; thresholds 0.7+ extracted no chunks.
limitations: Small number of participants (3 students in main experiment) limits generalizability; No comparison against baseline methods (e.g., LLM without RAG); Evaluation focused only on conformity and validity rates; no learner performance improvement or instructor time-savings measured; Computational cost and scalability not addressed; Difficulty of generating problems at extreme difficulty levels (e.g., 'extremely difficult'), some source problems have limited potential for increased difficulty; Primary cause of invalidity: LLM generates three correct answers instead of required two (over-interpretation of instructions)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Proposes a RAG-based automatic analogous-problem generation system with adjustable difficulty that introduces a feedback loop: a diagnostic phase collects user subjective difficulty evaluations (7-point scale), stores 'conforming problems' in a DB, and feeds them as few-shot examples to the LLM in a problem-generation phase. Optimizes RAG cosine similarity threshold (0.5) and extracts metainformation (Purpose/Viewpoint/Knowledge) from source problems to improve quality. Achieves 80-81% difficulty-agreement rate and >90% problem validity without fine-tuning.
notes: No fine-tuning performed; adaptation is purely via RAG + structured prompting + few-shot examples built from user-validated conforming problems. System implemented in Python/Streamlit using OpenAI Chat Completions API with GPT-4.1 and text-embedding-3-large. Background knowledge chunked at item/section level and stored as JSON with text-vector pairs. Gemini-2.5-pro was used only for translation/revision of the paper text (disclosed). Funded by JSPS KAKENHI JP24K03051. RQ coverage mapping: RQ1 (LLMs for educational content generation, problem generation), RQ2 (adaptation techniques, RAG/few-shot), RQ3 (output formats, MCQ problems), RQ4 (personalization, subjective difficulty calibration per user), RQ5 (evaluation, human + LLM-judge + ablation on similarity threshold), RQ6 (educational level, higher ed).

title: Optimization and Application of Generative AI Algorithm Based on Transformer Architecture in Adaptive Learning
venue: Information (Switzerland)
dataset_name: Self-constructed Chinese educational corpus (~124,000 structured training samples; final corpus 152,347 text fragments; originally ~150,000 text fragments)
dataset_size: ~124,000 instruction-input-output triples (corpus: 152,347 text samples)
dataset_language: Chinese (covers mathematics, physics, and English subjects)
dataset_domain: education (junior high and senior high school; mathematics, physics, English)
output_format: exercise; explanation; quiz-short-answer; feedback; tutoring-dialogue; problem
personalization_mechanism: Dynamic prompt construction mechanism combining a Transformer-based behavior encoder (3-layer Transformer, hidden dim 768) that encodes student behavioral features (previous answers, wrong answer types, learning time/frequency, feedback) into latent state vectors, plus a knowledge graph embedding for knowledge point retrieval, and a wrong-question guidance mechanism. The dynamic prompt template has three modules: 'Learner status description', 'Current knowledge point summary', and 'Task instruction prompt', with hierarchical tagging, cache mechanism and incremental encoding for low-latency inference.
subject_area: mathematics, physics, English (language learning, STEM)
evaluation_metrics: BLEU; ROUGE-L; Likert 5-point (content relevance, language adaptability, individual targeting, teaching effectiveness); ICC (inter-rater reliability); Pearson correlation (behavior latent state vs measured knowledge mastery); response time (s); GPU resource utilization (%); throughput (samples/s); pre/post-test scores (controlled experiment); ANCOVA (F, p, partial eta^2); paired t-test, Cohen's d
quantitative_results: Complete model: BLEU=0.91, ROUGE-L=0.92. Ablation (Table 5): LLaMA-2-13B base BLEU=0.73/ROUGE-L=0.77; +LoRA 0.81/0.84; +LoRA+instruction FT 0.86/0.88; LLaMA-7B (same process) 0.85/0.87; Complete model 0.91/0.92. LoRA rank comparison (20 epochs): r=4 BLEU mean=0.846 (SD=0.021), ROUGE-L mean=0.887 (SD=0.018); r=8 BLEU mean=0.912 (SD=0.015), ROUGE-L mean=0.913 (SD=0.012); paired t-tests BLEU t(19)=9.874 p<0.001 d=2.21; ROUGE-L t(19)=7.632 p<0.001 d=1.71. Inter-rater ICC=0.84 overall (0.82-0.86 per dimension). Behavior latent vector correlation with knowledge mastery: overall r=0.76 (Mathematics 0.78, Physics 0.73); 90.5% of dimensions significantly relevant. Multi-task scores 0.80-0.88 (Concept explanation 0.88, Knowledge point summary 0.87, Exercise generation 0.85, Learning suggestions 0.83, Q&A 0.82, Error analysis 0.80). Latency optimization: cache+incremental encoding reduces total delay from 150ms to 75ms (44% reduction). Learning effectiveness (n=68, 4 weeks, high school functions): experimental post-test mean=82.4 (SD=6.7) vs control 76.1 (SD=7.9); ANCOVA F(1,65)=9.84, p=0.003, partial eta^2=0.13.
limitations: Insufficient handling of complex multimodal information fusion (not addressed); Limited long-term learning trajectory modeling; Scarcity and heterogeneity of domain data increased complexity of model adaptation; Dataset not publicly available (privacy/permission); Data restricted to Chinese secondary school corpus in three subjects, generalizability to other languages/levels/subjects not verified; Short-term intervention (4 weeks); sustained impact on knowledge retention and cognitive load not measured
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Proposes an integrated Transformer optimization pipeline for adaptive learning combining LoRA fine-tuning (rank=8, Q/V injection) on LLaMA-2-13B, instruction fine-tuning with 6 structured task templates (task embedding vectors, [TASK:*] tags), and a dynamic prompt construction mechanism with a behavior encoder, knowledge graph embedding and wrong-question guidance; achieves BLEU/ROUGE-L >0.9 and moderate learning gains (eta^2=0.13) in a controlled classroom experiment, while reducing inference latency via caching and incremental encoding.
notes: Base model LLaMA-2-13B (plus LLaMA-7B compared in ablation). LoRA config: r=8, alpha=32, injected into Q and V matrices of all 24 Transformer layers, 2 LoRA modules per layer. Two fine-tuning stages described with different LRs (LoRA stage: max 3e-4, 30 epochs; instruction FT stage: 1e-4, 3 epochs, batch 64). FP16 precision reported; no explicit quantization (e.g. 4-bit/8-bit) mentioned, so not QLoRA. Training framework: PyTorch 2.6 + Hugging Face Transformers; code framework, data processing scripts and configuration files stated as available in an open-source repository (URL not given in PDF). Dataset is not publicly available (on request only, privacy/no research permission). Output formats include quiz-short-answer style Q&A, exercise generation, concept explanation, feedback, learning suggestions, knowledge point summary, error analysis (mapped to controlled vocab: exercise, explanation, quiz-short-answer, feedback, problem, tutoring-dialogue). Educational level: junior + senior high school (secondary). Evaluation includes classroom controlled experiment with 68 students (4 weeks, high school mathematics) plus longitudinal study with 60 students (1 month) for knowledge state tracking and Likert ratings by 5 senior teachers on 640 samples (user-study / human-expert). RQ coverage interpretation: covers base LLM choice (RQ1), adaptation/FT technique (RQ2), dataset characteristics (RQ3), output formats (RQ4), personalization (RQ5), evaluation methods/metrics (RQ6), educational level and subject (RQ7); RQ8 (reproducibility) partially addressed (code repository mentioned but dataset restricted).

title: Aduvia: A Multi-Purpose AI-Powered Learning Management System (LMS)
venue: Novel Intell. Lead. Emerg. Sci. Conf., NILES - Proc.
dataset_name: User-uploaded educational content (PDFs, images, slide decks) ingested via OCR and semantic chunking
dataset_size: unknown
dataset_domain: educational content / LMS subject-specific tutoring
personalization_mechanism: RAG-based retrieval over subject-specific ingested materials with dynamic prompt engineering, context management across multi-turn conversations, and few-shot examples tailored to the educational domain; adaptive similarity thresholds adjusted by query length and TF-IDF variance
subject_area: general (subject-specific tutoring based on uploaded course materials)
evaluation_metrics: cosine similarity (best/worst match); precision; recall; F1-score; response latency (streaming and non-streaming)
quantitative_results: e5-base-v2 embeddings outperformed MiniLM with tighter cosine similarity distributions (0.50-0.93 vs 0.27-0.83); best match 0.8874 vs 0.6782, worst match 0.8057 vs 0.4258. Relevance classification at similarity threshold 0.65: precision=0.85, recall=0.92, F1=0.88. Llama 3.3 reduced non-streaming response latency by ~0.4s and streaming latency by 30-40% vs Llama 3.0. OCR text extraction accuracy >95% with Tesseract v4.1.1.
limitations: OCR accuracy challenges for complex content; backend scalability issues; external API token constraints; evaluation lacks human-expert or student assessment (only automatic metrics); no fine-tuning reported; relies on RAG + prompting; narrow evaluation scope (retrieval/latency) without learning-outcome validation
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_contribution: Aduvia: a unified AI-powered LMS combining RAG (FAISS + HNSW indexing, e5-base-v2 embeddings) with Llama 3.3 for subject-specific conversational tutoring, automated quiz generation with instant feedback, and secure exam management; deployed as GPU-scaled Kubernetes microservices with streaming responses and iterative feedback loops.
notes: The paper describes 'fine-tuned for educational interactions' for the Quira chatbot but provides no fine-tuning methodology, dataset size, hyperparameters, or training details; the concrete adaptation is RAG + structured prompt engineering + few-shot, so ft_method is reported as 'unknown'. Llama 3.3 parameter size not specified in the paper. Embedding model: intfloat/e5-base-v2 (768-dim). Pipeline: Next.js frontend, Tesseract v4.1.1 OCR (>95% accuracy), spaCy v3.1 preprocessing, 300-token chunks with 30% overlap, Flesch-Kincaid readability filter (<60 excluded), FAISS-HNSW (M=32, efConstruction=200), MongoDB metadata, Handlebars.js prompt assembly, Docker/Kubernetes deployment, Prometheus+Grafana+ELK observability. No information on code/model release; not open-source indicated.

title: Dynamic-rag: a multimodal emotion-aware prompting and retrieval-augmented LLM framework for autism support in adaptive learning systems
venue: Quality and Quantity
dataset_name: Merged learner-state dataset (Engagnition + Autistic Children Emotions Dataset + ABIDE Phenotypic) + Educational Knowledge Base (~1,200 curated math documents) + GSM8K evaluation queries
dataset_size: 1,200 unique learner profiles; ~1,200 knowledge base documents; 40 GSM8K queries (grades 3-5 math) for evaluation; 300 latency measurements
dataset_domain: Elementary mathematics tutoring for autistic learners (grades 3-5)
personalization_mechanism: Pedagogical Readiness Score (PRS) computed from multimodal learner state (accuracy, engagement rate, valence, arousal, clinical severity via fuzzy membership). PRS drives state-conditioned retrieval (depth k in {3,5,7}, re-ranking thresholds), content complexity (Flesch-Kincaid filtering), and generation parameters (temperature 0.0-0.2, syntactic structure, tone). Three tiers: low (<0.45), medium (0.45-0.80), high (>0.80). Policy-based rules adapt syntax (short sentences + numbering if arousal<0.4, concrete examples if valence<0.4, simplified vocab if severe clinical severity).
subject_area: Mathematics (elementary, grades 3-5) for autistic learners (Autism Spectrum Disorder)
evaluation_metrics: Exact Match (EM); Token F1; TF-IDF cosine similarity; Hallucination rate (binary); Retrieval latency; End-to-end response time; Precision@k; Recall@k; F1@k; PRS-complexity correlation (Pearson r); Cohen's d; McNemar's test; Bootstrap 95% CI; Inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa)
quantitative_results: Exact Match: 47.5% to 65.0% (+17.5 pp, 37% relative gain); Token F1: 0.656 to 0.724 (+0.068); TF-IDF: 0.606 to 0.684 (+0.078). Hallucination rate: 45.0% to 14.3% (68.2% relative reduction, p<0.001). Differentiated gains by PRS tier: Low-readiness +28.6 pp EM (28.6% to 57.1%), Medium +16.7 pp (44.4% to 61.1%), High +9.1 pp (60.0% to 69.1%). Latency: median 1.88s (DYNAMIC-RAG) vs 1.93s (Plain LLM), sub-2s. Effect size Cohen's d=0.35; McNemar p=0.065 (exploratory, alpha=0.10); bootstrap 95% CI DeltaEM [+0.025, +0.325]. PRS-complexity correlation Pearson r=0.766 (p<0.001). Retrieval: 87.5% relevance rate, Precision@k=0.91, Recall@k=0.81, F1@k=0.86. Ablation: Static RAG +0.082, PRS-only +0.121, Full DYNAMIC-RAG +0.175 DeltaEM. Inter-rater Cohen's kappa: 0.84 (hallucination), 0.81 (quality), 0.79 (retrieval relevance).
limitations: Small sample size (n=40 queries) yielding exploratory evidence (p=0.065, approaches but does not exceed alpha=0.05); requires n>=120 for 80% power; Single-run evaluation (seed=42) without k-fold cross-validation or multiple random seeds; Evaluation limited to elementary mathematics (grades 3-5), narrow domain; Simulated multimodal inputs from merged learner-state datasets rather than real-time sensor data (EEG, eye-tracking, facial recognition not actually deployed); Between-subjects design without within-session state transitions; PRS coefficients heuristic (hand-tuned) rather than learned via gradient descent or RLHF; Generation drift dominates failures (85.7%), arithmetic errors persist despite correct retrieval; High-readiness learners dominate drift failures (10/12) reflecting productive-struggle trade-off; Knowledge base limitations cause 14.3% retrieval gaps (e.g., compound unit conversion); No full ablation isolating PRS-only vs Policy-only (planned for future work); Confirmatory validation across broader domains and populations pending before large-scale deployment
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: DYNAMIC-RAG: a closed-loop emotion-aware and readiness-aware retrieval-augmented generation framework for inclusive adaptive learning focused on autism support, following the cycle S(t), R(t), G(t), S(t+1). Introduces a multimodal Pedagogical Readiness Score (PRS) integrating behavioral, affective (valence/arousal), and clinical severity signals that jointly modulates retrieval depth (k in {3,5,7}), content complexity filtering (Flesch-Kincaid), and generation parameters (temperature 0.0-0.2, syntactic structure, tone) via rule-based policies. No model fine-tuning is performed; adaptability arises entirely from state-conditioned retrieval + dynamic prompting with GPT-4o-mini. Operationalizes Universal Design for Learning (UDL) and Differentiated Instruction (DI) algorithmically.
notes: Dataset publicness: Engagnition, Autistic Children Emotions Dataset and ABIDE Phenotypic are cited as external sources (likely public research datasets) but the merged learner-state dataset and the curated Educational Knowledge Base (~1,200 math documents) are not explicitly released. Code/model availability not stated in the text read; future confirmatory studies pre-registered via OSF. GPT-4o-mini parameter size not disclosed by provider (marked unknown). rq_coverage maps to: RQ1 factual accuracy (EM/F1/TF-IDF), RQ2 hallucination reduction, RQ3 pedagogical alignment (PRS-complexity correlation + differentiated gains), RQ4 system performance (latency), RQ5 ablation/component contributions, RQ6 retrieval quality by tier, RQ7 failure mode / causal analysis. Evaluation included 2 expert math educators (K-12) as annotators and n=50 pilot tutoring sessions with 2 special education teachers for PRS calibration (Pearson r=0.68 with teacher Likert ratings).

title: Empowering Education with AI: Automating Content Generation through Large Language Models
venue: International Journal of Information and Education Technology
dataset_name: unknown (custom domain-specific corpus: instructional materials and Bloom's Taxonomy-aligned resources)
dataset_size: unknown
dataset_language: English (with mention of potential future multilingual extension)
dataset_domain: education / instructional materials aligned with Bloom's Taxonomy (subjects mentioned: mathematics, history)
personalization_mechanism: Prompt engineering module with structured templates plus user-selectable parameters (subject matter, difficulty level, learning objectives) allowing educators to customize materials for specific learner profiles (e.g., visual learners, students with cognitive challenges). Fine-tuning on Bloom's Taxonomy-aligned resources supports differentiated instruction across cognitive levels.
subject_area: General education (examples reported: mathematics, history); targets primary, secondary and higher education learners.
evaluation_metrics: accuracy rate of generated content (factual correctness / freedom from errors as judged by educators); educator adoption likelihood (binary yes/no survey item); performance per Bloom's Taxonomy category (Remembering, Understanding, Applying, Analysing, Evaluating, Creating) comparing GPT-3.5 vs GPT-4; qualitative feedback from educators and subject-matter experts
quantitative_results: Overall generated-content accuracy: 96%. Educator adoption likelihood: ~85%. Per-Bloom's Taxonomy category, GPT-4 outperformed GPT-3.5 across all levels (GPT-4 reaches ~95-100% in most categories vs GPT-3.5 ~65-95%); GPT-3.5 showed a notable drop in Apply (~65%) and Creating (~72%), while GPT-4 remained near ~90-95% in those categories. Survey conducted among Maltese and international educators (~68% Maltese; ~30% male / 70% female in the Maltese subset).
limitations: Reduced accuracy and reliability of outputs in niche or highly specialised topics; Hallucinations (e.g., historical content inconsistent with facts) requiring manual review and correction; Potential bias in generated content stemming from biases in training data; Learning curve for educators in structuring effective prompts; Accessibility/equity gap: underprivileged schools may lack infrastructure to use AI-driven systems (digital divide); Ethical considerations around inclusivity, stereotypes and academic integrity; No formal reporting of fine-tuning hyperparameters, dataset size, or reproducibility artifacts
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_contribution: Design and evaluation of an LLM-driven automated content generation system for Intelligent Tutoring Systems, combining prompt engineering with fine-tuning on Bloom's Taxonomy-aligned instructional materials (using GPT-4, compared against GPT-3.5). The system generates diverse educational artifacts (lesson plans, quizzes, study guides, explanations, feedback) achieving 96% accuracy and ~85% educator adoption interest, and is designed to integrate with LMS/ITS via APIs.
notes: Published in International Journal of Information and Education Technology, Vol. 15, No. 9, 2025 (doi: 10.18178/ijiet.2025.15.9.2401). Authors: Andrew Emanuel Attard and Alexiei Dingli (University of Malta). Fine-tuning is described conceptually (domain-specific datasets + Bloom's Taxonomy alignment) but without disclosing any PEFT technique, hyperparameters, dataset size, or base-model version details beyond 'GPT-4' and 'GPT-3.5'; therefore ft_method is coded as SFT (closest match to 'fine-tuning the LLM with domain-specific instructional materials') with a note that no technical details are reported. Evaluation is survey-based with educators as human experts; 'accuracy' is reported as a single aggregate figure (96%) plus per-Bloom's-category bar charts comparing GPT-3.5 vs GPT-4. Future work mentions RAG and RLHF as planned extensions (not implemented in this study).
